tEn: Tony Fadell: The Magic of Early Programming and Creating Tools
tZh: 托尼·法德尔：早期编程与创造工具的神奇之处
sEn: Tony Fadell shares his first love for computers through punch cards and BASIC, and how Electronic Arts' construction sets inspired him to create.
sZh: 托尼·法德尔分享了他通过打孔卡和 BASIC 编程对计算机的初次热爱，以及电子艺界的构建工具如何激发他的创造力。

consensus.en: Great ideas start with a painkiller, not a vitamin, and deliver an emotional superpower.
consensus.zh: 伟大的创意始于止痛药而非维生素，并带来情感上的超能力。

consensus.en: For v1 products, decisions are opinion-based, not data-driven; leaders must own them.
consensus.zh: 对于初代产品，决策基于观点而非数据；领导者必须为此负责。

consensus.en: Start with a press release as a treatment to align the team on the why and story.
consensus.zh: 从一份新闻稿开始，作为剧本让团队对齐目标和故事。

consensus.en: Product management is the voice of the customer, hardest to hire for, and keeps the team grounded.
consensus.zh: 产品管理是客户的声音，最难招聘，并让团队保持脚踏实地。

consensus.en: Authenticity and risk-taking in communication are essential; leaders set the tone.
consensus.zh: 沟通中的真实性和冒险精神至关重要；领导者定下基调。

contrarian.en: Experts can be detrimental to innovation; beginners' mindset is better for novel problems.
contrarian.zh: 专家可能对创新有害；初学者心态更适合解决新问题。

contrarian.en: Steve Jobs was challengeable; he critiqued work, not people, and could be swayed by data.
contrarian.zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯是可以被挑战的；他批评工作而非人，并可能被数据说服。

contrarian.en: Plastic on iPhone was initially chosen; Jobs flipped the frame to glass by blaming customer for scratches.
contrarian.zh: iPhone最初选择塑料；乔布斯通过将划痕归咎于客户而转向玻璃。

contrarian.en: Marketing should be involved from the start, not just at the end to tell a story.
contrarian.zh: 营销应从开始就参与，而不仅仅是在最后讲故事。

contrarian.en: The iPod succeeded because Apple committed all marketing dollars to it, despite being a struggling company.
contrarian.zh: iPod成功是因为苹果将所有营销资金投入其中，尽管当时公司处境艰难。

transcript.sec: Introduction and early love for computers

turns.en: When did you first fall in love with computers or let's say computer engineering and design?
turns.zh: 你第一次爱上计算机，或者说计算机工程与设计，是什么时候？

turns.en: I first fell in love with computers and programming in a summer school class in fifth grade in Grosse Pointe Farms, Michigan. It was a simple BASIC programming class, but not like you might think—it was with punch cards. Literally, you had a stack of cards, and you'd use a number two pencil to write your program line by line. You had to make sure it was perfectly stacked with no errors. Then you'd take that set of cards, put it on a reader, and it would go off to an IBM microcomputer somewhere in the back—the cloud of that time. Then you'd sit at a Texas Instruments paper terminal, and it would print out the results. I could write things and program this machine to do stuff. It was nowhere near sexy—no graphics, right? Oregon Trail was all text. The cards were so cumbersome that if you got one thing wrong, out of order, or dropped a card, it would all fall apart. So just doing that—print statements, I can't even remember the exact BASIC commands—but it was programming. You're writing BASIC on paper and calling it programming. You're programming this computer in a remote location, and it came back. It was truly cloud computing in a way—terminal-based computing. The input and the program were separate; you'd put in the stack of cards to load the program into memory, then type RUN. The magic was that you could create—you had a language and you could create what you wanted. You could create a world, have interaction, compute things, do numbers. I was playing Oregon Trail, so you could play text games and imagine them in your brain. There's a meme I saw recently: if you want to feel bad about yourself as a programmer, realize that one person wrote Railroad Tycoon—a cool little builder game—in assembly, from scratch. For people who don't know, it looks like a Sim City type game, centered on railroads, with nice 3D graphics and all that rich, colorful stuff. All written in assembly, the lowest level code next to binary. You had to notice the magic at that low level. No graphics, no APIs, no sample code, no Stack Overflow, no internet. You had to know registers, op codes, and imagine the world of memory structures in your brain. There was no visualization—you visualized it yourself. That was magic. But the next part that hooked me even further was when Electronic Arts came out for the Apple II. I got an Apple II, and I was programming in BASIC, making my own games. Then two games blew my mind: Pinball Construction Set and Music Construction Set. They let me create pinball games and musical scores—I loved music—and then play them. So I could create myself, but others created tools so you could create at a visual level. I'd read the backstories because Electronic Arts back then had one programmer per game, and you could read their stories—like a musician. One guy wrote Music Construction Set all in assembly at 16 years old. I was probably 12 or 13, and I thought, if he could do this and publish it, what could I do? So it kept building from there. Those seminal things—first the introduction, then the power through programming to turn things into what you wanted—and you didn't have to be 40 or 50 with a PhD. I thought, this is really cool. I wish we treated programmers like artists, knowing their backstories. Today, not just programmers but engineers and designers—all the little details in a product we love probably have a human being behind them with their own inkling of genius. I wish we knew those stories. It always makes me sad when I don't.
turns.zh: 我第一次爱上计算机和编程是在密歇根州格罗斯波因特农场五年级的一个暑期学校课程。那是一堂简单的 BASIC 编程课，但不像你想的那样——是用穿孔卡片。literally，你有一叠卡片，用 2 号铅笔一行一行地写程序。你必须确保卡片叠放完美，没有错误。然后你把那叠卡片放在读卡器上，它会传到后面某处的 IBM 微型计算机——当时的“云”。然后你坐在德州仪器的纸终端前，它会打印出结果。我可以写东西，编程让这台机器做事。它一点也不性感——没有图形，对吧？《俄勒冈之旅》全是文字。卡片非常笨重，如果你弄错一个地方、顺序不对，或者掉了一张卡片，一切就都完了。所以光是做那个——打印语句，我甚至记不清具体的 BASIC 命令了——但那是在编程。你在纸上写 BASIC，并称之为编程。你在编程一台远程的计算机，然后结果回来。从某种意义上说，这真的是云计算——基于终端的计算。输入和程序是分开的；你放入卡片叠来把程序加载到内存，然后输入 RUN。神奇之处在于你可以创造——你有一种语言，可以创造你想要的东西。你可以创造一个世界，进行交互，计算东西，处理数字。我在玩《俄勒冈之旅》，所以你可以玩文字游戏，然后在脑海中想象。我最近看到一个 meme：如果你想对自己作为程序员感到沮丧，想想有一个人用汇编语言从零写出了《铁路大亨》——一个很酷的建造类游戏。对于不知道的人来说，它看起来像《模拟城市》类型的游戏，以铁路为中心，有漂亮的 3D 图形和所有丰富多彩的东西。全部用汇编语言编写，这是最接近二进制的低级代码。你必须注意到那种低层次的神奇。没有图形，没有 API，没有示例代码，没有 Stack Overflow，没有互联网。你必须知道寄存器、操作码，并在脑海中想象内存结构的世界。没有可视化——你自己可视化一切。那就是神奇之处。但让我更上瘾的下一步是当艺电（Electronic Arts）为 Apple II 推出游戏时。我买了一台 Apple II，用 BASIC 编程，自己做游戏。然后有两个游戏让我大开眼界：《弹球建造套件》和《音乐建造套件》。它们让我可以创建弹球游戏和音乐乐谱——我热爱音乐——然后演奏它们。所以我可以自己创造，但别人创造了工具，让你可以在视觉层面创造。我会阅读背后的故事，因为当年的艺电每个游戏只有一个程序员，你可以读到他们的故事——就像音乐家一样。有一个人 16 岁时用汇编语言写了《音乐建造套件》。我当时大概 12 或 13 岁，我想，如果他能做到并出版，我能做什么？所以从那以后不断积累。那些开创性的事情——先是入门，然后是通过编程将东西变成你想要的力量——而且你不需要 40 或 50 岁，不需要博士学位。我觉得这真的很酷。我希望我们像对待艺术家一样对待程序员，了解他们的背景故事。今天，不仅仅是程序员，还有工程师和设计师——我们喜欢的产品中的所有小细节，背后可能都有一个带着自己天才灵感的人。我希望我们知道那些故事。当我不知道的时候，我总是感到难过。

transcript.secZh: 引言与早期计算机热爱

transcript.sec: Hiding Engineers and Their Stories

turns.en: Obviously I love engineering and I interact with companies and they, you know, autonomous vehicles something I'm really interested about, and I see that companies generally—and will probably talk about this—but they seem to want to hide their engineers, like engineers hold the secrets, like the great secret we did not speak of. But then the result of that is you don't get to hear their stories, the passion that is there behind the engineers, and also the genius. There's a difference between the stuff that's patented, like the kernel of the idea, and the beautiful sort of side effects of the idea. And I wish companies revealed the beautiful side effects a little bit more. But yeah, so sorry for the distraction.
turns.zh: 显然，我热爱工程，也跟很多公司打交道。自动驾驶是我非常感兴趣的东西，我发现公司们通常——我们可能也会聊到——它们似乎想把工程师藏起来，好像工程师掌握着秘密，像那种不能说的天大秘密。但结果就是，你听不到他们的故事，听不到工程师背后的激情，也看不到他们的天才。专利保护的东西，比如想法的核心，和想法那些美妙的附带效应，是有区别的。我希望公司能多展示那些美妙的附带效应。好了，抱歉跑题了。

transcript.secZh: 隐藏的工程师及其故事

transcript.sec: First Computer Love: Apple II

turns.en: So what, you mentioned Apple II. What was the first computer you fell in love with? Like the product, the thing before you that was a personal computer.
turns.zh: 那么，你提到了 Apple II。你爱上的第一台电脑是什么？就是那个产品，你面前的那台个人电脑。

turns.en: It was the Apple II. So the Apple II was something I was just lusting over. You know, it was—I think it was at the time, it was the Person of the Year maybe, I don't remember what, but well, Apple II was the Person of the Year for Time magazine back then. I don't remember when, but it was around that same time. I was so young, but I had—there was the Apple II and I didn't know what it was, but I knew about tools because my grandfather taught me all about tools and creating things. And I saw this thing, and I had that IBM experience, that terminal experience, and I'm like, "Oh, I could have that at home!" And so I need to have that at home. And the only thing that was really talked about in our circles was the Apple II, and I was just like, "That's it." So I went, jumped up and down. It was very expensive. I have to have this. My parents were like, "What?" It was $2,500 back then, yeah, in 1981, which was crazy. So I was like, "I'm gonna make as much money as I can this summer." And my grandfather said—because he helped me learn all about tools and build things together—"I will match whatever you make, so you can get this computer." So I worked very, very hard as a caddy, golf caddy, caddying actually for the families at the country clubs in the town where we lived, and did whatever I could. And at the end of that summer, we got my Apple II. And now you couldn't tear it away from me. It was my friend. It was everything.
turns.zh: 就是 Apple II。Apple II 是我朝思暮想的东西。你知道，当时——我记得它好像被《时代》杂志评为年度人物，具体年份我记不清了，但大概是那个时候。我当时很小，但我知道工具，因为祖父教了我所有关于工具和创造的知识。我看到这个东西，又有了之前用 IBM 终端机的体验，我就想：“哦，我可以在家里拥有这个！”所以我必须在家拥有一台。我们圈子里唯一被热议的就是 Apple II，我就觉得：“就是它了。”于是我上蹿下跳地要买。它非常贵。我必须拥有它。我父母说：“什么？”当时是 1981 年，要 2500 美元，简直疯了。所以我说：“我今年夏天要拼命赚钱。”祖父说——因为他帮我学了所有工具知识，我们一起造东西——“你赚多少，我就补多少，这样你就能买这台电脑了。”于是那个夏天我非常努力地工作，在我们镇上的乡村俱乐部给那些家庭当高尔夫球童，做了一切我能做的事。夏天结束时，我们买到了我的 Apple II。从此它就离不开我了。它是我的朋友，它就是一切。

transcript.sec: Brilliant Design Choices of Apple II

turns.en: From a product perspective, what do you remember that was brilliant? The design choices, the ideas behind it? Or is it just that it exists, or the very idea of a personal computer is the brilliant design choice?
turns.zh: 从产品角度看，你觉得哪些地方很出色？是设计选择、背后的理念？还是仅仅因为它存在，或者个人电脑这个概念本身就是出色的设计选择？

turns.en: Yeah, it was that I could actually have this kind of tool in my house and I could use it anytime I wanted. I could program it any way. There was no internet connection, there was no—it was all just you. You either loaded software that you got from someone, or you created yourself. And then there was a whole other thing which started happening, which we were doing, and this was kind of like MP3s and stuff—we were sharing software. So you built this community of sharing stuff. You would go in, pirate—that was what it was called—pirate all this software. You'd never use it all, but it was just that fun thing of like, "I'm gonna get all this other stuff and then tear it apart and do disassembly on it and see behind the scenes." So you really had a sense that this was your world and you owned it. And you could like literally go into every register. We didn't have all those security layers like we do now. You could really touch bits and you could poke bits and you can make this light turn on. And the geek assignment just lit up. Now it's so abstract, people don't even understand—usually some programs don't even understand memory, they just think it's unlimited. And security, it's like now there's all this security you should have, but it's like the adults all showed up to the party and now you can't have all the fun. It's like, "No, no." This was the thing where if the power went out, you lost your whole program. You might have worked a whole day on it, and if you didn't press save at every other line and you were save, save, save, and it would like growl the word—the disk drive or the tape drive—every single step was contemplated because if you didn't, you lost maybe a ton of work. So a lot of the magic was in the software: the fact that you could have software, the fact that you could share software, the community around the software. It wasn't necessarily the hardware.
turns.zh: 是的，关键在于我能在家里拥有这种工具，随时使用，随意编程。没有互联网连接，什么都没有——全靠你自己。你要么加载别人给的软件，要么自己写。然后还发生了另一件事，我们也在做，有点像 MP3 那种——我们分享软件。于是你建立了一个分享社区。你会去“盗版”——当时就这么叫——盗版所有这些软件。你根本用不完，但乐趣就在于：“我要搞到所有这些，然后拆开它，反汇编，看看背后是什么。”所以你真正感觉到这是你的世界，你拥有它。你可以直接操作每一个寄存器。我们没有现在这么多安全层。你真的能触碰比特，拨弄比特，让灯亮起来。那种极客的满足感瞬间被点燃。现在太抽象了，人们甚至不理解——有些程序甚至不理解内存，以为它是无限的。至于安全，现在有各种安全措施，但就像大人们都来参加派对了，你就不能尽情玩了。不，不。以前是这样的：如果停电，你整个程序就丢了。你可能写了一整天，如果没隔几行就按保存，而且一直保存、保存、保存，磁盘驱动器或磁带驱动器会发出声音——每一步都得考虑，因为如果不保存，你可能丢掉大量工作。所以很多魔力在于软件：你能拥有软件，能分享软件，以及围绕软件的社区。并不一定是硬件。

turns.en: Well, that was the first step. The second step around the hardware was I got things like the Mockingboard, which paired with the music instruction set. You could now generate all kinds of tones and notes, and it was a synthesizer in the Apple II. So you would plug in this card and go, "Oh my god, look at this!" And it would—you know, you could start generating cool sounds. You're like—it was like a Moog, in a way, early Moog. What year are we talking about? This is '82, I think, '81, '82. And you could make all the kind of synthetic sounds that are very cool in the '80s. Yeah, 8-bit chip tunes. And then, when you wanted to add a joystick, you had to pull a chip out and you had to plug in a DIP socket to put in a joystick. And then I was like, "Oh, and then I had to get more memory. How do we do that?" And I wanted to speed up products. So that turned into a company actually from that, but it was in hardware and software at that. But it was all about modifying this thing in every way. First was software, and then you started gaining confidence, and then I got a little bit more money and stuff, and then you could get into the hardware and wire things. And the Apple II came with all the schematics. So in the back of the early Apple IIs, you could open up and all the schematics were there. You purchased the Apple II and the schematics come with it.
turns.zh: 嗯，那是第一步。第二步关于硬件，我买了像 Mockingboard 这样的东西，它配合音乐指令集，可以生成各种音调和音符，相当于 Apple II 里的合成器。你插上这块卡，就会惊叹：“天哪，看看这个！”然后就能开始制造酷炫的声音。有点像 Moog，早期的 Moog。那是哪一年？大概是 82 年，81、82 年。你能做出 80 年代所有那种很酷的合成音效，8 位芯片音乐。然后，当你想加一个摇杆时，你得拔掉一个芯片，插上一个 DIP 插座来连接摇杆。接着我又想：“哦，我还需要更多内存。怎么弄？”我还想加速产品。后来这甚至催生了一家公司，不过那是软硬件都涉及。但核心就是，用各种方式改造这个东西。先是软件，然后你有了信心，再后来我有了点钱，就能涉足硬件，自己接线。而且 Apple II 附带了所有电路图。早期 Apple II 的背面，你打开就能看到所有电路图。你买 Apple II，电路图就随附。

turns.en: That's an interesting choice from a company perspective. So it's like a real maker kind of thing. I wonder what they—so that was intentional?
turns.zh: 从公司角度看，这是个有趣的选择。这就像真正的创客风格。我想知道他们——这是有意为之的吗？

turns.en: This is absolutely intentional. This is the work for the cutting-edge folks too, or especially if it was only the cutting edge. It was geeks for geeks. So we were like, "Oh, how did they make it?" And then we got to learn through that. Apple I did the same thing. It just Apple II became more packaged up and had a little bit better software, came with BASIC. So it was really what we might think of as a Raspberry Pi today or something like that, but not with so much software. It was literally—and all the chips were out there, so you could inspect the—
turns.zh: 这绝对是故意的。这也是为前沿用户准备的，或者说，它本来就是给前沿用户的。这是极客为极客打造的。所以我们就会想：“哦，他们是怎么做的？”然后通过这个来学习。Apple I 也是同样的做法。只是 Apple II 包装得更完整，软件也好一点，附带了 BASIC。所以它真的有点像我们今天说的树莓派之类的，但没那么多软件。实际上——所有芯片都裸露在外，你可以检查——

transcript.sec: Open source hardware and maker communities

turns.en: Buses and the right because everything was just broken out. So I guess that's the idea behind stable big projects in open source like on GitHub that you have the schematics there and it's kind of a product. But I wonder why more companies don't do that kind of thing like we're going to release this to a small set of people self-selected perhaps that are kind of the makers, the cutting edge folks, the builders, the at-home engineers. In some way what Tesla is doing with the beta for the full self driving it's kind of like that, it's like selecting a group of people but that has to do more with how safe of a driver you are versus how much of a tinkerer you are because you don't get to tinker. I wonder is that a crazy idea to do for really cutting-edge technologies especially you're interested in like hardware stuff? Is that crazy? Why don't more companies do that kind of thing?
turns.zh: 总线什么的，因为所有东西都是开放的。所以我觉得这就是开源大项目背后的理念，比如 GitHub 上那些，你把原理图放在那里，它也算是一种产品。但我想知道为什么没有更多公司这么做——比如我们把产品发布给一小部分自选的人，可能是创客、前沿用户、建造者、家庭工程师。某种程度上，特斯拉在完全自动驾驶测试版上就是这么做的，他们选择了一群人，但这更多取决于你的驾驶安全性，而不是你有多爱捣鼓，因为你没法自己改装。我想知道，对于真正前沿的技术，尤其是你感兴趣的硬件领域，这么做是不是很疯狂？为什么没有更多公司这么做？

turns.en: I think back then it was about a community and serving that community of builders. And now this is about people who want to get the experience and want it really simple and easy. And so there is the audience or they believe the audience is small who would value those other things that we're just talking about. But if we look at things like Raspberry Pi and all of these other little boards, right, there's a whole world more than any that I've seen. It's amazing what you can do now with these little kits and the software that's created. And so there's a whole other, I think, another batch of makers and builders that are coming up through the ranks. And if we look at YouTube channels and stuff, right, they take these little boards, they hack them, then they print out parts on their 3D printer, assemble them, and they create robots and what have you. So I think it's happening. It's just not as raw as it used to be, but it's there and it's really expanding around the world. And that's really nice to see because it's a whole new generation who are empowered. I think there's a semi-dormant genius amongst millions. So like Raspberry Pi is revealing that a little bit. It's probably, I wouldn't be surprised if it's several million Raspberry Pis that have been sold. I think it should, and it's kind of this quiet storm of genius brewing of engineers. We don't get to hear it because they're not organized. I mean, we get to hear their inklings here and there like I said, YouTube, there's little communities that are local and so on. But if they were organized, if a leader would emerge.
turns.zh: 我认为过去这关乎一个社区，服务于那个建造者社区。而现在，这关乎那些想要体验、想要简单易用的人。所以，受众是存在的，或者说他们认为看重我们刚才讨论的那些东西的受众很小。但如果我们看看树莓派和其他那些小板子，你会发现一个比我见过的任何东西都更广阔的世界。现在用这些小套件和创建的软件能做的事情太惊人了。所以我认为，还有另一批创客和建造者正在崛起。如果我们看看 YouTube 频道之类的，他们拿这些小板子，破解它们，然后用 3D 打印机打印零件，组装起来，创造出机器人等等。所以我认为这正在发生。只是不像以前那么原始了，但它确实存在，并且在全世界范围内扩展。这真的很令人欣慰，因为这是一代被赋能的年轻人。我认为在数百万人中存在着半休眠的天才。树莓派就揭示了一点。我猜树莓派已经卖出了几百万台，我不会感到惊讶。我觉得它应该如此，而且这是一场酝酿中的天才工程师的静默风暴。我们听不到他们的声音，因为他们没有组织起来。我的意思是，我们偶尔能听到他们的只言片语，比如 YouTube，还有一些本地的小社区等等。但如果他们组织起来，如果有一个领导者出现。

transcript.sec: Early entrepreneurial experience with Quality Computers

turns.en: So when did you first start to dream about building your own things, designing your own products, designing your own systems and software and hardware?
turns.zh: 那么你是什么时候开始梦想建造自己的东西、设计自己的产品、设计自己的系统和软硬件的？

turns.en: Well, in high school there was a company that a friend of mine founded and I was the second employee. It was called Quality Computers and it was a mail order — mail order because there's no e-commerce then, there was no internet again. Yeah, you either mailed in your little coupon and said this is what I wanted to order, or you wrote in to get a catalog and deliver to you. Turnaround time and this stuff was like from the time you wanted the time you bought it was maybe eight to twelve weeks. That was just the normal way of getting things. So Quality Computers was a mail order for Apple II and it was software and all kinds of accessories, so hardware accessories, hardware plug-in cards, joysticks, all this stuff. And what we noticed was there were accelerator cards or memory cards, and to be able to use those cards you had to actually go and change the software you used to access this new memory. So you literally have to go and you took the program that you had, let's say was AppleWorks which was like an early Microsoft Office or something like that, and you had to literally change the code and you would install all these patches to then take advantage of the hardware. So what we started creating was software on top of it to do the automatic installation of all of these patches. So we made it much easier to take new hardware and the existing software you have and expand it into this new world. So it was creating tools and that really great customer support. And we started getting a lot of orders because we had the software make it easier to install, to give them the superpower. And at the same time, they would be able to change their software and have a new world that wasn't existing from the companies that were creating the initial products. And so it was more of that. And then that happened with hard drives. So I wrote a hard drive optimizer for the Apple II to read because you could get really fragmented, so I wrote that piece of software and we sold that through the company along with the hard drives that we sold from third parties. So that all happened in 12th grade, freshman year of college.
turns.zh: 嗯，高中时有一家公司，是我一个朋友创立的，我是第二个员工。公司叫 Quality Computers，是做邮购的——邮购，因为当时没有电子商务，也没有互联网。是的，你要么邮寄一张小优惠券，写上你想订什么，要么写信索取目录，然后等着送货上门。周转时间之类的，从你想要到买到手大概要八到十二周。这就是当时买东西的正常方式。所以 Quality Computers 是做 Apple II 邮购的，卖软件和各种配件，比如硬件配件、硬件插卡、操纵杆之类的。我们注意到有加速卡或内存卡，但要用这些卡，你必须去修改访问新内存的软件。所以你实际上得拿着你的程序，比如 AppleWorks，那就像早期的 Microsoft Office 之类的，然后你必须修改代码，安装所有这些补丁才能利用新硬件。所以我们开始创建上层软件来自动安装所有这些补丁。这样，使用新硬件和现有软件并扩展到新世界就变得容易多了。所以我们在创建工具并提供非常好的客户支持。我们开始接到很多订单，因为我们的软件让安装更容易，给了他们超能力。同时，他们能够修改软件，进入一个最初产品公司不提供的新世界。后来硬盘也出现了类似情况。我为 Apple II 写了一个硬盘优化器，因为硬盘会变得非常碎片化，所以我写了那个软件，通过公司连同我们从第三方购买的硬盘一起销售。这一切都发生在十二年级和大学一年级期间。

turns.en: You're a hard drive optimizer in 12th grade?
turns.zh: 你十二年级就写硬盘优化器了？

turns.en: Yeah, between 12th and freshman year. What programming language do you remember? Is it assembly? It was, there were certain inner loops were assembly and other loops actually there were really early Pascal, no C. C compilers?
turns.zh: 是的，在十二年级和大学一年级之间。你记得用什么编程语言吗？是汇编吗？有些内循环是汇编，其他循环实际上是早期的 Pascal，没有 C。C 编译器？

turns.en: What was the motivation behind these? Is it to make people's lives easier? Is it to create a thing experience that is simpler and simpler and simpler thereby more accessible to a larger number of people? Or did you just like to tinker?
turns.zh: 这些背后的动机是什么？是为了让人们的生活更轻松吗？是为了创造一种越来越简单的体验，从而让更多人能够使用？还是你只是喜欢捣鼓？

turns.en: No, no, it was two things really. Because one, we wanted to sell more hardware and software, right? So it was like, oh, make it easier for the user. And then the other thing was because I was also manning the customer support line. People would call and go, this doesn't work, and I'm like, oh, I gotta go fix the hardware and software, right? Or I gotta fix the software to make the hardware and the installation process better. So my whole world was out of box experience from when I was in high school because I had to man the customer support line, pack the boxes, and write some of the code. While we were doing well, Joe Gleason who was the founder of Quality Computers, he was off doing the marketing, placing the ads for the mail order, making sure we were running the credit cards right. Yeah, it was two of us, and then it turned into a third, and then we hired another person from high school to pack boxes so I could stay on the customer support line or doing the software. And it was all in his parents' basement. Yeah, right as you were scaling exponentially. Scaling right, yeah exactly. Bootstrapping.
turns.zh: 不，不，其实是两件事。第一，我们想卖更多硬件和软件，对吧？所以就是让用户更容易使用。第二，因为我还要接客服电话。人们会打电话说，这个不行，我就得去修硬件和软件，或者修改软件让硬件和安装过程更好。所以从高中开始，我的整个世界就是开箱体验，因为我得接客服电话、打包盒子、写一些代码。我们做得不错的时候，Quality Computers 的创始人 Joe Gleason 负责营销，为邮购业务投放广告，确保信用卡处理正确。是的，就我们两个人，后来变成三个人，然后我们从高中又雇了一个人来打包，这样我就能继续接客服电话或做软件。所有工作都在他父母的地下室里。是的，就在你指数级扩张的时候。扩张，没错。白手起家。

transcript.sec: The birth of iPod ideas

turns.en: So we'll jump around a little bit. But what were the — you said you loved music — what were the ideas that gave birth to the iPod if we jump forward? transcript.sec: Love for music and early hacking

turns.en: Music is so fundamental to who we are as a humanity, right? And to be able to put that in your pocket, make it truly portable, is fascinating. In a way that's truly portable, so it's digital as opposed to sort of like a Walkman or something like that. So what were the ideas that gave birth to the iPod?
turns.zh: 音乐对我们人类来说如此根本，对吧？而能够把它放进口袋，让它真正便携，这很迷人。以真正便携的方式，所以它是数字化的，而不是像随身听那样的东西。那么，是什么想法催生了 iPod？

turns.en: You know, I was in love with music since I was a kid. Just loved music from, I think, second grade when I got my first albums and stuff like that.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，我从小就热爱音乐。从二年级我拿到第一张专辑开始就爱上了音乐。

turns.en: What kind of music are we talking about?
turns.zh: 我们说的是哪种音乐？

turns.en: So this was Led Zeppelin, this was the Stones, Hendrix, Aerosmith, Cheap Trick, Ted Nugent. You know, just the real American and British rock and roll. There's a bunch of people listening right now who are like, "Who's that? Zeppelin? What is that?" It drove my parents crazy. Yeah, just blasted loud, loud, just right. And this was second, third, fourth grade. I just fell in love. And then we moved back to Detroit, and I loved listening to the radio station because there was all kinds of crazy music. You'd have an amalgam of rock, funk, and R&B. I loved to listen at night, so I had a clock radio. But if I had the clock radio on, everyone, like the parents, would say, "Go to sleep, stop that, turn that stuff off." So I hacked the clock radio and put a headphone jack in it. Nice, right? So I said, "Oh, they're like, wow, okay." And then I could listen to it all night and no one could hear me. I could just sit there, huddling around the radio, grooving out, just listening to Zeppelin, "Stairway to Heaven."
turns.zh: 有齐柏林飞艇、滚石、亨德里克斯、史密斯飞船、廉价把戏、泰德·纽金特。就是那种真正的美国和英国摇滚乐。现在有很多人在听，他们会问：“那是谁？齐柏林飞艇？那是什么？”这把我父母逼疯了。没错，就是放得超大声，刚刚好。那是二、三、四年级的时候。我彻底爱上了。后来我们搬回底特律，我喜欢听电台，因为那里有各种疯狂的音乐。你会听到摇滚、放克和 R&B 的混合。我喜欢晚上听，所以我有一个钟控收音机。但如果我开着收音机，父母就会说：“去睡觉，别听了，关掉。”所以我改装了收音机，加了一个耳机插孔。不错吧？然后我就可以整晚听，没人能听到。我就坐在那里，蜷在收音机旁，跟着齐柏林飞艇的《天堂阶梯》摇摆。

turns.en: What would you say is the greatest classic rock song of all time?
turns.zh: 你觉得有史以来最伟大的经典摇滚歌曲是什么？

turns.en: Well, it pops into my mind... Well, no, you know what I mean? This has to be... That's just really hard, dude. This is a serious journalistic interview. You're not going to back down from these kinds of questions? Oh my god, no. I don't know. Challenge. Yeah, it's hard to pick. But to me, "Stairway to Heaven" is a safe bet. It's so often considered one of the greatest songs of all time that you almost don't want to pick it, right? Exactly. But you've returned to it time and time again, and it's like, yeah, this is something pretty special. This is a rock opera of sorts. Well, the rock opera that really blew me away and still continues to blow me away is all of "Dark Side of the Moon." Like, I love that. Yeah, I love Zeppelin. I can't say which one's better, but "Dark Side of the Moon" for me was an audio experience, the whole thing from soup to nuts, plus all the synthesizers and all of those things.
turns.zh: 嗯，它突然出现在我脑海里……不，你懂我的意思吗？这必须是……这真的很难选，老兄。这是一个严肃的新闻采访。你不会回避这类问题吧？哦天哪，不。我不知道。挑战。是的，很难选。但对我来说，《天堂阶梯》是一个安全的选择。它经常被认为是有史以来最伟大的歌曲之一，以至于你几乎不想选它，对吧？没错。但你一次又一次地回到它，然后觉得，是的，这真的很特别。这有点像摇滚歌剧。嗯，真正让我震撼并且至今仍然震撼的摇滚歌剧是整张《月之暗面》。我喜欢那张。是的，我喜欢齐柏林飞艇。我不能说哪个更好，但对我来说，《月之暗面》是一种音频体验，从头到尾，加上所有合成器之类的东西。

turns.en: Okay, so back to the iPod. So that's you from an early age. You loved music, absolutely loved it. And you know, always was just around it and always playing. You know, I played it so loud that I actually hurt the hearing in my right ear, and I still suffer from that today. No regrets, no regrets whatsoever. Going to concerts in downtown Detroit and all that crazy stuff. So moving forward, in college I was a DJ, so I would DJ and hang out and play all the tunes I love for the crowd. And then I continued to do that in Silicon Valley when I moved right after school. And so I was lugging all of these CDs around with me, a thousand CDs, right? And at the same time, I was doing the Philips Nino and Velo, those are Windows CE based mobile computing products. The Nino was the first device to actually put Audible books on tape. So I worked with Audible. We met in a conference and they were like, "We don't want to do hardware, we just want to do content." I was like, "Well, we have this device, let's get it together." And we got Audible on that. And this was in '96 or '97, first Audible books. And you know, I was like, "Oh my god, that's audio. Well, what if we put music on it?" Right? And so I go, and the memory was very small at the time. There was almost no flash, it was all DRAM. When you did Audible, you started in DRAM, right? Which was okay probably because, how much books do you need? Is the idea. By the way, brilliant. I mean, just putting books on a mobile device is a brilliant step. I don't know, sometimes can't measure how much human progress occurred because of an invention. There's the sexy big products, but you never know. Maybe like Wikipedia is one of those things that doesn't get enough credit for the transformational effects it has. It's not seen as the sexiest of products, but maybe it is when you look at human history. Wikipedia arguably is one of the big things that basically unlocked human knowledge. Human knowledge, okay, and human editing, and just the human nature of building something together. Yeah, so it's fascinating. Sometimes you can't measure those things until many, many decades later. Anyway, sorry. So that was the Nino. That was there. And then there was Audible. You put books, why not put music? And I'm carrying around the music for the DJ gigs, and you're like, "Wait a second, two and two together." Right? Like, let's get rid of this. And then MP3 showed up. The actual encoder performance, the format MP3, showed up around '97, '98. So MP3 is compressed, so you can have the storage reduced significantly. Right? So you could go from a large, full, lossless digital track into something that can be stored in four to eight megabytes, something like that, for the audio. Now, that's reduced quality, but you could get it down there. And you're like, "Oh, okay." And now if we have enough flash or DRAM, we can put 10, 15, whatever, all in that same memory. And it starts to replicate a CD. And then ultimately, if you put it on a hard drive, you could start to put thousands of songs.
turns.zh: 好的，回到 iPod。所以那是你从小时候开始。你热爱音乐，绝对热爱。而且你总是围绕着它，一直播放。你知道吗，我放得那么大声，以至于我的右耳听力受损，至今仍受影响。但我毫不后悔，绝不后悔。去底特律市中心听演唱会，做那些疯狂的事情。所以后来，在大学里我是一名 DJ，我会做 DJ，闲逛，为人群播放我喜欢的音乐。毕业后我搬到硅谷，继续做这件事。所以我随身带着所有这些 CD，一千张 CD，对吧？同时，我在做飞利浦 Nino 和 Velo，那些是基于 Windows CE 的移动计算产品。Nino 是第一个真正把 Audible 有声书放上去的设备。所以我和 Audible 合作。我们在一次会议上见面，他们说：“我们不想做硬件，我们只想做内容。”我说：“嗯，我们有这个设备，我们把它整合起来。”然后我们就把 Audible 放上去了。那是在 96 或 97 年，第一批 Audible 有声书。然后我想：“哦天哪，那是音频。那如果我们把音乐放上去呢？”对吧？然后我去做，当时内存非常小。几乎没有闪存，全是 DRAM。当你做 Audible 时，你从 DRAM 开始，对吧？这可能没问题，因为你需要多少本书呢？就是这个想法。顺便说一句，这很聪明。我的意思是，仅仅把书放在移动设备上就是一个聪明的步骤。我不知道，有时候你无法衡量一项发明带来了多少人类进步。有那些性感的大产品，但你永远不知道。也许维基百科就是那种没有得到足够认可其变革性影响的东西之一。它不被视为最性感的产品，但当你审视人类历史时，也许它是。维基百科可以说是解锁人类知识的重大事物之一。人类知识，还有人类编辑，以及人类共同构建事物的本性。是的，这很迷人。有时候直到几十年后你才能衡量这些东西。总之，抱歉。那就是 Nino。那是存在的。然后有了 Audible。你放了书，为什么不放音乐？而我正带着音乐去 DJ 演出，然后你想：“等等，把二和二加起来。”对吧？比如，让我们摆脱这个。然后 MP3 出现了。实际的编码性能，MP3 格式，出现在 97、98 年左右。所以 MP3 是压缩的，所以你可以大幅减少存储空间。对吧？所以你可以从一个大的、完整的、无损的数字音轨变成可以存储在四到八兆字节的东西，差不多。现在，那是降低了质量，但你可以把它压缩到那里。然后你想：“哦，好吧。”现在如果我们有足够的闪存或 DRAM，我们可以放 10、15 首，随便多少，都在同样的内存里。它开始复制 CD。最终，如果你把它放在硬盘上，你可以开始放几千首歌。

transcript.sec: The Birth of iPod: From Consultant to Product Vision

turns.en: Ideas and things of that nature were and my passions were born into then. The iPod, you know, it was too. Apple needed something and I wanted to fix something, and it all kind of came together at this right place, right time, plus the right technology came at this. It was just like the stars aligned. So how did it come to life? The details of the stars aligning, but the actual design, the actual engineering of getting a device to be small, the storage, the interface, how it looks, the storage, the details of the software, all that kind of stuff. What are some interesting memories from that design process? What are some wisdoms you can part from that process?
turns.zh: 那些想法和我的热情在那时诞生。iPod 也是，苹果需要点什么，而我想修复点什么，一切都在对的地点、对的时间，加上对的技术，就像星辰连成一线。那么它是如何成真的？星辰连线的细节，但实际的设计、实际的工程——让设备变小、存储、界面、外观、软件细节等等。那个设计过程中有哪些有趣的回忆？你能分享哪些智慧？

turns.en: Well, you know how long do you want to go? Because I can go deep. So let's go at least 20 hours. Let's go. Okay, yeah, that's one of those lengthy documentaries. We're gonna turn it into episodic, yeah, binge Game of Thrones. So let's just start with, you know, after I was asked to be a consultant to put this thing together. So I had already had knowledge of the space and the technology and all that stuff, but I had to very quickly, and a lot of the suppliers because of what I was doing at Fuse trying to create that thing. So as a contractor, I was like, okay, what is the first thing you need to do? So after I showed, you know, different architectures and what three different products could be to Steve about options for storage, options for battery, options for form factor, there were three options. And as I was told, given very good advice: give the two options you really do not like, but their options, and give the best option last, because Steve will shoot all those down and give the best option last, and then you could talk about that. And so that was the one that had a 1.8 inch hard drive and a small screen, like with the screen you know, it is not on the original iPod classic iPod. And then I had enough of the idea of the three or four different CPUs and processor suppliers and kind of systems that were out there that I had gone and found and put together on power supplies, you know, disk drive interfaces, FireWire interface, all that stuff. So I put together all of those schematics or block diagrams, they weren't schematics yet because it was just me, and coming up with a bill of materials, coming up with what it could look like, what would be the input output, how we could make a better headphone jack that was also on there, screen suppliers, tearing apart calculators. So I got all calculators and all kinds of electronics to get the right size, different sizes of small LCDs. So I got all kinds of different battery types, I got different types of, you know, in different battery sizes, double A's, AAA's, working through all the different, and there was lithium ion, nickel metal hydride. So I took all the battery types, I took all of the memory types, processing types, LCD types, and connectivity and all that stuff, not wireless but wired, and laid out these things as Lego blocks. So literally I had all of these things as just, and so I made them so I could like, you know, put them together and figure out what the compact form factor would be. Oh, like how do we shove them together? What's the smallest possible box you can get? So the questions without the storage, so the hard drive, batteries, double A, triple A, you're right, screens, so screen size, and then for that you're tearing apart calculators, calculators, digital cameras, whatever, and getting little things right, so you can make it physical, right? If you can make the intangible tangible, like, and so I can say, look, we can make this, and I could bring this whole bag of goods, and it was like, right, like here's this, here's this, this is why double A's won't work, and because it makes it too fat and everything. So just educate everybody through here's the parts that we can use. You should not sheet a paper, it's physical, you're playing in the physical space. Well, I would go back and forth. So truth be told, because there weren't good enough graphical tools on the Mac, I was using a PC with Visio and some 3D tools, and I was doing 3D design at the same time. I was taking all these physical parts and going, okay, what feels right? Because you have to go from the details and then the rough, and you go back and forth and you iterate, right? And so it was just a lot of fun. And then I ultimately ended up with a styrofoam model and printouts that came from Visio that I glued together and put my grandfather's fishing weights in, because I also modeled the weights, right? So I said, oh, this is this many ounces, this is this many ounces in grams, and then I went and got all that and made the weighted these styrofoam models to then match that. So when you picked it up, it felt more or less form factor, right? And it also you felt how much, you know, was it going to be dense enough? Is it going to feel solid and rigid in your hand, right? Why does it need to feel rigid? Because it has to feel substantial, it has to feel like I have a like a bar of gold in my hand, right? You know, maybe you know this when you open and close a car door, you know that thunk and you go bam, and you go that feels solid, that feels real. And then you get this tinny car that's like ding, and you're like, does this feel safe? Does this feel like a value? And so when you have a device like that, you want to make sure that there's not too much air in it, that you've distributed the density of the masses in the right way so it feels like it's the right thing. So you have to model battery life, costs, you know, mass sizes of different things, and then you have to also think about what the UI is going to look like, right? So you have all of these constraints, your work variables you're working with, and you have to kind of, you know, you can't get the perfect of everything. What's the best, you know, local maximum of all of these components that come together to provide an experience? Local max, it's always trade-offs.
turns.zh: 嗯，你想聊多久？我可以深入讲。至少 20 小时吧。来吧。好，那就像一部超长纪录片，我们把它做成剧集，像《权力的游戏》那样追。那就从我被请来做顾问组装这个东西开始。我当时已经了解这个领域和技术，但必须很快——因为我在 Fuse 做的那些事，很多供应商我都认识。作为承包商，我想，第一步该做什么？我给 Steve 展示了三种不同架构和产品方案，关于存储、电池、外形选项。有人给了我很好的建议：先给两个你其实不喜欢的选项，把最好的放最后，因为 Steve 会先毙掉那些，然后你就能讨论最好的那个。那个方案用了 1.8 英寸硬盘和小屏幕——不是初代 iPod classic 那种。然后我研究了市面上三四种 CPU 和处理器供应商，把电源、磁盘接口、FireWire 接口这些都整合起来。我画了框图（还不是原理图，因为就我一个人），列出物料清单，构想外观、输入输出、如何改进耳机插孔，还有屏幕供应商，拆解计算器。我搞来各种计算器和电子设备，找到合适尺寸的小 LCD。我还收集了各种电池类型——AA、AAA、锂离子、镍氢——所有存储、处理器、LCD 类型，以及有线连接方式，把它们像乐高积木一样摆出来。我做了实物模型，这样就能拼在一起，找出最小的紧凑外形。比如，怎么把它们塞进去？能做出多小的盒子？没有存储的话，硬盘、电池、屏幕尺寸……为了做实物，我拆了计算器、数码相机，拿到小零件。如果你能把无形的东西变得有形，我就能说，看，我们能做成这样。我带来一整袋零件，说，这就是为什么 AA 电池不行，因为它太厚了。通过实物零件来教育大家。你不能只画在纸上，这是物理世界。我反复迭代。说实话，因为 Mac 上没有足够好的图形工具，我用 PC 上的 Visio 和一些 3D 工具，同时做 3D 设计。我拿着这些物理零件，感受什么手感对。因为你要从细节到粗略，来回迭代。这很有趣。最后我做了个泡沫模型，把 Visio 打印的图纸粘上去，还放了我祖父的钓鱼铅坠，因为我也模拟了重量。我说，这是多少盎司，多少克，然后配重，让泡沫模型手感接近。当你拿起来时，感觉差不多就是那个外形。你还会感受它够不够密实，在手里是否坚固。为什么需要坚固？因为它必须有分量感，像手里握着一根金条。你知道，开关车门时，那种沉闷的“砰”声让你觉得扎实、真实；而廉价车的“叮”声让你觉得不安全、不值钱。所以设备不能有太多空气，质量密度要分布得当，手感才对。你还要模拟电池寿命、成本、质量大小，同时考虑 UI 设计。所有这些约束都是变量，你不可能做到完美。什么是所有组件组合起来提供体验的最佳局部最大值？局部最大值，永远是权衡。

turns.en: What about buttons?
turns.zh: 那按钮呢？

turns.en: Well, there was also the buttons too, right? Oh, by the way, a lot of these battles fought inside your mind or is it with other people? Is it with Steve? Is it lower? Like, what this was all independent. This was me before being able to present to Steve, because I had to feel really confident that if I was going to put this in front of him, that it could be made, right? So I had to convince myself and go work through all the details through the very, very rough mechanical design, electrical design, software things, because I didn't want to present something that was going to be fictional, right? My credibility would be like trashed, right?
turns.zh: 按钮也是，对吧？顺便问一句，这些斗争是在你脑子里，还是跟别人？跟 Steve？还是更低层级？其实这完全是我独立完成的。在我能向 Steve 展示之前，我必须非常确信这东西能造出来。所以我得说服自己，把非常粗糙的机械设计、电气设计、软件细节都过一遍，因为我不想展示一个虚构的东西，那样我的信誉就毁了。

turns.en: You mentioned convince yourself. You're painting this beautiful picture of a driven engineer, designer, futurist. How much doubt were you played by through that? Like, is this even doable? Because it's not...
turns.zh: 你提到说服自己。你在描绘一个充满动力的工程师、设计师、未来学家的美好画面。在这个过程中，你被多少怀疑困扰？比如，这真的可行吗？因为这不是……

transcript.secZh: iPod诞生：从顾问到产品愿景

transcript.sec: Doubt and Confidence in Building the iPod

turns.en: You already were sitting on a track record of successes and failures in your own mind where you had sort of already a confidence, a calmness. But still, was there a doubt that you could get this done?
turns.zh: 你心里已经有了成功和失败的记录，你已经有了一种自信和冷静。但即便如此，你仍然怀疑自己能否完成这件事吗？

turns.en: Always. Always.
turns.zh: 一直都有。一直都有。

turns.en: 要达到那种能向史蒂夫展示并真正相信这件事可行的自信有多难？你对那个时刻有什么记忆？
Yeah, I think it was after I triple-checked. I couldn't bring anyone in, right? I couldn't let anyone in on this, so it was just me.
turns.zh: 是的，我想那是在我反复确认之后。我不能让任何人参与进来，对吧？我不能让任何人知道这件事，所以只有我一个人。

turns.en: Are they going to trample on it, that kind of thing?
turns.zh: 他们会不会把它搞砸，诸如此类？

turns.en: No, no, because I couldn't bring anyone in on this one. It was a highly confidential program inside of Apple. There were like four people who knew about it, right? And so I couldn't bring anyone from Apple because I was a contractor. I couldn't bring anyone else from the outside world. I'm working for Apple and I'm under this crazy NDA, right, in this contract. So it was just me. So I'm doing this. Oh, and at the same time, I'm also buying every competitive product — MP3 players — and tearing them all apart, yeah, right? Tore them all apart and looking at them and trying to learn from those as well. So it was all of this stuff in six weeks. So I didn't sleep, right? Yeah, yeah. But I was like, because I was trying to make this from — I was envisioning this since the Nino, right? And I was like, oh my god, right? But there was another doubt that I had, and it wasn't just could you make the product, but could Apple actually have the balls to make it? Because Apple was not the same company that you know today. In 2001, really, it was cautious, conservative, careful. It was barely break-even. It was a four or five billion dollar company.
turns.zh: 不，不，因为我不能带任何人参与这个项目。这是苹果内部一个高度机密的项目，只有大约四个人知道，对吧？所以我不能从苹果内部带人，因为我是合同工。我也不能从外面带任何人。我在为苹果工作，签了疯狂的保密协议，对吧？所以只有我一个人。所以我就在做这件事。哦，同时，我还在购买所有竞争产品——MP3 播放器——然后把它们全部拆开，对吧？全部拆开，研究它们，并试图从中学习。所以所有这些事情都在六周内完成。所以我没睡觉，对吧？是的，是的。但我当时想，因为我从 Nino 时代就开始构想这个了，对吧？我当时想，哦，天哪，对吧？但我还有另一个疑虑，不仅仅是你能不能做出产品，而是苹果是否真的有勇气去做它？因为苹果不是今天你所知道的那家公司。在 2001 年，真的，它很谨慎、保守、小心翼翼。它几乎不盈利。它是一家四五十亿美元的公司。

turns.en: So the guts required there is not necessarily in the innovation. It's like this is going to cost a lot of money and we're going to potentially lose all of it because it'll be a flop.
turns.zh: 所以那里需要的勇气不一定是在创新上。而是这要花很多钱，我们可能会全部亏掉，因为它会失败。

turns.en: Well, there's not just that, but there was only the Mac, yeah, and the Mac wasn't doing very well. Yeah, there was less — it was about a one percent only in the US market share for the Mac, right? The company was in debt. Bill Gates had to give him a loan, right? Michael Dell at the time was saying shut down the company and give the money back to the shareholders. So this is not the company that you know — that people, oh my god, the iPhone came out — it's a very different level of confidence and financial situation that the company was in versus the iPod.
turns.zh: 嗯，不仅如此，而且当时只有 Mac，是的，Mac 做得不太好。是的，Mac 在美国的市场份额只有大约 1%，对吧？公司负债累累。比尔·盖茨不得不给他贷款，对吧？当时的迈克尔·戴尔说关掉公司，把钱还给股东。所以这不是你所知道的那家公司——人们，哦，天哪，iPhone 出来了——公司当时的信心和财务状况与 iPod 时期截然不同。

transcript.sec: The Presentation to Steve Jobs

turns.en: Given that, what was the conversation when you finally presented to Steve? What was that conversation like?
turns.zh: 鉴于此，当你最终向史蒂夫展示时，你们的对话是怎样的？

turns.en: The conversation was — well, we went through it, the presentation and all that stuff happened, and he was just like — and you know, he never — he would flip through it real quick, throw the presentation aside, and said, okay, let's talk about this, right? And so we went through it all. And one was a big conversation about Sony. And Sony was the number one in all audio categories — home, portable, what in the world. Okay, I had already been gone through 10 years of failure, and I was like, wait a second, how are we going to compete with Sony? And I was always worried that Sony was going to come out with whatever it was they were going to come out with — their MP3 player — and that was it, game over, right? And so I was like, Steve — and this is why it took me four weeks to finally sign on to join Apple after he greenlit the iPod program in that meeting — was because I had built other things in the past at Philips, the Nino and Velo, but they didn't know how to sell it or market it, they didn't know how to retail it, right? So I was like, we could build this. And I was like, Steve, I'm pretty sure I can build this, I've done this before. But how are we going to sell it? You have all your marketing dollars on the Mac. And he looked at me and he goes, you build this with your team and our team and Apple open business and me, right? And I dedicate that we will make sure that at least two quarters of all marketing dollars will only go to this product and nothing else. Wow, right? That was Mac was the lifeblood of all revenue of the company. So Steve saw something special here, exactly. And he said, I'm going to commit all the marketing dollars if you can deliver the experience that we're all talking about. If we can do that — and that was Jeff Robbin as well, because iPod would have never happened without iTunes, you know? People don't understand that was a bundle. You couldn't do one without the other, and vice versa. So Jeff and I were — you know, if Jeff and you can present and bring that experience to life, I will put all the marketing dollars behind it.
turns.zh: 对话是这样的——嗯，我们进行了演示，所有那些事情都发生了，他就像——你知道，他从不——他会快速翻看演示文稿，把它扔到一边，然后说，好吧，我们来谈谈这个，对吧？所以我们讨论了所有内容。其中一个是关于索尼的大讨论。索尼在所有音频类别中都是第一——家庭、便携，无论什么。好吧，我已经经历了 10 年的失败，我当时想，等等，我们怎么和索尼竞争？我一直担心索尼会推出他们将要推出的任何产品——他们的 MP3 播放器——然后游戏就结束了，对吧？所以我就想，史蒂夫——这就是为什么在他那次会议上批准 iPod 项目后，我花了四个星期才最终签约加入苹果的原因——是因为我以前在飞利浦做过其他东西，Nino 和 Velo，但他们不知道如何销售或营销，他们不知道如何零售，对吧？所以我想，我们可以做出这个。然后我说，史蒂夫，我很确定我能做出这个，我以前做过。但我们怎么卖呢？你所有的营销预算都花在 Mac 上。他看着我，然后说，你和你的团队以及我们的团队、苹果开放业务和我一起做，对吧？我保证，至少两个季度的所有营销预算将只用于这个产品，而不是其他任何东西。哇，对吧？那时 Mac 是公司所有收入的命脉。所以史蒂夫看到了特别的东西，没错。他说，如果你能实现我们都在谈论的体验，我就投入所有的营销预算。如果我们能做到——还有杰夫·罗宾，因为没有 iTunes，iPod 永远不会发生，你知道吗？人们不明白那是一个捆绑。你不能只做一个而不做另一个，反之亦然。所以杰夫和我——你知道，如果杰夫和你能呈现并让那种体验成为现实，我会把所有的营销预算都投进去。

transcript.sec: The Marriage of iPod and iTunes

turns.en: When did the marriage of iPod and iTunes sort of — what was that a birth of ideas that made up iTunes?
turns.zh: iPod 和 iTunes 的结合是什么时候——构成 iTunes 的想法是如何诞生的？

turns.en: iTunes existed before the iPod, okay? And so Jeff Robbin had his company — oh man, I can't remember her name, but it was bought. He was making an MP3 player app for the Mac. Steve saw it because there were MP3 player apps like Winamp and other things that were on the PC, RealPlayer. And Steve saw that going on and saw that Jeff and his small team had this — I can't remember, Sound something — anyways, he bought that and that became the basis of iTunes. And then Jeff ran all of iTunes. And so what happened specifically there was they were starting to hook up to all these third-party MP3 players, because there's a lot of Korean — the MP Man, like Walkman but MP Man — all these, and they were trying to hook them up and they were like, these are horrible experiences. And through that, and they said iTunes was —
turns.zh: iTunes 在 iPod 之前就存在了，好吗？所以杰夫·罗宾有他的公司——哦，天哪，我记不起名字了，但它被收购了。他在为 Mac 制作一个 MP3 播放器应用。史蒂夫看到了它，因为 PC 上有像 Winamp 和其他东西这样的 MP3 播放器应用，RealPlayer。史蒂夫看到了这种情况，看到杰夫和他的小团队有这个——我记不清了，Sound 什么的——总之，他收购了它，这成为了 iTunes 的基础。然后杰夫管理整个 iTunes。所以具体发生的事情是，他们开始连接所有这些第三方 MP3 播放器，因为有很多韩国的——MP Man，就像 Walkman 但叫 MP Man——所有这些，他们试图连接它们，然后他们说，这些体验太糟糕了。通过那个，他们说 iTunes 是——

transcript.sec: The iPod origin story

turns.en: Something that was going to help grow the Mac base because we were trying to get more people on the Mac. So this program would be a great new thing you could add to the Mac, and there was also internet connectivity at the time for the iMac. And so they did that, and then they were trying to do these hookups, they weren't going well. And that's when they said we need to build our own, or Steve said we need to build our own since these are such horrible experiences. People don't want to just burn CDs from iTunes, we need to get that music on the go but in an Apple fashion. That's when I was called to come in to do that, the iPod thing, after the six weeks. Then he already envisioned — I'm sure he had envisioned because they were trying to do this thing. Okay, now that's it. iTunes, what you know, it wasn't called iPod yet, you know what would become the iPod, that is going to be the thing that then propels Apple into this new thing because you bring all these music lovers in that are going to need their next generation or Sony Walkman version 2.0. So when you look at — again, apologies to linger on iPod, but it's one of the great inventions in tech history — what wisdom do you draw from that whole process about spotting an idea? This is something you talk about in your book Build. How do you know that an idea is brilliant at which stage? When did you know it was a good idea? And maybe is there like some phase shifts: first your complete doubt, then maybe hmm, and then maybe it becomes more than and becomes like a little more confidence, that kind of stuff. And also wisdom about who to talk to, right, so they don't trample the idea in the early stages, that kind of stuff. And your thoughts about this.
turns.zh: 当时有个项目是为了帮助扩大 Mac 的用户基础，因为我们想让更多人用上 Mac。所以这个项目会成为 Mac 上一个很棒的新功能，而且那时候 iMac 已经有了网络连接。他们尝试了那些合作，但进展不顺利。于是他们说我们需要自己造一个，或者 Steve 说我们需要自己造一个，因为那些体验太糟糕了。人们不想只从 iTunes 刻录 CD，我们需要让音乐随身携带，但要以 Apple 的方式。那就是我被叫来参与 iPod 项目的时候，在六周之后。他已经预见到了——我确信他预见到了，因为他们已经在尝试做这件事。好了，就是这样。iTunes，你知道的，那时候还不叫 iPod，你知道后来会成为 iPod 的东西，那将推动 Apple 进入新领域，因为你吸引了所有音乐爱好者，他们需要下一代或索尼 Walkman 2.0 版本。所以当你回顾——再次抱歉，在 iPod 上多花点时间，但它是科技史上最伟大的发明之一——从整个过程中，关于发现一个想法，你得到了什么智慧？这是你在《Build》一书中谈到的。你怎么知道一个想法在哪个阶段是 brilliant 的？你什么时候知道它是一个好主意？也许有一些阶段转变：先是完全怀疑，然后嗯，然后可能变得更多，信心多一点，诸如此类。还有关于该和谁谈的智慧，对吧，这样他们就不会在早期阶段践踏这个想法，诸如此类。你的想法是什么？

turns.en: We could go on again, how long do you want to go? This is a Netflix series, I told you, multi-season. So a lot of lessons learned over those years of failure and success. But the first thing it starts with — there's a whole chapter called "Great Ideas Chase You." And so it kind of goes into in Build, and you can go and it goes through kind of chapter and verse about all of those how Nest became into being. But let's talk about it specifically for iPod, right. So for me, I always had pain: the pain of carrying these CDs everywhere, right. And I had the joy of music, right. If you could say all of a sudden I could get the music I love all the time in a portable package, and I can have all the music I love all the time. I was solving a pain, which was for me it was thousands of these, other people might be 10 or 15 CDs, right. And then I can have the joy of all this music uninterrupted. That was taking the pain, making a painkiller for it. And then at the end was a superpower, an emotional superpower that said, "Oh my, this is something different." So when you can actually focus on a pain — not of not — and get a painkiller for it, not a vitamin. So the difference between a painkiller and a vitamin is very clear: one you need, "I got to get rid of this pain"; a vitamin, maybe it works, maybe it doesn't, maybe somebody needs it, maybe not, it's all marketing story, right. So you start with the pain, give them a painkiller, and hopefully if you can do it in the right way, you give them a superpower, an emotional superpower that is always. And that's the way to know that you're hitting on something that's really powerful: the pain and the joy. Exactly.
turns.zh: 我们可以继续聊，你想聊多久？我跟你说过，这是一部 Netflix 系列剧，多季的。所以这些年从失败和成功中学到了很多。但首先，它始于——有一整章叫“伟大的想法追逐你”。在《Build》里，它详细讲述了 Nest 是如何诞生的。但我们专门谈谈 iPod 吧。对我来说，我一直有痛苦：随身携带这些 CD 的痛苦，对吧。而且我有音乐的快乐，对吧。如果你说突然之间，我可以随时随地在便携设备上听到我爱的音乐，并且我可以一直拥有所有我爱的音乐。我在解决一个痛苦，对我来说是几千张 CD，其他人可能是 10 或 15 张 CD，对吧。然后我可以不间断地享受所有这些音乐的快乐。那是把痛苦变成止痛药。最后，它变成了一种超能力，一种情感上的超能力，说：“哦，天哪，这不一样了。”所以当你真正专注于一个痛苦——不是没有——并为之找到止痛药，而不是维生素。止痛药和维生素的区别很清楚：一个是你需要的，“我必须摆脱这种痛苦”；维生素，也许有效，也许无效，也许有人需要，也许不需要，全是营销故事，对吧。所以从痛苦开始，给他们止痛药，希望如果你做得对，你给他们一种超能力，一种情感上的超能力，始终如此。这就是你知道自己触及了真正强大的东西的方式：痛苦和快乐。没错。

turns.en: Are you always aware of the pain? So it seems like a lot of great products, it's like we do a lot of painful things and we just kind of assume that's the way it's supposed to be. Like with much autonomous vehicles, we'll all assume we're supposed to be driving, right? And it doesn't — you don't think of it as a pain, right? Well, you've habituated it away.
turns.zh: 你总是能意识到痛苦吗？似乎很多伟大的产品，我们做了很多痛苦的事情，却只是认为本该如此。比如自动驾驶汽车，我们都假设我们应该开车，对吧？而且你并不认为那是痛苦，对吧？你已经习惯了。

turns.en: Yeah, you've habituated it away. For me, you know, when I go to other places, living in Bali or living in Paris or whatever, and I'm not driving, I'm walking or using a scooter or what have you, different thing. And you go, "Oh my god," when you left that environment because everyone else is driving all the time, you're like, "That's what you do." And you find out there's other ways of living, and there's freedom when you get rid of that. You're like, "Oh my god, I didn't know that this was so much better." So there's something in the book that's called — I deemed it the "Virus of Doubt." And what the Virus of Doubt is, is when there's pain and it's been habituated away, you use the right marketing messages to bring people back to that initial experience they had, or the initial experience that they had of that pain. "Do you remember when the first time you did blah and it felt like this?" Right. And then you reawaken that habituated pain, and people — it becomes visceral, and then they're like, "Oh yes, I hate that." And then you go, "Now I have the painkiller and the joy for you." Yes, that's when it all comes together.
turns.zh: 是的，你已经习惯了。对我来说，你知道，当我去其他地方，住在巴厘岛或巴黎之类的地方，我不开车，我走路或用滑板车，不同的事情。你会说，“哦，天哪，”当你离开那个环境，因为其他人一直在开车，你会想，“那就是你做的事。”然后你发现还有其他的生活方式，摆脱它之后有自由。你会说，“哦，天哪，我不知道原来这么好。”所以书里有一章叫——我称之为“怀疑病毒”。怀疑病毒的意思是，当存在痛苦并且已经被习惯化时，你用正确的营销信息把人们带回到他们最初的体验，或者他们对那种痛苦的初始体验。“你还记得第一次做某事时感觉像这样吗？”对吧。然后你重新唤醒那种习惯化的痛苦，人们——它变得发自内心，然后他们会说，“哦，是的，我讨厌那个。”然后你说，“现在我有止痛药和快乐给你。”是的，那时一切就都对了。

turns.en: Let me on this on the pain and the joy, that's brilliantly put. You mentioned selling and marketing, right, marketing dollars. I have a love-hate relationship with marketing, like with a lot of things that require artistic genius. To me, the best marketing I suppose is the product itself and then word of mouth. So like create a thing that people love. Oh, absolutely, that's fundamental. Yeah. But so any other marketing requires genius to be any extra thing because every — to me, I don't — it's yeah. Maybe you can, by way of question because I'm just speaking off the top of my head as a consumer, what is great marketing? What does it take to reveal the pain and the joy of a thing?
turns.zh: 让我谈谈痛苦和快乐，说得太好了。你提到了销售和营销，对吧，营销预算。我对营销又爱又恨，就像很多需要艺术天才的事情一样。对我来说，最好的营销我想就是产品本身，然后是口碑。所以创造人们喜欢的东西。哦，绝对，那是根本。是的。但任何其他营销都需要天才才能成为额外的东西，因为对我来说，我不——是的。也许你可以，以提问的方式，因为我只是作为消费者随口说说，什么是伟大的营销？需要什么来揭示一件事的痛苦和快乐？

turns.en: Okay, it all starts at the beginning. And let me give you — I'm going to give you a couple of different ways of looking at it, okay. And again, we're going to might go a little long here, so just stay tuned in. Yeah. So the first thing is the beginning. Let's start at the beginning. In the early part of my career, you know, like General Magic and Philips and what have you, and especially when I was a teenager, when I was like doing making my own chips and stuff like that, I really worried about just putting cool things together. I'm like, "When I put those two cool things together as an engineer, you go, 'That's cool.'" And then I would talk to the other friends who might be geeks too, and they go, "Yeah, that's cool," because we knew the bits, so we put them together and that's a new way of doing it. You're like, "Wow, that's all." What? It's not. "Why are you doing this?" "We know what we're doing, but we don't know why we're doing it," because we're not articulating for ourselves because it's just something we're like putting it together like, "Yeah, that's cool," because we think we're solving some problem we have, but we're not really articulating it. So what normally happens — and this happens because we invest in so many companies around the world — you have these brilliant engineers, designers, scientists, researchers, they put together these "whats" and then they develop it, develop it, develop it, and then at the end they call in marketing and say, "Now tell a story about this." And let's —
turns.zh: 好的，一切都从开始说起。我给你——我给你几种不同的看法，好吗。再说一次，我们可能会聊得有点长，所以请继续听。是的。第一件事是开始。让我们从开始说起。在我职业生涯的早期，你知道，像 General Magic 和 Philips 之类的公司，尤其是我十几岁的时候，当我做自己的芯片之类的东西时，我真的只关心把酷东西拼在一起。我想，“当我作为一个工程师把这两个酷东西放在一起时，你会说，‘那很酷。’”然后我会和其他可能也是极客的朋友聊，他们说，“是啊，很酷，”因为我们知道那些比特，所以我们把它们放在一起，这是一种新的做法。你会想，“哇，就是这样。”什么？不是的。“你为什么这么做？”“我们知道我们在做什么，但我们不知道为什么这么做，”因为我们没有为自己阐明，因为那只是我们拼凑起来的东西，就像，“是啊，很酷，”因为我们以为我们在解决某个问题，但我们并没有真正阐明它。所以通常发生的是——这会发生，因为我们在世界各地投资了很多公司——你有这些 brilliant 的工程师、设计师、科学家、研究人员，他们把这些“什么”拼凑起来，然后开发、开发、开发，最后他们叫来营销人员说，“现在讲个故事吧。”然后——

transcript.sec: The Press Release as a Product Treatment

turns.en: Get it out to the world. Okay, what happens then is marketing is like, 'Well, why do people need this? Tell us why people need it.' And so they create a story around this product. But the product was born out of what's not wise. And so they start telling — marketing starts telling a story — and it turns out to be a fictional story usually. They say, 'Oh, this is going to do these things.' The product comes in, is delivered, and it falls flat on its face because the marketing doesn't match the product, because they weren't both created at the beginning together, right? There are what's when you create a product, but there's a lot more Y's. And the Y's help inform the what's, and the Y's also inform the marketing. So that's what you mean deeply at: we should start at the beginning. So the designer should be also the marketer, the engineer should be the marketer. Exactly. Stop impressing the geek next to you. What is the superpower you're bringing or the pain you're killing for the end customer? Right now, let's contrast that. Think about a movie. A movie starts with a treatment. It has an audience that says — the audience, here's the characters, here's the storyline, the plot, here's the arc of the story, right? It pulls that all out. Then there's a script that's written, and that script is then produced, and then you add all the flourishes and what have you — music and graphics and what have you, right? And then it comes out, and then there's the marketing of the movie. And that story was created at the beginning. What you need to do if you're going to do a great product is create that treatment for your product. And I call that the press release. Do the press release like the treatment. Who's the audience? What features do you have? What pains are you solving for people? Have the virus of doubt there to remind them what pains they have and why you're solving them. The price, all of those things. And you use that as the bar, the measuring stick for what you do during development. Because what happens along the route, you know, 'Oh, we're not going to be able to get that feature done on time — throw that one overboard. We got to hit the date. Oh, we're not sure this product's right yet — add another feature, add another feature.' Feature creep, right? If you don't have that story, you know you're going to tell at the beginning, you don't have that bar, right? And then at the end, you don't know when you're done. If you don't have that story, so you can actually look at that press release. You know, you might modify it over time, that draft. But then when you're done, you know the what's and the why's. You have all the things of the audience and everything. And then you can give that to marketing and say — well, marketing's been along the way, let's be clear — but then everybody's in sync. And that's when you can tell a cohesive, non-fictional story about, and the product delivers on that story, or hopefully over-delivers on that story.
turns.zh: 把它推向世界。好的，接下来会发生的是，市场部会问：“嗯，为什么人们需要这个？告诉我们为什么人们需要它。”于是他们围绕这个产品编了一个故事。但产品本身源于“什么是不明智的”。然后他们开始讲故事——市场部开始讲故事——结果通常是一个虚构的故事。他们说：“哦，这能实现这些功能。”产品上市、交付，然后彻底失败，因为营销和产品不匹配，因为它们从一开始就不是一起创造的，对吧？当你创造产品时，有很多“为什么”。这些“为什么”帮助定义“是什么”，同时也指导营销。所以这就是你深层的含义：我们应该从起点开始。因此设计师也应该是营销人员，工程师也应该是营销人员。没错。别再取悦你旁边的极客了。你为最终用户带来的超能力是什么？或者你为他们消除了什么痛苦？现在，我们来对比一下。想想一部电影。电影从一份剧情梗概开始。它有一个目标观众——观众，这是角色，这是故事线，这是情节，这是故事的弧光，对吧？它把所有东西都梳理出来。然后写出剧本，剧本被制作出来，然后你添加各种修饰——音乐、画面等等，对吧？然后电影上映，接着是电影的营销。而那个故事从一开始就创建好了。如果你想做出一款伟大的产品，你需要为你的产品创建一份类似的剧情梗概。我称之为“新闻稿”。像写剧情梗概一样写新闻稿。目标受众是谁？你有哪些功能？你为用户解决了什么痛苦？引入“怀疑病毒”来提醒他们有什么痛苦，以及你为什么解决这些痛苦。价格，所有这些东西。你把它作为开发过程中的衡量标准和标尺。因为在过程中会发生这种情况：“哦，我们无法按时完成那个功能——把它扔到船外。我们必须赶上日期。哦，我们还不确定这个产品对不对——再加一个功能，再加一个功能。”功能蔓延，对吧？如果你没有那个故事——你知道你从一开始就要讲的故事——你就没有那个标尺，对吧？然后到最后，你不知道什么时候算完成。如果你没有那个故事，你就无法真正对照那份新闻稿。你知道，你可能会随着时间的推移修改那份草稿。但当你完成时，你知道了“是什么”和“为什么”。你有了关于受众的所有信息。然后你可以把它交给市场部——当然，市场部一路都在参与——但这样大家就同步了。这时你才能讲一个连贯的、非虚构的故事，产品也兑现了那个故事，或者希望是超额兑现。

turns.en: So in the drafting from the beginning to the end of the press release, what does a successful team look like? Who is part of the draft? Is it engineers, designers? What's the purpose of a marketing department in a company? Small, let's say a small company but more than two people. Okay, so from where does the why come from? Should it always come from the designer, or should there be a marketing person that steps in and asks the question?
turns.zh: 那么，从新闻稿的起草到完成，一个成功的团队是什么样的？谁参与起草？是工程师、设计师吗？在一个公司里，市场部的目的是什么？比如一个小公司，但不止两个人。好的，那么“为什么”从何而来？它应该总是来自设计师，还是应该有一个营销人员介入并提出问题？

turns.en: So I'll just keep asking random questions. I know these are great questions. Okay, so it's because you're just like — I'm like, 'I can't wait to tell you the answer.' Yeah. Um, so it's in the book as well, but you have to separate out the various functions of marketing. When that's what I thought — I was like, 'Marketing's marketing,' you know? When I was — and it's really not. There's so many disciplines, just like in engineering: mechanical, electrical, software, and even software it's you know, use cloud services, firmware, applications. Marketing has that much diversity as well, okay? And you have to honor that. And so there is marketing communications, like PR, press. There is social marketing. There is marketing creative, right? There's marketing activation. But there's another thing that also comes out and people confuse it with marketing, which is called product marketing or product management. And product management or product marketing is the voice of the customer. They're the person who sits there and listens to what's going on and the competition in the marketplace, understanding the needs and those pains of the customer, and they're representing them in every single meeting so things don't get off track, right? So that — and they're creating the messages, not the marketing. What happens is there's messages that product marketing creates, like those are the deep messages, like 'We need to save 20% of energy,' let's say, right? And then marketing turns that into something that's with creative and everything and brings that message across — maybe it doesn't say that but it comes maybe visually or some other way. So product management does that and holds that press release along the route and makes sure that we're tracking. And then also marketing is tracking with that press release to make sure they're not telling a fictional story, right? Because they could also add extra adjectives or something and then the product can't deliver that. It's like, 'No, no, no, no, no.' Keeps everybody grounded to the press release, to the raw, right? To the customer needs, yeah. Right, because they're always representing the customer. So you have to have a product manager — typically that's the founder right in the beginning — and then over time you hire a product management team to then really, you know, watch over this the whole way. And they are talking to customer support, they're talking to engineering, they're talking to design, they're talking to sales and marketing, and they are always in the mix. And it's the hardest thing to hire for. Oh, yeah.
turns.zh: 那我就继续随机提问。我知道这些都是好问题。好的，因为你就像——我就像，“我等不及要告诉你答案了。”是的。嗯，书里也提到了，但你必须区分营销的各种职能。当我以为——我过去想，“营销就是营销”，你知道吗？但事实并非如此。营销有很多学科，就像工程学一样：机械、电气、软件，甚至软件也分为云服务、固件、应用。营销也有同样多的多样性，好吗？你必须尊重这一点。所以有营销传播，比如公关、媒体。有社交媒体营销。有营销创意，对吧？有营销激活。但还有另一件事，人们常常把它和营销混淆，那就是产品营销或产品管理。产品管理或产品营销是客户的声音。他们是坐在那里倾听市场动态和竞争情况、理解客户需求和痛点的人，他们在每一次会议中代表客户，这样事情就不会偏离轨道，对吧？而且他们创造信息，而不是营销。实际情况是，产品营销创造信息，比如深层信息，比如“我们需要节省 20% 的能源”，对吧？然后营销部门用创意等方式将其转化，传达那个信息——也许不是直接说出来，而是通过视觉或其他方式。所以产品管理做这些事，并一路持有那份新闻稿，确保我们跟踪进度。同时营销部门也跟踪那份新闻稿，确保他们不是在讲虚构的故事，对吧？因为他们也可能添加额外的形容词之类的东西，然后产品无法兑现。就像，“不，不，不，不，不。”它让每个人都扎根于新闻稿，扎根于原始需求，对吧？扎根于客户需求。是的。因为他们始终代表客户。所以你必须有一个产品经理——通常一开始就是创始人——然后随着时间的推移，你雇佣一个产品管理团队来真正全程监督。他们与客户支持、工程、设计、销售和营销沟通，他们始终参与其中。这是最难招聘的职位。哦，是的。

turns.en: Why is it the hardest to hire for?
turns.zh: 为什么这是最难招聘的？

turns.en: Because you have to understand — you first, nobody reports to you, right? You're allowed — so you're alone. And you have to build great ties with all of these different functions. Yes. You have to understand what they do, have be empathetic with what they do. And you have to get — have to project the customer's empathy or empathy for the customer to them and tell them why and why this customer needs us, why this doesn't work. And so that they learn more — they're not just doing, but they learn about the customer's point of view and sit in there and stand in their shoes to be able to then make better decisions on the engineering details or the operational details, customer support details. So they understand the — if they're not the customer that it's intended for, they start to live through and through their eyes and see through their eyes of that customer so they make better decisions.
turns.zh: 因为你必须理解——首先，没有人向你汇报，对吧？你被允许——所以你孤身一人。你必须与所有这些不同的职能部门建立牢固的联系。是的。你必须理解他们做什么，对他们的工作有同理心。而且你必须——必须将客户的同理心投射给他们，告诉他们为什么以及为什么这个客户需要我们，为什么这个行不通。这样他们就能学到更多——他们不仅仅是执行，而是学习客户的观点，设身处地，从而在工程细节、运营细节、客户支持细节上做出更好的决策。所以如果他们不是目标客户，他们就开始通过客户的眼睛去生活和观察，从而做出更好的决策。

transcript.secZh: 新闻稿作为产品说明书

transcript.sec: Tension between engineering and communications

turns.en: There's probably fascinating beautiful tensions between that and sort of the engineers developing the what exactly. And which makes it an extra hard job, I'm sure.
turns.zh: 这之间可能存在着迷人而美妙的张力，与那些开发产品的工程师之间。而且这肯定让工作更难了。

turns.en: Exactly.
turns.zh: 确实。

turns.en: Can I ask a sort of a little bit of a personal question? One subfield of marketing mentioned comms in PR. How do I ask that? I can hear your struggle in your thigh. Why do the comms and PR folks sometimes kill the heart and soul of the magic that makes a company? Or is that wrong to say?
turns.zh: 我能问一个有点私人的问题吗？营销的一个子领域提到了公关传播。我该怎么问呢？我能听出你的纠结。为什么公关传播人员有时会扼杀公司魔力的核心和灵魂？还是说这么说不对？

turns.en: Give me an example. I will say the spirit of the example, which is it feels like often the jobs of communications is to provide caution. It almost works together with legal to say, 'Yeah, we should probably not say this. We're probably, let's be careful, let's be careful.' Now that makes sense, except in this modern world, authenticity is extremely valuable, and revealing the beauty that is in the engineering, the beauty of the ideas, the chaos of the ideas, I think requires throwing caution to the wind to some degree. I agree. And I just find that boy, I mean it's a really so to push back on myself, I think it's an extremely difficult job because people hold you responsible if you're doing communications when you take risks, right? And especially when they fail. So it's a difficult job, so I understand why people become cautious. But to me, communications is about taking big risks and throwing caution into the wind at its best, because your job is to communicate in the long term, communicate the genius, the joy, the genius of the products, right? And that sometimes is a tension with caution.
turns.zh: 我给你举个例子。我想说的是，传播工作往往就是提供谨慎。它几乎和法律部门一起说：'对，我们可能不该这么说。我们最好小心点，小心点。' 这有道理，但在当今世界，真实性极其宝贵，而揭示工程之美、创意之美、创意之混乱，我认为在某种程度上需要把谨慎抛到脑后。我同意。而且我发现，天哪，我的意思是，这真的——我反驳一下自己——我认为这是一份极其困难的工作，因为如果你在传播中冒险，人们会追究你的责任，尤其是当冒险失败的时候。所以这是一份艰难的工作，我理解为什么人们会变得谨慎。但对我来说，传播的本质就是承担大风险，把谨慎抛到脑后，因为你的工作是长期沟通，传达产品的天才、喜悦和天才，对吧？而这有时会与谨慎产生矛盾。

turns.en: Sorry, so I, because I've gotten a chance to meet a lot of very interesting people and interesting engineering teams and so on, I look at what they're doing and I look at what's being communicated, and it's just there's a mismatch because the communication is a lot more boring. It's like there's something very, just straight up boring about the way they're communicating because of caution.
turns.zh: 抱歉，我因为有机会见到很多非常有趣的人和有趣的工程团队等等，我看着他们在做什么，再看看他们传达的内容，就觉得不匹配，因为传播内容无聊得多。他们的沟通方式因为谨慎而变得非常、直接了当的无聊。

turns.en: Okay, and you have just teed me up for another diatribe. Okay, I'm gonna get on my podium here. Yes, please. It all comes out of the leader. If the leader doesn't know how to storytell, or the leader doesn't know how to do bold storytelling, then you get even more conservatism from the PR and communications folks, because they're always so—if you have a not bold leader, they're always going to be a filter, right? They're always going to try to smooth things out and take off the rough edges, and try—so they're going to be even more—if you have a conservative messaging leader, you're going to have even a more conservative communications department. Why? Because they want to keep their jobs. Okay, it's really simple. Yes, they got to keep their jobs. If they say one wrong thing, it could be the end of it. So if you have a very conservative leader, they're going to be even more conservative. If you have a bold leader, there will be—oh, they'll always take a little more conservative bent, but you're still going to have bold communications. Yeah, that's brilliant.
turns.zh: 好了，你正好给我搭了个台子，让我再来一番长篇大论。好的，我要站上我的讲台了。请便。这一切都源于领导者。如果领导者不会讲故事，或者不会大胆讲故事，那么公关和传播人员就会更加保守，因为他们总是——如果你有一个不大胆的领导者，他们就会一直充当过滤器，对吧？他们总是试图把事情抹平，去掉棱角，试图——所以他们会更加——如果你有一个保守的信息传达领导者，你的传播部门就会更加保守。为什么？因为他们想保住工作。就这么简单。是的，他们得保住饭碗。要是说错一句话，可能就完了。所以如果你有一个非常保守的领导者，他们会更加保守。如果你有一个大胆的领导者，他们会——哦，他们总会稍微偏向保守一点，但你仍然会有大胆的传播。没错，这很精彩。

turns.en: Okay, so it starts with the leader. Now that said, when you think about the messages and the joy and revealing things, right, many of these leaders don't tell great stories. So what we do at Future Shape, our investment firm, is we take those scientists and all the great minds and everything, and what do we surround them with? Marketing and communication people and storytellers to give them the confidence to tell a much broader story about the impacts of what they're creating and how big the global change can be with those technologies. Because usually they don't—those leaders who created those technologies, they don't really know how to communicate really well, and they don't feel very comfortable in how they speak.
turns.zh: 好的，所以这始于领导者。话虽如此，当你思考信息、喜悦和揭示事物时，对吧，很多这些领导者并不擅长讲故事。所以我们在 Future Shape（我们的投资公司）所做的，就是把这些科学家和所有伟大的头脑聚集起来，然后用什么包围他们？营销、传播人员和讲故事的人，给他们信心，去讲述一个更宏大的故事，关于他们创造的影响，以及这些技术能带来多大的全球变革。因为通常他们不会——那些创造了这些技术的领导者，并不真正知道如何很好地沟通，他们对自己的表达方式感到不太自在。

turns.en: Yeah, so it's interesting because stories—and I'm a huge fan of stories—have you ever read the book Story by Robert McKee? You should read this. And this is what I read when I was 26.
turns.zh: 是的，所以这很有趣，因为故事——我是故事的超级粉丝——你读过罗伯特·麦基的《故事》吗？你应该读读这本书。这是我 26 岁时读的。

transcript.sec: Storytelling and Emotion in Product Design

turns.en: Story by Robert McKee is a book all about script writing, the prototypical types of scripts, drama, comedy, and how it's been shown over millennia how these stories are done. It's fascinating and gives you insight. It's written for Hollywood and movies, but it's incredibly useful for what we do as designers, engineers, and technology leaders. There's some aspect in this modern day, like this podcast, what I love is the humans behind the story too. So part of the story is the human beings: humor, drama, heartbreak, hope, emotions. It's not just about painting a beautiful story that's flawless. It's vulnerability, being a dreamer, over-promising and then failing in real life, changing your mind, realizing the whole of it, and embracing the full richness and complexity of the personality of the leader or the different people involved. You can't just present this beautiful, always Pleasantville view of a product. There has to be this humanity that's part of it, the full roller coaster of humanity, which I think has been very difficult for companies to embrace. Maybe it's just an old school way of doing things. People think we present a facade, we generate the story and tell the story. But especially in the technical world, we present the story as 'it's faster, it's smaller, it's longer battery life' — bits and numbers and metrics. That resonates with other geeks. What resonates with the planet is all emotions. If you can bring a great emotional story but with a great rational story at the same time, why you should do this, then you bring that superpower, that joy, and it all hangs together.
turns.zh: 罗伯特·麦基的《故事》是一本关于剧本写作的书，讲述了剧本的原型类型——戏剧、喜剧，以及几千年来这些故事是如何被讲述的。它引人入胜，能给你带来洞察。这本书是为好莱坞和电影写的，但它对我们作为设计师、工程师和技术领导者来说非常有用。在当今这个时代，比如这个播客，我喜欢的是故事背后的人。所以故事的一部分就是人本身：幽默、戏剧、心碎、希望、情感。这不只是描绘一个完美无瑕的美丽故事。它关乎脆弱、做一个梦想家、过度承诺然后在现实中失败、改变主意、意识到全部，以及拥抱领导者或不同参与者个性的全部丰富性和复杂性。你不能只呈现一个美好的、永远像“欢乐谷”一样的产品视图。必须有这种人性作为一部分，人性的完整过山车，我认为公司很难接受这一点。也许这只是老派的做法。人们认为我们呈现一个假象，我们编造故事并讲述它。但在技术世界里，我们讲述故事的方式是“它更快、更小、电池寿命更长”——全是数字和指标。这能引起其他极客的共鸣。但能引起全世界共鸣的是情感。如果你能同时带来一个伟大的情感故事和一个伟大的理性故事，解释为什么应该这样做，那么你就带来了那种超能力、那种喜悦，一切就都融为一体了。

turns.en: And there's personal drama too, the human side. Here's the pain I had, remember that thing. You're obviously this extremely well-known human being behind a lot of great inventions in the technology world, but you're also just a human being. You have a distinct personality that comes through — your eyes light up, the way you communicate is just you. Some people are more stoic, some are like Elon, all over the place, chaos. Steve Jobs, there's a very distinct — it's hard to put into words, I can be poetic — but there's a very distinct personality on stage. That's not just the product, that's something else too. And you have to reveal that a little bit and allow people to reveal that a little bit, just let them be themselves.
turns.zh: 还有个人戏剧的一面，人性的一面。“这是我经历的痛苦，还记得那件事吗”。你显然是科技世界许多伟大发明背后非常知名的人，但你也是一个人。你有鲜明的个性——你的眼睛会发光，你沟通的方式就是你本人。有些人更内敛，有些人像埃隆·马斯克一样，天马行空、混乱。史蒂夫·乔布斯，有一种非常鲜明的——很难用语言形容，我可以诗意一点——但舞台上就有一种非常鲜明的个性。那不仅仅是产品，还有别的东西。你必须稍微展现那一点，也允许别人展现那一点，让他们做自己。

turns.en: Well, look, why do I think your podcast is so amazing? Because you are yourself. You talk about yourself, you bring your emotions into it, and you don't modulate it. You are you. It comes through, it feels right. You dress the way you want to dress, you say 'this is me and this is all of me,' and you become vulnerable. It's much easier to do a podcast like that than run a very large company, where a lot of people would feel the pain if you say something stupid. So it's much easier to be afraid and be careful. But nevertheless, the same applies: authenticity and risk-taking is the only way to be successful in the long term.
turns.zh: 你看，为什么我觉得你的播客如此精彩？因为你就是你自己。你谈论自己，把你的情感带进来，而且你不去调节它。你就是你。这很真实，感觉对了。你穿你想穿的衣服，你说“这就是我，我全部的我”，你变得脆弱。做这样的播客比经营一家大公司容易得多，在大公司里，如果你说错话，很多人会感到痛苦。所以更容易害怕和小心。但尽管如此，同样的道理适用：真实和冒险是长期成功的唯一途径。

turns.en: Let me just linger on the iPod, one of the great designs of all time. What does it take to design a great product? We can look at Nest, iPod, iPhone, many great things you designed. Looking at that one transformational thing, what can you say about what it takes to do a great design, or what makes a great design?
turns.zh: 让我在 iPod 上多停留一会儿，它是有史以来最伟大的设计之一。设计一个伟大的产品需要什么？我们可以看看 Nest、iPod、iPhone，以及你设计的许多伟大产品。看看那个变革性的产品，你能说说做出伟大设计需要什么，或者什么造就了伟大设计吗？

turns.en: Well, we talked about a painkiller and the joy that comes from it. But then there's the behind the scenes: the team, everyone who brings it to life, brings that story to life. If you have a great story and you know the why, then you can communicate it to those people working on it, and then they bring their own thing into it. It becomes emotional for them too. It's not just a job, it's a mission. And so many of the details are born out of these early prototypes, things you still haven't given full form to. It may be 80% done, or even 60% done, but you can see enough in there. Then you take those great ideas and give the whys to the team so they feel it, they can understand it. Then they bring their best and their ideas to the table, and you can select from those. It could be just a pixel change, a slight change in how you do the audio feedback, or maybe a curve on the mechanics of how it feels. Because everybody brings themselves trying to feel this thing. They're not just doing something someone told them to do. If you can instill that mission and that why into that team, it doesn't have to be big. You get a 10x effect. Everyone comes together in a special way, and the magic is created. You put the love into it, and the customer feels the love on the other side.
turns.zh: 我们谈到了止痛药和它带来的喜悦。但还有幕后：团队，每个把它变为现实、把故事变为现实的人。如果你有一个伟大的故事，并且你知道为什么，那么你可以把它传达给那些正在做这件事的人，然后他们把自己的东西带进来。这对他们来说也变得有情感。这不仅仅是一份工作，这是一个使命。很多细节都源于这些早期的原型，那些你还没有完全成型的东西。可能完成了 80%，甚至 60%，但你已经能看到足够多的东西。然后你把这些好主意和“为什么”传达给团队，让他们感受到，让他们理解。然后他们把自己的最佳想法带到桌面上，你可以从中挑选。可能只是一个像素的改变，音频反馈方式的细微调整，或者手感上机械曲线的变化。因为每个人都把自己投入进去，试图感受这个东西。他们不只是做别人让他们做的事。如果你能把那个使命和“为什么”灌输给团队，不一定非得是大事。你会得到 10 倍的效果。每个人都以一种特殊的方式凝聚在一起，魔法就诞生了。你把爱投入进去，客户在另一端也能感受到爱。

turns.en: So making the team take in the vision, onto the why, now they feel all the little details. We think of the original iPod and all the many generations after, all those little details are in them. It's the emotion of the engineers and the designers that it's their baby. It's like a knight's struggle: 'this isn't right.' Like you said, changing little pixels here and there, changing the shape of things, changing the feel of things, the materials, everything on the software part, the packaging, the words on the packaging, the words on the website. Always jumping from the very specific detail problem to the big picture, how the thing feels overall. Always jumping back and forth: what does it look like to the customer? How are we going to implement it in the most efficient way? Because a lot of stuff you don't know, some of that stuff is hacked in, maybe hacked in at the end. It may not be the most beautiful architecture that a geek would look at and go 'oh my god, that's so beautiful.' Some of it could just be hacked in.
turns.zh: 所以让团队接受愿景，接受“为什么”，现在他们感受到了所有的小细节。我们回想最初的 iPod 和之后的许多代，所有那些小细节都在里面。那是工程师和设计师的情感，那是他们的宝贝。就像骑士的挣扎：“这不对。”就像你说的，这里改改像素，那里改改形状，改变手感，改变材料，软件部分的一切，包装，包装上的文字，网站上的文字。总是在非常具体的细节问题和整体画面之间跳跃，整体感觉如何。总是来回跳跃：客户看起来怎么样？我们如何以最高效的方式实现它？因为很多事情你不知道，有些东西是临时拼凑的，也许是在最后拼凑的。它可能不是极客看了会惊叹“哦天哪，太美了”的最优雅的架构。有些可能只是临时拼凑的。

transcript.secZh: 产品设计中的叙事与情感

transcript.sec: Design vs. Engineering

turns.en: Maybe this is a good moment to draw a distinction between design and engineering. Does such a distinction even exist? Are these distinct disciplines or no?
turns.zh: 也许现在是个好时机来区分设计和工程。这种区别真的存在吗？它们是不同的学科，还是不是？

turns.en: I don't think they're just distinct; I think they're different types of design. There's always this idea of the designer on the mount, and it all flows down like some magic. It's not.
