There are electrical designs, AI designers, data scientist designers—everybody has design. There's a chapter in the book all about that. It's not that you go to the mount and come down enlightened. Each person brings their form of design and their craft. If they're really good, they're artists in their own right. They're not just engineers or designers; they're artists, they're empathetic, they really want to bring their best. A lot of the best engineers I've worked with are not the technical ones who get it exactly right; they're the artists. They came from music or other things, and they see that. When you work with very rigid engineers—'this is the only way'—those are not the engineers I want to work with. They're all a bit artists at heart. They understand the practicalness, but they don't have the rigidity of 'this is the way it's done.' If you're building something new and revolutionary, none of us are experts at it. If you come with that expert mindset—'just tell me'—I don't want you on the team. We are all learning about something that has never existed before, and we have to bring vulnerability and openness to new ideas. So you want people with a beginner's mind. Don't come in as an expert.
turns.zh: 我认为它们不仅仅是不同的，而是不同类型的设计。总有一种观念，认为设计师高高在上，一切像魔法一样流下来。其实不是。有电气设计、AI 设计师、数据科学家设计师——每个人都有设计。书里有一整章都在讲这个。并不是你登上山顶，下来就开悟了。每个人都有自己的设计形式和技艺。如果他们真的很优秀，他们本身就是艺术家。他们不只是工程师或设计师，他们是艺术家，有同理心，真正想做到最好。我合作过的很多最优秀的工程师，并不是那些技术上一丝不苟的人，而是艺术家。他们来自音乐或其他领域，他们能看到这一点。当你和非常僵化的工程师合作——‘只有这条路’——那不是我想共事的人。他们内心都有点艺术家气质。他们理解实用性，但没有‘就该这么做’的僵化。如果你在建造全新的、革命性的东西，我们谁都不是专家。如果你带着专家的心态——‘告诉我怎么做就行’——我不希望你加入团队。我们都在学习从未存在过的东西，必须带着脆弱和开放的心态接受新想法。所以你需要有初学者心态的人。不要以专家自居。

turns.en: What's the story?
turns.zh: 有什么故事吗？

turns.en: Okay, so you asked what were the risks on the early iPod. There were a few big ones. One was putting rotating media in your pocket—it could drop at any time, and the heads and hard drive media are so close that if it smacks, it's dead. So we had to design special tests and software for that. But there was another one: in the early days, the first generation iPod had to hack the IDE interface to the hard drives. I was like, okay, we're going to use this chip for hard drive to make a portable hard drive via FireWire. Then we had the MP3 player and user interface. There were times when it was just a hard drive and times when it was an MP3 player, and I had to hot-switch between what the hard drive thought it was talking to. So I designed this thing, tore it apart, and thought, maybe I'm going to screw up IDE. So I went to the expert at Apple who understood IDE. The mass storage specialist came over, and I put on the whiteboard: 'Here's how we're going to do this thing, here's the commands, and this is how it hot-switches.' He said, 'That's never going to work.' I said, 'Well, let me show you this prototype right here—it's been working for days. I just wanted to see if you could find any holes.' He didn't even look and just stormed out of the room.
turns.zh: 好吧，你问早期 iPod 有哪些风险。有几个大的。一个是把旋转介质放在口袋里——它随时可能掉落，磁头和硬盘介质离得太近，一撞就坏了。所以我们不得不设计特殊的测试和软件。但还有另一个：早期第一代 iPod 必须破解硬盘的 IDE 接口。我想，好吧，我们要用这个芯片通过 FireWire 把硬盘做成便携式硬盘。然后我们还有 MP3 播放器和用户界面。有时候它只是硬盘，有时候是 MP3 播放器，我必须在硬盘认为它在和什么通信之间热切换。所以我设计了这东西，拆开它，心想，我可能会搞砸 IDE。于是我去找苹果公司懂 IDE 的专家。那个大容量存储专家来了，我在白板上写：‘我们要这样做，这些是命令，这是热切换的方式。’他说：‘这永远行不通。’我说：‘好吧，让我给你看看这个原型——它已经运行好几天了。我只是想看看你能不能找出漏洞。’他看都没看就冲出了房间。

turns.en: I've had a lot of experience like this with experts. For example, this ridiculous room—I had a person, and many like them, that I showed the situation. Acoustics. They said, 'No, no, no, this is horrible, this is not going to work. The reflection, the curtains are not going to stop the reflex.' There's a bunch of terminology. It's a similar situation to the IDE story. I said, 'I just need to see if there are major issues, low-hanging fruit that are fixable, and major holes I should be aware of.' Not 'that's $100,000 to upgrade.' For what exact purpose? Why?
turns.zh: 我和专家有过很多类似的经历。比如这个荒谬的房间——我找了一个人，还有很多像他一样的，我给他们看了情况。声学问题。他们说：‘不，不，不，这太糟糕了，这行不通。反射，窗帘挡不住反射。’一堆术语。这和 IDE 的故事很像。我说：‘我只是想看看有没有大问题，容易解决的明显问题，以及我需要注意的重大漏洞。’而不是‘升级要花 10 万美元。’具体为了什么目的？为什么？

turns.en: Exactly. The why—focusing on the story and the content, the why. I've experienced that in artistic realms too, like photography, videography, cinematography. I talk to photographers who are experts, and it's always about the equipment—the sensors, the lighting. But what about the feeling of the story you create visually? The difference between a movie that's really well told without all the effects, versus some superhero movies with lots of action and CGI. CGI superhero can tell a better story, but you have to have a good story to begin with.
turns.zh: 没错。就是那个“为什么”——关注故事和内容，关注“为什么”。我在艺术领域也经历过，比如摄影、摄像、电影摄影。我和那些所谓的专家摄影师聊天，他们总是谈论设备——传感器、灯光。但你视觉上创造的故事感觉呢？一部没有特效但讲得很好的电影，和那些有很多动作和 CGI 的超级英雄电影之间的区别。CGI 超级英雄可以讲更好的故事，但首先你得有一个好故事。

turns.en: If you're focused on the story, you need to start with a story. If you bring in experts, they can often be detrimental to the why. They're too good at doing the what.
turns.zh: 如果你专注于故事，你需要从故事开始。如果你请来专家，他们常常会对“为什么”有害。他们太擅长做“是什么”了。

turns.en: You can bring in experts for why. Too many times we get experts for what, and they only focus on the what—the specs, feeds, numbers. But what you're really asking for is somebody about the why, understanding what we're trying to get done, and fitting the whats into that why. That's why one of the qualities I really enjoy in people to work with is humility for a particular problem. When you approach it, basically, 'I don't know how to solve this, but we're going to figure it out,' as opposed to 'Oh, I've solved this many times before.'
turns.zh: 你可以请专家来谈“为什么”。很多时候我们请来的是“是什么”的专家，他们只关注“是什么”——规格、参数、数字。但你真正需要的是关于“为什么”的人，理解我们想要达成什么，然后把“是什么”融入那个“为什么”。这就是为什么我非常喜欢与人合作的一个品质是面对特定问题时的谦逊。当你面对它时，基本上是‘我不知道怎么解决，但我们会想办法’，而不是‘哦，我以前解决过很多次了。’

transcript.sec: Working with Steve Jobs

turns.en: I know exactly what to do. Humility before the chaos, sort of having an open mind that this is going to require a totally new way of doing things. It's a really nice quality to see. You know, you're one of the fascinating humans in the history of Silicon Valley. Steve is another one of those. So those two humans came together for a time to work together. What was it like working with Steve Jobs? What aspect of his behavior and personality, let's say, brought out the best in you?
turns.zh: 我完全知道该怎么做。在混乱面前保持谦逊，保持开放的心态，意识到这需要一种全新的做事方式。这是一个非常好的品质。你知道吗，你是硅谷历史上最迷人的人物之一，史蒂夫也是其中之一。所以这两个人曾一度合作共事。和史蒂夫·乔布斯一起工作是什么感觉？他的行为和个性中，哪一方面激发了你最好的一面？

turns.en: Pushing you, really pushing you. Relentless on the details, challenging you for the right reasons. It wasn't bullying, it wasn't demeaning. He would critique the work, not judge the person, at least not in front of them or inside of a group or anything like that. I know it was really that attention to detail. And when he would make a decision, you know, when you make the first version of anything revolutionary, there are a lot of opinion-based decisions. And there's only one or two people, three people who hold those opinion-based decisions and what they should be. And when you have those opinions and you're trying to work with the team to implement those decisions, you have to really tell the why of those decisions. Just don't go do it, but why it's there so you can feel part of that decision. You can understand what were the trade-offs of the different other answers to that opinion, right? And say this is the reason why we picked what we picked because it's this for the customer or this for the overall story, what have you. So that you felt really good. Because a lot of times, most people want a data-driven decision, but with v1s you don't get data, right? Maybe in a B2B you could a little bit because you can talk to customers, but you can't do that with a consumer property version one. B2B business versus what's the alternative? Business to consumer v1, okay, which is defining centers, yes, yes.
turns.zh: 推动你，真正地推动你。在细节上毫不留情，出于正确的理由挑战你。这不是霸凌，也不是贬低。他会批评工作，而不是评判人，至少不会当着他们的面或在群体面前那样做。我知道那真的是对细节的关注。当他做出决定时，你知道，当你做任何革命性产品的第一个版本时，有很多基于观点的决定。只有一两个人或三个人持有这些基于观点的决定，以及它们应该是什么。当你有这些观点并试图与团队合作实施这些决定时，你必须真正解释这些决定背后的原因。不要只是去做，而是要知道为什么这么做，这样你才能感受到自己是决定的一部分。你可以理解这个观点的不同答案之间的权衡，对吧？然后说这就是我们选择这个的原因，因为这对客户来说是这样，或者对整个故事来说是这样，等等。这样你就会感觉很好。因为很多时候，大多数人都想要数据驱动的决定，但对于第一版，你没有数据，对吧？也许在 B2B 中你可以有一点，因为你可以和客户交谈，但你不能对消费者产品的第一版这样做。B2B 业务与替代方案相比？B2C 第一版，好吧，这就是定义中心，是的，是的。

turns.en: So when you say data-driven decisions versus opinion-based decisions, so like gut, you have to use. You don't have any, you can't fall back on any data or any previous history to kind of inform you of what's going on, right?
turns.zh: 所以当你说数据驱动的决定与基于观点的决定时，就像直觉，你必须使用。你没有任何数据或任何以前的历史可以依赖来告诉你发生了什么，对吧？

turns.en: And so if you look at most companies who are paralyzed and cannot make new innovations and new products, it's because they're trying to turn, and this is what I saw at Philips, they're trying to turn opinion-based decisions into data-driven decisions so they don't lose their jobs. So if you look at management consulting, management consulting is all about taking those opinion-based decisions, giving them to someone else to turn into data that comes back to them and says they can blame the management consultants, yeah, when something goes wrong as opposed to it wasn't me, yeah, right. When you need to have to tell that story, you have to understand that especially v1, you need to be able to articulate those opinion-based decisions and you need to own them. And if you fail with some of them, you didn't get it right, you then own them and fix them and move on, right? Version one of the iPod wasn't perfect, version one of the iPhone wasn't perfect. We got a lot of opinion-based decisions wrong. But as you go through it, because you got more data, because v2 you had data on those original opinions, and then you were able to then modulate off of that, right? And you still have new opinions because those are differentiators that we call differentiators, the things that move the product forward in its evolution. But at the revolution stage, opinions, opinions, opinions, no data. And so you have this discussion, you and Steve and the whole team with opinions. And there you have to be harsh. I wouldn't say harsh, but you have to be very determined, right?
turns.zh: 所以如果你看看那些瘫痪、无法进行新创新和新产品的公司，那是因为他们试图把，这就是我在飞利浦看到的，他们试图把基于观点的决定变成数据驱动的决定，这样他们就不会丢掉工作。所以如果你看看管理咨询，管理咨询就是把这些基于观点的决定交给别人，让他们变成数据，然后回来，这样他们就可以责怪管理顾问，是的，当事情出错时，而不是说“不是我”，是的，对吧。当你需要讲述那个故事时，你必须明白，尤其是第一版，你需要能够清晰地表达那些基于观点的决定，并且你需要拥有它们。如果你在其中一些上失败了，你没有做对，那么你就拥有它们，修复它们，然后继续前进，对吧？iPod 的第一版并不完美，iPhone 的第一版也不完美。我们有很多基于观点的决定做错了。但当你经历这个过程时，因为你获得了更多数据，因为第二版你有了关于那些原始观点的数据，然后你能够在此基础上进行调整，对吧？而且你仍然有新的观点，因为这些是差异化因素，我们称之为差异化因素，是推动产品进化的东西。但在革命阶段，观点，观点，观点，没有数据。所以你们会有这样的讨论，你和史蒂夫以及整个团队都有观点。在那里你必须严厉。我不会说严厉，但你必须非常坚定，对吧？

turns.en: You know, there are two real opinion-based decisions that happened on the iPhone. One was the keyboard: should we have a hardware keyboard or should we have a virtual keyboard? The BlackBerry was the number one productivity messaging device of its time. It was called a CrackBerry for a reason, because people loved it because it had, it was easy to type and they could get their work done. But when you're saying we're going to move from that, everyone's talking about that in the market and you say we're going to move to a virtual keyboard and it's not going to work as well as the hardware keyboard, that's an opinion-based decision, right? Because the data is telling you, yeah, all the best sales are over here. God, that takes guts. But you have to look at it from a different point of view. And this is how I learned to come to understand this, because I had been building virtual keyboards before and I knew the goodness and the badness in them, right? But he was like, look, those are productivity devices. We're making ours born out of an entertainment device and productivity. Right? We need to show full-screen videos. We are going to have apps here, not apps but our apps, the Apple apps, because there were no App Store yet, are going to take over the whole screen. You want a full-screen web browser. You don't want one that's like half of the device is just a keyboard. Maybe you don't need that keyboard in every instance. So we want that part of the screen to change based on the tool you may need at the time. And maybe it's just full view, right? So you have to go and understand it's a different type of device. Just because that's that and it's successful for that reason, the CrackBerry for the keyboard, that's not the only thing you're going to do with this device. Because people only did messaging and maybe a few phone calls, right? This was going to be so much more. It was going to be an entertainment web browsing device. So you wanted those tools to go away. But it wouldn't be as good as the hardware keyboard. So that's an opinion. But let me give you another opinion-based decision that got turned around before it shipped. Steve said, "No SIM slot. I don't want any slots. We're going to make it very pure." Johnny was like, "Of course, no slots, Johnny." And we all looked around and go, "That doesn't work. You can't do that." "Well, why?" And then he would always, and this was the magic of Steve, like when you said no that doesn't work, you go, "Well, why does Verizon not have any SIM slots?" Right? They showed that you can do a mobile phone with those limits. You're like, "Okay, here we go." And so a few days later we come back with, and so product marketing, voice of the customer, engineering, we all come back with all the data showing how many data networks and mobile networks required SIM cards versus did not and what the trends were. And we showed the data and that killed the, or excuse me, brought back the SIM slot on the original iPhone. Because we're like, because he was just like, "We're going to tell AT&T to not use a SIM, yeah right? We're going to just tell them to do it differently." Right? But we were like, "If we want this thing to go anywhere..."
turns.zh: 你知道，iPhone 上有两个真正的基于观点的决定。一个是键盘：我们应该用硬件键盘还是虚拟键盘？黑莓是当时排名第一的生产力消息设备。它被称为“CrackBerry”是有原因的，因为人们喜欢它，因为它打字方便，他们可以完成工作。但是当你说我们要从那个转变时，市场上每个人都在谈论那个，你说我们要转向虚拟键盘，而且它不会像硬件键盘那样好用，这是一个基于观点的决定，对吧？因为数据告诉你，是的，所有最好的销售都在这里。天哪，这需要勇气。但你必须从不同的角度来看待它。这就是我学会理解这一点的方式，因为我以前构建过虚拟键盘，我知道它们的好坏，对吧？但他说，看，那些是生产力设备。我们正在制造我们的设备，它源于娱乐设备和生产力。对吧？我们需要显示全屏视频。我们将在这里有应用，不是应用，而是我们的应用，苹果的应用，因为当时还没有 App Store，它们将占据整个屏幕。你想要一个全屏的网页浏览器。你不想要一个设备的一半都是键盘。也许你不需要在每个实例中都使用那个键盘。所以我们希望屏幕的那部分根据你当时可能需要的工具而改变。也许它只是全屏视图，对吧？所以你必须去理解这是一种不同类型的设备。仅仅因为那个是那个，并且它因为那个原因而成功，黑莓的键盘，那不是你将用这个设备做的唯一事情。因为人们只做消息和也许几个电话，对吧？这将是更多的东西。它将是一个娱乐网页浏览设备。所以你希望那些工具消失。但它不会像硬件键盘那样好。所以那是一个观点。但让我给你另一个在发货前被推翻的基于观点的决定。史蒂夫说：“没有 SIM 卡槽。我不想要任何插槽。我们要让它非常纯粹。”乔尼说：“当然，没有插槽，乔尼。”我们都环顾四周说：“那行不通。你不能那样做。”“嗯，为什么？”然后他总是，这就是史蒂夫的魔力，当你说不，那行不通时，你会说：“嗯，为什么 Verizon 没有任何 SIM 卡槽？”对吧？他们展示了你可以用那些限制做一部手机。你就像：“好吧，我们开始吧。”所以几天后我们回来，产品营销、客户之声、工程，我们都带着所有数据回来，显示有多少数据网络和移动网络需要 SIM 卡，有多少不需要，以及趋势是什么。我们展示了数据，那杀死了，或者抱歉，带回了原始 iPhone 上的 SIM 卡槽。因为我们就像，因为他就像：“我们要告诉 AT&T 不要使用 SIM 卡，对吧？我们只是要告诉他们以不同的方式做。”对吧？但我们就像：“如果我们想让这个东西走向任何地方……”

transcript.sec: Opinion vs Data in Decision Making

turns.en: Around the world you want to put that friction in. People are going to move from place to place, they have different SIMs because of the prices and all that stuff. We had to show all of that data, and then that opinion-based decision got turned into a data-driven decision, and the SIM slot obviously showed up. So those are two very... at the very same time, yeah? Right, opinion can hold, and so can data. Data can overrule opinion when data does exist. For a V1, but at the end of the day, you don't know what the right answer is. So doing no SIM card slot may have been the right decision; we won't know because maybe if that was the decision, then like many times throughout Apple's history, you basically changed the tide of how technology is done.
turns.zh: 在全球范围内，你想把这种摩擦加进去。人们会从一个地方搬到另一个地方，因为价格等原因，他们会有不同的 SIM 卡。我们必须展示所有这些数据，然后那个基于意见的决定变成了基于数据的决定，SIM 卡槽显然就出现了。所以这两者同时存在，对吧？意见可以成立，数据也可以成立。当数据存在时，数据可以压倒意见。对于第一版来说，但归根结底，你不知道正确答案是什么。所以取消 SIM 卡槽可能是正确的决定；我们不会知道，因为如果那是决定，那么就像苹果历史上多次发生的那样，你基本上改变了技术发展的潮流。

turns.en: Yeah, absolutely. You know, you never know. Apple started Wi-Fi; people don't understand. Wi-Fi came out of there. There was no Wi-Fi in 2001. Apple started Wi-Fi, and then everyone else got on board. If you look at where we're going now, we're going to phones without SIM slots because we have eSIMs, right? And now the SIM slot is becoming legacy. That legacy port will probably be gone by six, maybe ten years. It'll be gone, I'm pretty sure of that, because it's so much easier for carriers; they don't have to have physical things to go out and... right. So right now it's just the early days, but it will happen, and it will go its way. It'll fall away, but it will take time. You just couldn't do it back then. So timing is essential here. But at the end of the day, it's opinions, and that's where the genius is. Sometimes the data tells you one thing, but the data at the end of the day does represent the past. Exactly. And the future may be different than the past, right? Sometimes there's wisdom in the past, and sometimes it's actually representative of something that should be overcome. And progress looks like leaving that stuff behind.
turns.zh: 是的，绝对如此。你知道，你永远不知道。苹果开创了 Wi-Fi；人们不明白。Wi-Fi 就是从那里来的。2001 年还没有 Wi-Fi。苹果开创了 Wi-Fi，然后其他所有人都跟进了。如果你看看我们现在的发展方向，我们正在走向没有 SIM 卡槽的手机，因为我们有 eSIM，对吧？现在 SIM 卡槽正在成为遗留物。那个遗留端口大概会在六到十年内消失。它肯定会消失，我很确定，因为这对运营商来说容易得多；他们不需要有实体东西去分发……对吧。所以现在只是早期阶段，但它会发生，它会走自己的路。它会逐渐消失，但这需要时间。你当时就是做不到。所以时机在这里至关重要。但归根结底，这是意见，而这就是天才所在。有时数据告诉你一件事，但数据归根结底代表的是过去。没错。而未来可能与过去不同，对吧？有时过去有智慧，有时它实际上代表了一些应该被克服的东西。进步看起来就是把那些东西抛在脑后。

transcript.sec: The Headphone Jack and AirPods

turns.en: Like the headphone jack, right? I mean, when different folks were getting rid of the headphone jack, boy, I would love to be a fly on the wall of those discussions. We had that. Oh, that was a discussion that happened almost every year.
turns.zh: 就像耳机插孔，对吧？我的意思是，当不同的人都在取消耳机插孔时，我真想偷听那些讨论。我们有过那种讨论。哦，那几乎是每年都会发生的讨论。

turns.en: Yeah, that was an every year: 'Should we get rid of headphone jacks on the iPod?' Yeah, right. 'When are wireless headsets going to happen?' What? Right. And it took years to build all the right protocols, the chips, all those things to make the experience that is the AirPods today. To have the confidence, because Bluetooth was good, but it wasn't Apple-like. So it's like we got to make our own chips, we got to make our own software stacks. Now we have the confidence to remove the headphone jack and actually make you pay $200 more for your iPhone that you were just paying because it's a headphone jack. Now we've grown our revenue, we've given a new experience to the user, right? And ta-da, you know, it's just magic. And now the world's transformed to everyone moving to that, right? But it took years to understand the problem, develop the technology, and not just rush it to market to get a half experience, but to get it right and refine it, then ship it. And only then, after it was probably four or five years in development, just like the M1 processor, right? That was a work from 2008, right? Grinding away, grinding away, grinding away, then saying, 'Okay, now we have the confidence. We're doing our own silicon for all the iPhones and iPods and iPads and such. Now we're going to turn to the Mac and make sure we have the best processor.' Right? Not just that we have the best integrated design team, and then saying we're going to... you know, and then besting everyone, making sure the software and the hardware are designed at the same time, making sure the kernels, all those things, are going to use the best efficiency, and then popping it out, and then it feels seamless. It's magic. There were no... as far as I could tell, unless you were in real esoteric drivers or something like that, it just worked. It was magic. Like the transition was not even a speed bump, it was not even a crack in the road.
turns.zh: 是的，那是每年都会讨论的问题：“我们该取消 iPod 上的耳机插孔吗？” 是的，没错。“无线耳机什么时候会出现？” 什么？没错。而花了多年时间才构建出所有正确的协议、芯片以及所有那些东西，才造就了今天的 AirPods 体验。要有信心，因为蓝牙不错，但它不像苹果的风格。所以我们得制造自己的芯片，得打造自己的软件栈。现在我们有了取消耳机插孔的信心，并且实际上让你为你的 iPhone 多付 200 美元，而之前你付的钱只是因为有个耳机插孔。现在我们增加了收入，给了用户新的体验，对吧？然后，你知道，这就像魔法一样。现在世界已经转变，每个人都转向了那个，对吧？但这花了多年时间理解问题、开发技术，而不是匆忙推向市场得到一个半成品体验，而是把它做对、完善，然后发货。而且只有在那样之后，大概经过了四五年的开发，就像 M1 处理器一样，对吧？那是从 2008 年开始的工作，对吧？不断打磨、打磨、打磨，然后说：“好了，现在我们有了信心。我们正在为所有 iPhone、iPod、iPad 等制造自己的芯片。现在我们要转向 Mac，确保我们拥有最好的处理器。” 对吧？不仅仅是我们拥有最好的集成设计团队，然后说我们要……你知道，然后超越所有人，确保软件和硬件同时设计，确保内核以及所有那些东西都能达到最高效率，然后把它推出来，然后感觉天衣无缝。这就是魔法。据我所知，除非你是在真正深奥的驱动程序之类的东西里，否则它就能正常工作。这就是魔法。就像过渡连个减速带都没有，连路上的裂缝都没有。

transcript.sec: Steve Jobs: Constructive vs Destructive

turns.en: Perhaps famously, Steve had a bit of a temper. Steve Jobs, would you say his particular personality in this aspect was constructive or destructive in the process of shaping these opinion-based ideas?
turns.zh: 也许众所周知，史蒂夫有点脾气。史蒂夫·乔布斯，你觉得他在这方面的人格在塑造这些基于意见的想法过程中是建设性的还是破坏性的？

turns.en: In Build, I write a chapter called 'Yes, And.' You lay out beautifully the types of... and maybe you could speak to the constructive and the destructive types of 'okay.' So there's really two delineations that I've found, real fundamental ones. And that is again the 'why.' Why do I feel this person is an... okay, they might not be. I feel this is a person who's... are they motivated by their ego, or are they motivated by their mission, something they're trying to do that's in service of something else? Right. Sometimes those lines can be blurry, but it's usually pretty clear when it's ego motivated. It's clear they're just trying to get up in the ranks, push people down, shove people aside. I think we saw a president do that on a stage once, you know, 'I'm me and I'm the... you know, I'm the guy,' right? And I'm gonna prove it by pushing everyone away and being nefarious or what have you, either passive-aggressive or aggressively aggressive. But they're doing it about themselves. There's another one, which is someone who's so attentive to detail and unrelenting that they're trying to get the right things for the customer or in service of their mission, and they want to make sure we fulfill those things, right? And they really care. They don't micromanage all the details, but they micromanage the details where the customer touches the customer in some way. People who work with those types of people who are unrelenting and push you and might make you upset, a lot of times it's a knee-jerk reaction to go, 'They are... get off my back.' You're right, and you're protecting your ego because what's happening is that person is usually pushing you beyond your boundaries. They see something that we can do or you can do that you're just either not wanting to do for whatever reason, you're not confident in that, you're like, 'I don't want to take the extra time,' and saying, 'No, we need to get that done,' and pushing you. Okay. And so when we came to those areas, it wasn't just a one-on-one. It could be Steve against the team, going, 'We need glass instead of plastic on the front face of the iPhone, and we're going to do this.' And we're like, 'God, you know...' And so we did it, and he pushed us because he didn't know all the details, but he could see in our minds that we're like, 'Yeah, we could probably... yeah, we could probably, but man, it's really putting us at risk.' And we laid out the risks for him, and he's like, 'I'm willing to take those risks. We'll do that.' We're like, 'We might be three months late.' He's like, 'This is so important, we need to stay on time.' You know? But it would be all the time push, push, push. It reminds me of like kids growing up and me as growing up, you know, when your parents...
turns.zh: 在《Build》一书中，我写了一章叫“是的，而且”。你很好地阐述了……也许你可以谈谈建设性和破坏性的“好吧”。所以我发现实际上有两种划分，非常根本的划分。那就是再次回到“为什么”。为什么我觉得这个人是一个……好吧，他们可能不是。我觉得这个人……他们是受自我驱动，还是受使命驱动，他们试图做的是为了服务于其他事情？对吧。有时这些界限可能模糊，但通常很清楚是自我驱动。很明显他们只是想往上爬，把别人推下去，把别人推到一边。我想我们曾经看到一位总统在舞台上这样做过，你知道，“我就是我，我就是……你知道，我就是那个人，”对吧？我要通过推开所有人、做坏事或别的什么来证明这一点，要么是消极攻击，要么是积极攻击。但他们是为了自己。还有另一种人，他们对细节如此关注且毫不松懈，他们试图为客户或为服务他们的使命做正确的事情，他们想确保我们完成这些事情，对吧？他们真的很在乎。他们不会微观管理所有细节，但他们微观管理那些客户以某种方式接触到的细节。与那些毫不松懈、推动你、可能让你沮丧的人一起工作的人，很多时候会条件反射地说：“他们……别烦我。” 你是对的，你在保护你的自我，因为发生的事情是那个人通常把你推到了你的边界之外。他们看到了我们可以做或者你可以做的事情，而你出于某种原因不想做，你对此没有信心，你就像，“我不想多花时间，”而他们说，“不，我们需要完成这个，”并推动你。好吧。所以当我们遇到这些领域时，这不仅仅是单对单。可能是史蒂夫对抗整个团队，说：“我们需要在 iPhone 正面用玻璃而不是塑料，我们要这样做。” 我们就像，“天哪，你知道……” 然后我们做了，他推动我们，因为他不知道所有细节，但他能看出我们心里在想，“是的，我们可能……是的，我们可能可以，但是，伙计，这真的让我们面临风险。” 我们向他列出了风险，他说：“我愿意承担这些风险。我们就这么做。” 我们说：“我们可能会晚三个月。” 他说：“这太重要了，我们必须按时完成。” 你知道？但一直都是推、推、推。这让我想起孩子成长和我成长的过程，你知道，当你的父母……

transcript.secZh: 乔布斯：建设性与破坏性

transcript.sec: Pushing people to grow vs. bullying

turns.en: Push you to make you grow beyond your boundaries, your personal boundaries, and you're like, 'God damn it, I'm sad,' you know. But they do it for the right reasons. Now let's see, it's not bullying, it's not about bullying, it's not about demeaning. It's about either pushing you to another part of the mission that needs to get done, or it's about critiquing your work but not judging you.
turns.zh: 他们推你，让你超越自己的边界、个人边界，然后你会想，‘该死的，我很难过’，你知道的。但他们这么做是出于正确的理由。现在我们要明白，这不是霸凌，不是关于霸凌，也不是贬低。要么是把你推到任务中需要完成的另一部分，要么是批评你的工作，而不是评判你这个人。

turns.en: Yes, well, there's a lot to say there. So one, uh, it's fascinating, it's really is fascinating, and you laid out a very nice picture. But it does feel like there's sometimes gray areas, which is why it makes all this very complicated. So one question I have for you in terms of glass on the iPhone: how important is it that Steve is right? Because I could argue each side. It seems like in one sense, just having a strong vision and opinion is already going to make everybody grow, even if it turns out to be wrong. As long as you are sort of standing your ground, you know, Napoleon invading Russia in the winter — it's just not gonna be good, right? This is not a good idea, but I'm gonna hold, do that, and then once you decide, you go all in. And then from that, even if the whole team knows it's the wrong decision, just sticking by it, powering through, you will learn through the pain of it. Like everybody will learn. So that's one side. The other is maybe the vision-driven gets to be more and more of a thing. If they have a track record of through that process having built people up, having made the correct decisions, they can't — they're not allowed to be an — they're in rare air and no one can challenge them, right?
turns.zh: 是的，这方面有很多可说的。首先，这很迷人，真的很迷人，而且你描绘了一幅很好的画面。但确实感觉有时存在灰色地带，这就是为什么这一切变得非常复杂。所以关于 iPhone 的玻璃，我有一个问题：Steve 正确有多重要？因为我可以从两边论证。从某种意义上说，仅仅拥有强烈的愿景和观点就已经能让每个人成长，即使最终证明是错的。只要你坚持自己的立场，你知道，拿破仑冬天入侵俄罗斯——那不会有好结果，对吧？这不是个好主意，但我坚持这么做，一旦你决定了，就全力以赴。然后即使整个团队都知道这是错误的决定，只要坚持下去，挺过去，你会从痛苦中学习。每个人都会学习。所以这是一方面。另一方面，可能是愿景驱动变得越来越重要。如果他们有通过这个过程建立团队、做出正确决策的记录，他们就不能——他们不被允许成为——他们处于罕见的高度，没人能挑战他们，对吧？

turns.en: Steve was never that. That's the great thing. He was never unchallengeable. You could challenge him. Now, the plastic to glass story is a perfect example of this. So at the beginning of the project, well before we were going, we had always had these things about, you know, plastic front iPods, these kinds of things, these scratches and all that stuff. So we said, 'Are we going to have a glass or a plastic display cover for the display?' Because the display was glass underneath it. We argued back and forth about glass versus plastic, and then we all landed together on plastic. Okay, the original decision was plastic. And the reasons were: okay, we don't want to make a mistake. Glass can break, you know, people drop them all the time. So we don't want to have a fragile device because you're going to be using it even more than a music player, right? And you're going to be holding it, putting it in your pocket, and all that stuff. So we went down the road with plastic. When the product was shown at Macworld in 2007, the first time, that was plastic. We had just enough of them in the field at the time we started to start seeing light scratches on the plastic. Reviewers who didn't have the device yet — because it was behind glass — if you remember 2007, the Jesus phone comes up and no one could even touch them. You could just look at it in this beautiful museum-quality box, like it came from the future or whatever, the past. It was like, 'Oh,' and you just looked, and that was all you got. But then people said, 'Well, what screen is that? What covers on that?' You know, reviewers who knew better, you know, and they're like, 'Oh, it's plastic,' and they were like, 'Really?' And so there was enough of a doubt there. And then when we started to do it, and then Steve changed the frame of reference of the question, or of the result of what the customer would think. And he was like, 'If we designed it with plastic and it's in their pocket all the time and it gets scratched by coins, lightly scratched, or by keys or something like that, that is a design problem we need to fix. That was our bad. If they go off and drop it, or even slightly drop it, and it cracks, it's the customer's fault. And they have much lower — they have less likelihood to complain. Yes, they'll complain, but they're part of that failure.'
turns.zh: Steve 从来不是那样。那是很棒的一点。他从来不是不可挑战的。你可以挑战他。现在，从塑料到玻璃的故事就是一个完美的例子。在项目初期，远在我们开始之前，我们一直有这些关于塑料前面板的 iPod 之类的东西，这些划痕等等。所以我们说，‘我们是要用玻璃还是塑料作为显示屏的盖板？’因为显示屏下面是玻璃。我们反复争论玻璃和塑料，然后最终一致决定用塑料。好吧，最初的决定是塑料。原因是：我们不想犯错。玻璃会碎，你知道，人们总是摔东西。所以我们不想有一个易碎的设备，因为你会比音乐播放器更频繁地使用它，对吧？你会拿着它，放进口袋，等等。所以我们选择了塑料。当产品在 2007 年 Macworld 上首次展示时，那就是塑料的。当时我们刚好有足够多的设备在市场上，开始看到塑料上有轻微划痕。那些还没有拿到设备的评测人员——因为它被放在玻璃后面——如果你记得 2007 年，那款‘耶稣手机’出现了，没人能碰它。你只能在一个漂亮的博物馆品质的盒子里看它，就像它来自未来或过去。就像，‘哦’，你只能看着，仅此而已。但后来人们说，‘那是什么屏幕？上面覆盖着什么？’你知道，那些懂行的评测人员，他们说，‘哦，是塑料的’，然后他们就说，‘真的吗？’所以那里有足够的疑虑。然后当我们开始做的时候，Steve 改变了问题的参照系，或者说改变了客户会怎么想的结果。他说，‘如果我们用塑料设计，它一直在他们口袋里，被硬币轻微划伤，或被钥匙之类的东西划伤，那是我们需要解决的设计问题。那是我们的错。如果他们摔了它，甚至轻轻摔了一下，它裂了，那是客户的错。他们抱怨的可能性要低得多。是的，他们会抱怨，但他们是失败的一部分。’

turns.en: Oh, that's fascinating. And then there's truth to that, right? Because then they were part of why it failed, whereas the design — they didn't do anything wrong, it was just sitting in their pocket and it's, you know, it's scratching, and that's normal use. Abnormal use has been dropped. And we're like, 'Oh, now we get it.' And so we all moved to that mindset. When you framed the problem and the solution in that way versus the original framing where we all landed on plastic. So then he was unrelenting on that, but we all had moved and we have moved mindset and we understood the why and we marshaled together. And then by the end of June, and it was crazy, the mechanical product design teams, sourcing, all of us, all the partner Corning pulled together to make that happen because it was the right reason, right?
turns.zh: 哦，这太迷人了。而且这确实有道理，对吧？因为那样他们就成了失败的一部分，而设计——他们没有做错任何事，它只是放在口袋里，你知道，它被划伤了，那是正常使用。异常使用是摔落。然后我们想，‘哦，现在我们明白了。’所以我们都转向了那种心态。当你以那种方式框定问题和解决方案，而不是我们最初都同意的塑料方案时。然后他对此毫不妥协，但我们都转变了，我们改变了心态，我们理解了为什么，我们团结在一起。然后到六月底，那真是疯狂，机械产品设计团队、采购、我们所有人，还有合作伙伴康宁，都齐心协力实现了它，因为那是正确的理由，对吧？

turns.en: So you look at these stories and you hear just the top-line rumors of the takeaways, but that's not usually how it all happened. Like one leader was — that's not how Steve was. Now I've seen leaders who were just pounding, you know, and just had no real empathy for the team and understanding the why, and it's just, 'It is the way I want it, right? I am the supreme leader.' That wasn't like that. He just had a very strong opinion, a very strong opinion, but it was challengeable. It was challengeable. And if you came with the right thing, you know, you could modulate that. But you had to come with a team. It couldn't just be you, and you had to become a team and data and to overcome because it was a very strong opinion. And there's personal quirks of character, like you said, good days, bad days, and so on. So there's also the three options you said — you notice that the third option is always going to be the one that's picked. Sure, those kinds of idiosyncrasies. And that brings up another thing. Okay, you said 'challenge the idea, not the person.' You know, I'm somebody who has a temper, I use colorful language and so on when I'm on teams. I work in my private life, I'm much calmer and so on. But I get — when I get really passionate with engineering teams, and I've been called — I mean, I am distinctly aware that you cross lines often. There's like levels, right?
turns.zh: 所以你看这些故事，你听到的只是高层传闻的要点，但通常事情不是那样发生的。比如一个领导者——那不是 Steve 的方式。我见过一些领导者，他们只是强压，你知道，对团队没有真正的同理心，也不理解为什么，只是‘这就是我想要的，对吧？我是最高领袖。’那不是那样的。他只是有非常强烈的意见，非常强烈的意见，但它是可挑战的。它是可挑战的。如果你带着正确的东西来，你知道，你可以调整它。但你必须带着团队来。不能只是你一个人，你必须成为一个团队，带着数据，去克服，因为那是一个非常强烈的意见。而且还有性格上的个人怪癖，就像你说的，好日子、坏日子等等。所以还有你说的三个选项——你注意到第三个选项总是会被选中的。当然，那些特质。这又引出了另一件事。好吧，你说‘挑战想法，而不是人。’你知道，我是一个有脾气的人，我在团队里会用一些粗俗的语言等等。我在私人生活中更冷静等等。但当我真的对工程团队充满热情时，我被叫过——我的意思是，我清楚地意识到你经常越界。有层次，对吧？

turns.en: Sure. You know, you could say a lot of stuff to me. You could swear, you could say stuff that sounds personal, like, 'Lex, sometimes I think you're the dumbest human on the face of the earth,' or something. I don't know, this sounds very personal, right? But I'm not going to take that personally. I understand what's being said. And I've also noticed that there are other people that take stuff more personally. This has to do with teams and figuring out like, okay, who's going to take certain words personally or not. And you have to know that — that's what makes a great coach, a great leader, a mentor. You have to factor all that in. But there's something about just being an...
turns.zh: 当然。你知道，你可以对我说很多话。你可以骂人，你可以说一些听起来很个人化的话，比如‘Lex，有时候我觉得你是地球上最笨的人’之类的。我不知道，这听起来很个人化，对吧？但我不会把它当成针对个人的。我理解说的是什么。我也注意到其他人会更个人化地看待事情。这关系到团队，要弄清楚，好吧，谁会个人化地看待某些词语，谁不会。你必须知道这一点——这就是造就伟大教练、伟大领导者、导师的原因。你必须把所有这些都考虑进去。但只是作为一个……

transcript.sec: Passion and Crossing Lines

turns.en: Being passionate and really driven, that sometimes you do cross lines. And that's... I don't know what to do with that because it feels like it comes with the territory. It seems like you can't just have a perfectly optimized...
turns.zh: 充满激情和干劲，有时确实会越界。我不知道该怎么处理，因为这似乎是工作的一部分。似乎你不可能拥有一个完美优化的……

turns.en: No, no, absolutely not. We're humans. We don't have a program where everyone's programmed the same way to react the same way to a given stimulus.
turns.zh: 不，不，绝对不是。我们是人类。我们没有一个程序让每个人都以同样的方式对给定的刺激做出同样的反应。

turns.en: You said, 'Oh, you're the dumbest human on earth' or whatever. I would never say that, absolutely never. And if someone said that to me, or I saw someone else say that to another person on the team, absolutely not. That is not allowed, because that's judging someone. You may be heated, and you can get heated, and you can say it in your intonation, but to then try to put a label on it and put a label on a person, that is not allowed. So if you let that kind of culture happen, and it becomes somewhat... you know, sometimes it's in jest, it has to be very much ingested, and those two people have to have a really good working relationship. But other than that, I'm sorry, it's going to be a lot more... you could say septic in that way, that you're not going to add that stuff in. But you can do it with all other types of ways without saying that, because then people who do react to that kind of language and don't have those shields, because they might not have that same confidence level that you do, and you can just brush it off, you can... that can be very cancerous in a team, because people then meme that and then they see, 'Oh, that's the right way to be.' You got to snuff that out, and you got to be that change or that model that you want to show the team.
turns.zh: 你说过“你是地球上最笨的人”之类的话。我绝不会那么说，绝对不。如果有人对我说，或者我看到别人对团队里的另一个人说，绝对不行。那是不允许的，因为那是在评判一个人。你可能会情绪激动，你可以情绪激动，你可以用语调表达，但试图贴标签、给人贴标签，那是不允许的。所以如果你让这种文化发生，它就会变得……你知道，有时是开玩笑，但必须非常谨慎，而且那两个人必须有非常好的工作关系。但除此之外，抱歉，那会变得……你可以说是有毒的，你不会加入那些东西。但你可以用其他各种方式来表达，而不说那些话，因为那些对这类语言有反应、没有防护的人，他们可能没有你那样的自信水平，你可以一笑置之，但这对团队来说可能是非常有害的，因为人们会模仿，然后他们会觉得“哦，那就是正确的做法”。你必须扼杀它，你必须成为你想展示给团队的那种改变或榜样。

turns.en: Yes, it's true. Even if it doesn't affect me, it's going to affect a significant enough fraction of brilliant people where that shouldn't be part of the culture. Exactly. And other people see that happen and then think, 'Oh, I guess that's acceptable.' Just like politics in the workplace, is that acceptable or not? I call it out exactly when I see it, in front of everyone, because it's just another ego-driven thing. You have to set the tone as a leader for what you want your organization to be and how it gets reflected in the world, and you have to uphold that. You can have an excursion outside of that, but you have to go back and say, 'I'm sorry.' You have to apologize, heal, and say, 'I was not the person I wanted to be that day. I'm really sorry.' Even in front of the team, have that humility and say, 'We're all human here, and just because I'm the leader doesn't mean I don't make mistakes.' So have the self-awareness, apologize. That's also part of the culture.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。即使它不影响我，它也会影响到足够多的优秀人才，而这不应该成为文化的一部分。没错。其他人看到这种情况发生，就会想“哦，我想这是可以接受的”。就像职场政治，是可以接受还是不可以？我只要看到就会指出来，当着所有人的面，因为那只是另一种自我驱动的事情。作为领导者，你必须为你的组织定下基调，以及它如何反映在世界上，你必须坚持这一点。你可以有偏离的时候，但你必须回头说“对不起”。你必须道歉、修复，并说“那天我没有成为我想成为的人。我真的很抱歉。”即使在团队面前，也要有那种谦逊，说“我们都是人，仅仅因为我是领导者并不意味着我不会犯错”。所以要有自我意识，道歉。这也是文化的一部分。

transcript.sec: Differences from Steve Jobs as a Leader and Designer

turns.en: How are you different from Steve as a leader and designer? You've spoken about what made you stronger, which is he was able to challenge, he was able to push you to bring out the best.
turns.zh: 作为领导者和设计师，你和史蒂夫有什么不同？你谈到过是什么让你变得更强，那就是他能够挑战你，能够推动你发挥出最佳水平。

turns.en: Well, I come from the technical angle, right? Deep technology, software, hardware, systems thinking, implementation, all that stuff. So I have a different bent. He wanted to be an engineer, started, but really he was much better at all the other things: the storytelling, the interfacing, and being the voice of the customer, and being that product marketer in a way, right? That we talked about. I grew into being the product marketing and marketing; he came really from the other way, right? And never got really deep technically. So that's two different mindsets. No one is better or worse, it's just how it is. And it takes all kinds to do great designs.
turns.zh: 嗯，我是从技术角度出发的，对吧？深层技术、软件、硬件、系统思维、实现，所有这些。所以我有不同的倾向。他想成为工程师，开始过，但实际上他在其他方面更擅长：讲故事、与人打交道、成为客户的声音，以及在某种程度上成为产品营销者，对吧？我们谈过这个。我逐渐成长为产品营销和市场营销；他则是从另一个方向来的，对吧？而且从未真正深入技术。所以这是两种不同的思维方式。没有谁更好或更差，就是这样。而且需要各种类型的人才能做出伟大的设计。

turns.en: Did it manifest itself differently, just the fact that you came from those different places?
turns.zh: 它是否以不同的方式表现出来，仅仅是因为你们来自不同的背景？

turns.en: Absolutely. Like the discussion about glass on the iPhone probably had a different flavor to it. When you started getting into the technical details enough, you're getting the third-order technical details, and he can't argue with that anymore. He's like, 'Okay, at some point, I can't win this war.' And he learned that very early on, because he didn't like the way the look of the Macintosh board, the PCB, was laid out. He wanted it to be beautiful on the outside and on the inside. He's like, 'Why are all these wires running this way? Why doesn't it have all this symmetry? We have to make it beautiful on the inside.' And even the traces on the boards have to look a certain way. So the teams made the board that they knew would work, and then they made the board the way Steve wanted it, and that didn't work. And then Steve instantly figured out, 'At some point, don't micromanage every single detail. There are some things he doesn't know enough about.' So he would get out of that. But that was one of those instances where he pushed really hard, and that's his opinion. So they said, 'Okay, we're going to make it a data-driven decision. We're going to make both, and I'm going to show you the results.' And from there, he didn't get into those details. So from that, you could have a great challenge, because then you could get those data and say, 'We can't do that, and let me show you why,' or 'We can do that,' and then Steve would go, 'You can't do that,' and you're like, 'Oh, we can do that, let me show you.' So there were certain times when you were bringing something to reality that he didn't think could exist. So it was that creative tension, that interaction, that was so successful.
turns.zh: 当然。比如关于 iPhone 玻璃的讨论，可能就有不同的味道。当你开始深入技术细节，达到第三阶技术细节时，他再也无法争辩了。他会想，“好吧，在某个点上，我赢不了这场战争。”他很早就学到了这一点，因为他不喜欢 Macintosh 主板（PCB）的布局方式。他希望内外都美观。他说，“为什么所有这些线都这样走？为什么没有对称性？我们必须让内部也美观。”甚至电路板上的走线也必须看起来有某种方式。所以团队做了他们知道能用的板子，然后做了史蒂夫想要的那种板子，但那个不工作。然后史蒂夫立刻明白了，“在某个点上，不要微观管理每一个细节。有些事情他了解得不够。”所以他就不再插手了。但那是他非常坚持的一个例子，那是他的意见。所以他们说，“好吧，我们做一个数据驱动的决定。我们两个都做，我给你看结果。”从那以后，他就不再介入那些细节了。所以从那里，你可以有一个很好的挑战，因为你可以得到那些数据，然后说，“我们不能那样做，让我告诉你为什么，”或者“我们可以那样做，”然后史蒂夫会说，“你不能那样做，”而你会说，“哦，我们可以，让我演示给你看。”所以有些时候，你把一些他认为不可能存在的东西变成了现实。所以正是那种创造性的紧张和互动，才如此成功。

turns.en: But I think there was one other fundamental thing that was different, and it grated on the team, and I made sure, and I learned from, to not do. And I overdo it now in the opposite direction. Which is: when there's a great idea that comes from the team, acknowledge that person and say, 'That is a great idea.' As the leader, you know, the opinion-driven, 'That's a great idea, let's build on that, let's see if we can do that,' or 'It's a great idea but not for now, put it aside.' But call out when people have great ideas, because it's infectious. And that means not ideas that bubble up to the customer level, but inside the organization, people like they get rewarded for their ideas. Steve was always like: you give an idea, and he would go, 'Hmm, okay, I don't know.' The next day, 24 hours later, it would come back with slight modifications, 'I've had this genius idea.' And sooner or later, we'd look around the table and roll our eyes and go, 'Here we go again.' So it demotivates you from generating ideas a little bit. We got used to it, but later on in the team, it just didn't bring the best. Because if the reaction is never 'That's a genius idea,' it was always either negative or neutral, then it doesn't have that same emotional effect that you want to bring your best.
turns.zh: 但我认为还有另一个根本性的不同，它让团队感到不快，我确保并从中学习，避免这样做。现在我可能做得有点过头了，走向了相反的方向。那就是：当团队提出一个好主意时，要认可那个人，并说“这是个好主意”。作为领导者，你知道，以意见为导向，“这是个好主意，我们在此基础上发展，看看能不能做到”，或者“这是个好主意，但不是现在，先放一放”。但当人们有好主意时，要指出来，因为这具有感染力。而且这不仅仅是上升到客户层面的想法，而是在组织内部，人们会因为他们的想法而得到奖励。史蒂夫总是这样：你提出一个想法，他会说，“嗯，好吧，我不知道。”24 小时后，它会带着轻微的修改回来，“我有了一个天才的想法。”迟早，我们会环顾桌子，翻个白眼，心想“又来了”。所以这有点打击你产生想法的积极性。我们习惯了，但后来在团队里，这并不能带来最好的结果。因为如果反应从来不是“这是个天才的想法”，而总是消极或中性的，那么它就没有那种你想要的、能让你发挥最佳状态的情感效果。

transcript.secZh: 与乔布斯作为领导者和设计师的差异

transcript.sec: Balancing Praise and Criticism

turns.en: Sometimes it's fun when people get excited about it, just yeah, yeah, yeah. You kind of build on top of excitement. But it could be coupled with harshness when the idea is bad, and you call out the bad ideas too. So it's the good and the bad. You could say, you don't have to say 'bad ideas,' like 'maybe not now, let's table that for later, let's discuss it,' or say 'that's a decent idea, but did you think about it this way?' Not just 'no' or 'yes,' but let's talk about why that might not be applicable in this case, so that they can learn. So the next time they bring an idea, they can modulate and understand and start seeing through the opinion-based decision makers or the databases to bring better formatted arguments or ideas, so that you have a better chance of success the next time.
turns.zh: 有时候人们兴奋起来也挺有意思的，就是“对对对”，你会在那种兴奋之上继续构建。但同时也得在想法不好的时候严厉指出，把坏点子也点出来。所以好坏都要有。你可以说，不一定要直接说“这想法不好”，可以说“现在可能不太合适，我们先放一放，回头再讨论”，或者说“这个想法还行，但你有没有从这个角度想过？”不只是说“不”或“是”，而是聊聊为什么在这个情况下可能不适用，这样他们就能学到东西。下次他们再提想法的时候，就能调整、理解，开始看穿那些凭主观做决定的人或者数据，拿出更有条理的论据或想法，这样下次成功的几率就更高。

turns.en: Yeah, right. You gotta train through those moments. You gotta teach; those are teaching moments. Yeah, moments I aspire to be that kind of person. I'll usually say that that idea is... like, I remember that this brilliant person just gave that really shitty idea, so I remember to make sure the next time they give a good idea, really compliment that good idea. But I personally, I mean it's emotion, but I call out the really shitty ideas. But you should call out the really great ones too. Yeah, if you let the pendulum swing both ways, then everybody goes, 'He's balanced.' Yeah, it's always one way. Why bring any idea? I'm all about the pendulum. Right, you gotta have both the joy and the pain. Don't just give me pain all the time.
turns.zh: 对，没错。你得在这些时刻里训练他们。你得教，这些都是教学时刻。是啊，我希望自己能成为那样的人。我通常会这么说，那个想法……就像，我记得某个很聪明的人刚刚提了个特别烂的主意，所以我会记住，下次他们提出好主意的时候，一定要大力表扬那个好主意。但就我个人而言，我是说这跟情绪有关，我会直接指出那些特别烂的想法。但你也应该把特别好的想法也点出来。对，如果你让钟摆两边都摆，大家就会觉得“他很平衡”。是啊，如果总是一边倒，谁还愿意提想法呢？我完全赞成钟摆效应。对，你得既有快乐也有痛苦，别老是给我痛苦。

transcript.sec: The Origin Story of the iPhone

turns.en: So you mentioned the Glass and the iPhone. So you're one of the — not just the iPod, not just Nest — you were one of the key figures in the creation of the iPhone. What's the interesting aspects? What's the good, the bad, and the ugly of the origin story of the iPhone? Again, this is a Netflix series that spans multiple seasons, but what changed my flight? Please, yeah. What wisdom? What interesting memories do you have from the final? So the pain and the joy that was foundational to the iPod — all the CDs you had to lug around. What was the pain and the joy and the vision of the iPhone in your mind, in the mind of the team, and Steve's mind, and so on?
turns.zh: 你提到了玻璃和 iPhone。所以你是——不只是 iPod，不只是 Nest——你是 iPhone 创造过程中的关键人物之一。iPhone 起源故事中有哪些有趣的方面？有哪些好的、坏的和丑陋的地方？再说一次，这就像一部 Netflix 系列剧，有好几季，但什么改变了我的航班？请说吧，对。有什么智慧？你对最终版本有什么有趣的回忆？那么，作为 iPod 基础的那种痛苦和快乐——所有那些你得拖着走的 CD。在你看来，在团队看来，在史蒂夫看来，iPhone 的痛苦、快乐和愿景是什么？

turns.en: Well, you know, there's multiple pains. You also have to look — there's not just customer pain, but there's business pain. Okay, and it's about the — so Apple now is getting out of that place where it was in 2001. Now people are starting to pay attention. Apple's starting to get in the culture again, it's becoming relevant. Cash is starting to flow. iPod is 60% of the revenue, total revenue of Apple, doing 85% market share. You're starting to get a win at your back. You got confidence. Like Apple had been beaten down since probably the first time the Mac was — since the Mac, it was a beaten company ever since the Mac. So we're talking 15 years at that point, right? This is the first time you're seeing — and Steve stood proudly in front of us and said, 'Today I can tell you all, the employees, we are now out of debt. We paid off our debt.' It was a joyous moment for him, right? And then ultimately for our team, because no more debt. It's wonderful, right? So now what you have is you have this successful thing changing the face of Apple, and you hear these heavy stomping footsteps of the mobile phone industry. Boom. And it's the feature phones at that time. They're adding cameras, they're adding color displays. They're seeing the success of the iPod, going, 'That's just music. We have some storage. We can load music on our phones, and we can do what the iPod does, plus more.' Boom, yeah, right? And you're like — and how many? Hundreds of millions of them are being sold at that point. It wasn't billions yet, but it was still 100 million, 200 million a year. iPod hadn't gotten there; it was 4, 20, 40, 50 million, something like that. So now you're like, 'Okay, what are we gonna do about this? You know, Goliath who wants to take our lunch, right? The schoolyard bully.' And so, well, there was one: let's partner with them. So iTunes Music Store was there. All of these phones are going to need music, so they can come to the iTunes Music Store and get that music for those phones. Because it wasn't just about the hardware player at that point; it was about the software that you need on the desktop and the content that you needed to download. So now Apple had multiple legs of the stool, as Steve would always refer to it. So now the mobile phone industry — okay, we're going to work with them. They are going to make a red — an iPod shuffle basically inside of a phone. 'Can I have 99 songs total?' And they're going to come to our store. And I was like, 'Okay, great. It's all gonna be well and good.' And that became the Motorola ROKR project. It was Apple and Motorola getting together. There's gonna be software on this smartphone — or not smartphone, but feature phone — to connect to iTunes to get your music. It wasn't even downloadable over a cloud or anything, because that wasn't available yet. There wasn't data networks yet. Um, it was a disaster from the beginning. Two different cultures, two different types of leadership styles, not necessarily the most competent engineers on the other side. And it turned out to be an absolute horrible disaster. I watched the pains because, luckily, I didn't have to be part of it. I watched the pains on Jeff Robin's face each time we would meet, and he would be like, 'These guys are just — you know, like, really? Do we have to do this, Steve?' And he's like, 'We're contractually obligated.' And when it came out on stage and Steve showed it, it was maybe a one-minute — you know, Steve loves those extended, you know, like drawn out — it might have been a one-minute, two-minute kind of thing. And he literally threw that phone out of his hand as fast as he could. Yeah, right? Because it was horrible. So that was the pain of 'we're not gonna partner.' So if we can't partner with these guys, we have to become one of them to actually compete. Yeah, to save the thing that is bringing Apple from that 15 years of malaise, right? So then from that, we made a prototype of an iPod plus phone — a classic with, well, it was an iPod but had a phone inside, with all the music and all the other stuff, and you use your headset, wired headset, to do the audio, right? There was another project at the same time, because we were doing videos in the iTunes Music Store — iTunes Video Store — for music videos and movies. And it would be a full-screen iPod. So instead of the classic, the way you know it, it would be full screen and it would have a virtual click wheel. You'd have a virtual, like, single-touch touch screen that you could scroll. Right? Think of maybe an iPhone-like you knew it, right? And then there was a third project going on — not in those two were going on in my team, but the third project going on was the multi-touch screen technology to drive a Mac tablet. And so that Mac tablet, that touch screen technology — there was just —
turns.zh: 嗯，你知道，有很多痛点。你还要看到——不只是客户的痛点，还有业务的痛点。好吧，这关系到——苹果当时正在走出 2001 年的困境。现在人们开始关注了。苹果再次进入文化主流，变得重要起来。现金流开始涌入。iPod 占了苹果总营收的 60%，市场份额达到 85%。你开始有胜算在握了。你有了信心。就像苹果自从 Mac 以来——自从 Mac 以来，它就一直是一家被打压的公司。所以到那时已经 15 年了，对吧？这是你第一次看到——史蒂夫自豪地站在我们面前说：“今天我可以告诉你们所有员工，我们现在没有债务了。我们还清了债务。”对他来说那是一个喜悦的时刻，对吧？最终对我们的团队也是，因为没有债务了。太棒了，对吧？所以现在你有了这个成功的东西，正在改变苹果的面貌，然后你听到了手机行业沉重的脚步声。砰。那时候是功能手机。他们在加摄像头，加彩色屏幕。他们看到了 iPod 的成功，心想：“那只是音乐。我们有存储空间。我们可以在手机上加载音乐，我们能做到 iPod 能做的，甚至更多。”砰，对吧？然后你想——有多少？那时候每年卖出几亿部。还没到几十亿，但每年也有一亿、两亿部。iPod 还没到那个量；它只有 400 万、2000 万、4000 万、5000 万部左右。所以现在你想：“好吧，我们该怎么办？你知道，那个想抢我们饭碗的歌利亚，对吧？那个校园恶霸。”所以，有一个办法：跟他们合作。iTunes 音乐商店已经存在了。所有这些手机都需要音乐，所以他们可以到 iTunes 音乐商店为那些手机获取音乐。因为那时候不光是硬件播放器的问题；还有桌面端需要的软件和需要下载的内容。所以现在苹果有了多条腿，就像史蒂夫常说的那样。所以现在手机行业——好吧，我们要跟他们合作。他们要做一个红色的——基本上就是把 iPod shuffle 塞进手机里。“我能有总共 99 首歌吗？”然后他们会来我们的商店。我当时想：“好吧，太好了。一切都会顺利的。”这就成了摩托罗拉 ROKR 项目。是苹果和摩托罗拉合作的。这个智能手机——或者不是智能手机，是功能手机——上会有软件连接到 iTunes 获取音乐。甚至还不能通过云下载什么的，因为那时候还没有。还没有数据网络。嗯，从一开始就是个灾难。两种不同的文化，两种不同的领导风格，对方那边也不一定是最有能力的工程师。结果成了一个彻头彻尾的灾难。我目睹了那些痛苦，因为幸运的是我不必参与其中。每次我们见面，我都看到杰夫·罗宾脸上的痛苦，他会说：“这些人就是——你知道，真的吗？史蒂夫，我们非得这么做吗？”史蒂夫会说：“我们有合同义务。”当它出现在舞台上，史蒂夫展示它的时候，可能只有一分钟——你知道，史蒂夫喜欢那种延长的、拉长的展示——可能就一两分钟的事。然后他 literally 以最快的速度把那部手机从手里扔了出去。对吧？因为它太糟糕了。所以那就是“我们不会合作”的痛苦。所以如果我们不能跟这些人合作，我们就得成为他们中的一员才能真正竞争。对，为了拯救那个把苹果从 15 年的低迷中拉出来的东西，对吧？所以从那以后，我们做了一个 iPod 加手机的原型——一个经典款，嗯，就是一个 iPod 但里面装了电话，有所有音乐和其他东西，你用耳机，有线耳机，来做音频，对吧？同时还有另一个项目，因为我们在 iTunes 音乐商店——iTunes 视频商店——做视频，包括音乐视频和电影。那会是一个全屏的 iPod。所以不是经典款，你知道的那种，它会是一个全屏的，有一个虚拟的点击轮。你会有一个虚拟的、单点触控的触摸屏，可以滚动。对吧？想想你可能知道的 iPhone 的样子，对吧？然后还有第三个项目在进行——那两个是在我的团队里，但第三个项目是驱动 Mac 平板的多点触控屏幕技术。所以那个 Mac 平板，那个触屏技术——就是——

transcript.sec: The Birth of the iPhone: Combining Technologies

turns.en: Way too much you had to change on the software and everything to be able to use a tablet, right? We see this all the time. People say there aren't enough tablet apps today that are modified for tablet; they're just phone apps that are grown up. So then they would just be Mac touch stuff. You'd have to have a whole developer community. That probably wasn't the best place to take that technology first. So you take that technology, marry it with the full-screen iPod and the phone stuff we were working on, because the iPod phone with a rotary dial was just like a rotary phone. We couldn't make that interface work well for data input. You put those three together, and now that's where those three things created the form or the technology and the form inside what would become the iPhone, married with a bunch of low-level software from the iPod and manufacturing software and drivers and communication stuff, combined with a very reduced Frankenstein Mac OS. And I mean that in the best way. It wasn't Mac OS just changed a little; it was totally hacked out and changed, and new code was inserted. It really was a whole set of things from all different places to make that first iPhone OS. And then there was another team working on the apps, and another team working on the design of how it looked overall. Between all that stuff, all of those things came together to create what we know as the first-generation iPhone. And those are all probably fascinating engineering challenges, correct? And great teams. Like the creating Frankenstein OS, that's fascinating because you're simplifying, simplifying, but then you're just pulling different stuff from, and you're basically inventing—I mean, they're probably not thinking of it that way—but a new era of computing, a new kind of computer. It really is Frankenstein, right? And you didn't have to run Mac software. If you look at some of the other smartphones of the time, like Windows and stuff, they were like, "We need to make sure it runs Excel and it runs Word or something like that in some reduced thing." This was like, "No, no, no, no." This was born out of entertainment. So we didn't have to go and take all the same applications. All those other ones were about compatibility; this was about a whole new way of being.
turns.zh: 要使用平板电脑，你必须在软件和所有方面做太多改动，对吧？我们一直看到这种情况。人们说今天没有足够多的平板应用是为平板优化的；它们只是放大了的手机应用。所以它们就只是 Mac 触摸版的东西。你需要一整个开发者社区。那可能不是最先应用这项技术的最佳场所。所以你把那项技术，与全屏 iPod 和我们正在做的手机功能结合起来，因为带旋转拨盘的 iPod 手机就像一部旋转拨号电话。我们无法让那个界面很好地处理数据输入。你把这三者结合起来，现在这三样东西创造了形态，或者说技术以及 iPhone 内部的形态，再结合来自 iPod 的大量底层软件、制造软件、驱动程序和通信组件，再加上一个大幅精简的“弗兰肯斯坦”版 Mac OS。我这么说完全是褒义。它并不是 Mac OS 稍作改动；而是被彻底改造、删改，并插入了新代码。它真的是来自不同地方的一整套东西，共同构成了第一个 iPhone OS。然后还有另一个团队在开发应用，另一个团队在设计整体外观。所有这些元素汇聚在一起，创造了我们所知的第一代 iPhone。这些可能都是迷人的工程挑战，对吧？还有伟大的团队。就像创造“弗兰肯斯坦”操作系统，那很迷人，因为你不断简化，但你又从不同地方提取东西，你基本上是在发明——我的意思是，他们可能不这么想——但这是一个新的计算时代，一种新型计算机。它真的是“弗兰肯斯坦”，对吧？而且你不需要运行 Mac 软件。如果你看看当时其他的智能手机，比如 Windows 之类的，它们会说：“我们需要确保它能以某种精简形式运行 Excel 和 Word。”但这个不一样：“不，不，不。”它诞生于娱乐。所以我们不必去移植所有相同的应用。其他那些都是关于兼容性；而这是关于一种全新的存在方式。

transcript.sec: Steve Jobs' Presentation: Storytelling and Showmanship

turns.en: What did you think about the Steve Jobs presentation of the iPhone, the sort of the first iPhone? You know, "Phone, internet communicator, and iPod in your pocket." They're going to sort of present announcing three new products kind of thing, and then saying that it's all in one. This is a good example of one of the sort of historic presentations of a product. Clearly there's some showmanship that works. Some reason it works, right? It doesn't always work; it often doesn't work, but it did in this case. It often did for Steve. What like, how did that feel? What part of the actually the design process was that presentation? You know what I mean? Like from the early—because you said for sure—so consider the why, the press release at the very—exactly. Steve was doing that the entire time. He was working on that story from day one. Yeah, he was pitching us this, this, this, and then this, and then he would look at our faces because you wouldn't—most people wouldn't, at least if you're working for him—wouldn't tell him what you really thought of what he was saying, but he would look at your faces. Yeah, and then he would talk to a few real trusted confidants outside of the organization and see what they thought, right? And they could give him feedback on it, and they could really challenge him, but he would also look at their faces and go. And so when you see that, then he would modulate it and change it slightly and change. So he was working during all of that time on the story and the storytelling, right? And the whys, while we're working on that and helping us refine it, just like the switch from plastic to glass, right? All the time working on that. So when he comes out on stage, he does something that every marketer is told not to do: say these three things are now combined in one. That is like the—they say that that is the laziest form of storytelling possible for marketing, right? Yeah, right. But it was the best one because it was all those pains. It was like, "I want my iPod, but I want my communications, and I want my internet browsing because I want it on the go so I can look up things because it was information." And when you were on the road, you had a laptop, you had an iPod, and you had a phone, and you had to carry all of these things with you at once. Now we're going to solve that pain for you and put it all together. So he was just showing you the pain and viewing that virus of doubt and going, "It's now in this one magical thing." And he could come up and masterfully tell that story because he told it almost every day to all of these people inside very quietly, and then it was just right. It was like a Tony Award-winning play that had been worked on for 10 years. But also the human came through, the timing, it was all that. It was a little bit—and of course he was dramatic at certain points, and he would raise his voice and a wry smile or whatever. It was all those touches. He was an actor as well as a storyteller. Yeah, and that—but it was the truth, right? The truth came through. It was a non-fiction story, and then he added those personal flourishes on top of it for dramatic effect. It's amazing.
turns.zh: 你怎么看史蒂夫·乔布斯对 iPhone 的演示，就是第一款 iPhone？你知道，“手机、互联网通讯器和 iPod 装入口袋”。他们打算宣布三款新产品，然后说它们合而为一。这是一个很好的例子，属于那种历史性的产品演示。显然有些表演技巧奏效了。出于某种原因它奏效了，对吧？它并不总是奏效；通常它不奏效，但这次奏效了。对史蒂夫来说经常如此。那感觉如何？那个演示实际上在设计过程中扮演了什么角色？你明白我的意思吗？从早期开始——因为你肯定知道——所以考虑那个“为什么”，那个新闻稿，完全正确。史蒂夫一直在做那件事。他从第一天起就在构思那个故事。是的，他向我们推销这个、这个、这个，然后这个，然后他会看我们的脸，因为你不会——大多数人不会，至少如果你为他工作——不会告诉他你对他所说的话的真实想法，但他会看你的脸。是的，然后他会和组织外少数真正信任的知己交谈，看看他们的想法，对吧？他们可以给他反馈，他们可以真正挑战他，但他也会看他们的脸，然后思考。所以当你看到那个，他就会调整它，稍微改变它，再改变。所以他在那整个时间里都在打磨故事和讲故事的方式，对吧？还有那些“为什么”，而我们在工作的同时，他也在帮助我们完善，就像从塑料到玻璃的转变，对吧？一直在琢磨。所以当他走上舞台时，他做了一件每个营销人员都被告诫不要做的事：说这三样东西现在合而为一。他们说那是营销中最懒惰的讲故事方式，对吧？是的，没错。但这是最好的方式，因为它直击所有痛点。就像，“我想要我的 iPod，但我也想要通讯，我还想要上网浏览，因为我想要在移动中查东西，因为那是信息。”当你出差时，你带着笔记本电脑、iPod 和手机，你必须同时携带所有这些。现在我们要为你解决那个痛点，把它们合而为一。所以他只是向你展示那个痛点，审视那种怀疑的病毒，然后说，“现在它就在这个神奇的东西里。”他能够巧妙地讲述那个故事，因为他几乎每天都在内部对所有人悄悄地讲，然后它就恰到好处了。就像一部托尼奖获奖戏剧，已经打磨了 10 年。但人性也流露出来，时机，所有的一切。有一点——当然他在某些时刻很戏剧化，他会提高声音，露出苦笑或别的什么。所有这些细节。他既是演员又是讲故事的人。是的，而且——但那是真相，对吧？真相流露出来了。那是一个非虚构的故事，然后他在上面添加了那些个人修饰以达到戏剧效果。太神奇了。

transcript.secZh: 乔布斯的演讲：叙事与表演

transcript.sec: Learning from Jony Ive: Design Process and Materials

turns.en: So there's a designer you mentioned, Jony Ive. You both are brilliant designers, great human beings. There were some battles fought in the distant past between the two of you. Looking back, what is the positive characteristic of Jony that made you a better person and designer having worked with him?
turns.zh: 你提到了一位设计师，乔纳森·艾维。你们俩都是杰出的设计师，伟大的人。在遥远的过去，你们之间有过一些争执。回顾过去，乔纳森有什么积极的品质，让你在与他共事后成为了更好的人和设计师？

turns.en: Watching the process that the design team that Jony led—I don't know where, because that was over years, I didn't see all of those things—but watching the design process of really, because it was really a team that was about materials, it was about form, it was about colors, it was about these physical characteristics. It, when we talked about this earlier, was design. What is design? Design is everywhere. Okay, so what they were really focused on was form, how the feel was, how it looked, the aesthetics, the physical aesthetics. And watching going through that process, I learned so much in that process about how to do colors, how to do materials, how to think deeply about curves, right? And shadows, and how it would look not just in your hand but how it would look in the photograph you were going to take from marketing, right? So how it would look, how it would feel, all of it. It was all of those physical things around that. And watching the process to get there, that was enlightening for me, right? It opened my mind to go, "Oh, okay, just like there's a process for all these other things, it wasn't just magic." And you say, "Ah, there it is." It was really a process of refinement, you know, of opening the—
turns.zh: 观察乔纳森领导的设计团队的过程——我不知道具体在哪里，因为那是多年的事，我没有看到所有那些——但观察真正的设计过程，因为它确实是一个关于材料、形态、颜色和这些物理特性的团队。当我们之前谈到这个时，那就是设计。什么是设计？设计无处不在。好吧，所以他们真正关注的是形态、手感、外观、美学、物理美学。通过观察那个过程，我学到了很多关于如何处理颜色、如何处理材料、如何深入思考曲线，对吧？还有阴影，以及它不仅在手中看起来如何，而且在营销照片中看起来如何，对吧？所以它看起来如何，感觉如何，所有的一切。都是围绕那些物理特性。观察达到那个目标的过程，对我来说很有启发性，对吧？它打开了我的思维，让我想，“哦，好吧，就像所有其他事情都有流程一样，它不仅仅是魔法。”然后你说，“啊，就是这样。”那真的是一个精炼的过程，你知道，打开——

transcript.secZh: 向乔纳森·艾维学习：设计流程与材料

transcript.sec: Design Philosophy: Balancing Ideals and Constraints

turns.en: Funnel at the beginning and refining down over time to get to that final final and selecting and doing the selection and certain types you could certain times they were opinion-based design details but a lot of data a lot of data-driven designs of what can we what can we deliver in volume what can we do different things so you always had these constraints that you had to work with under and sometimes they uh and the team not just i would say we need this we're like we can't deliver that but maybe we were able to work together to find different design characteristics and different implementation characteristics that could get to that point without what they were describing and instead of yes yes yes no no no let's find some other way to solve the problem together yeah is and i've seen this um and several companies have more closely interacted with like tesla as an example sometimes you know talking about curves sometimes it's very painful on the engineering side to deliver a very specific kind of absolute thing and one question that comes up in my mind is like well how far should we go to try to deliver a tiny adjustment in a curve in the curvature or in um like the whatever the form factor is in the color of the material when the cost is like 10x to deliver not financially but just like you know yeah there are companies to do it how many problems still have to solve um i don't know i don't know if you can say any wisdom to that because it because when you're thinking about curves you're designing in the space of ideas you're like platonic forms kind of thing uh not always grounded to like how much this is uh how much pain is going to be involved in delivering this but that's as you should perhaps because then uh if you're always thinking about the pain required to deliver this thing you you'll be too conservative you wouldn't do the wild ideas right exactly
turns.zh: 一开始像漏斗一样，随着时间推移不断收窄，最终得到那个最终版本。选择过程里，有些是凭观点的设计细节，但也有很多数据驱动的设计——我们能大批量交付什么？我们能做哪些不同的事？所以你总是要面对这些约束。有时候团队会说“我们需要这个”，但我们会说“我们交付不了”，不过也许我们能一起找到不同的设计特性和实现方式，在不完全按他们描述的情况下达到目标。不是一味地说“是是是”或“不不不”，而是一起寻找其他解决方案。我在好几家公司都见过这种情况，比如和特斯拉合作时，有时候讨论曲线，工程端要实现一个非常具体、绝对的东西会非常痛苦。我脑子里冒出一个问题：我们到底应该走多远，去实现曲线上一个微小的调整，或者外形、材料颜色上的变化，如果交付成本是 10 倍（不是财务上的，而是工程上的）？有些公司确实会这么做，但还有多少问题要解决？我不知道你是否有什么智慧可以分享，因为当你思考曲线时，你是在想法的空间里设计，有点像柏拉图式的形式，并不总是考虑实现这个东西要付出多少痛苦。但也许正因如此，如果你总是想着实现它要付出的痛苦，你就会过于保守，就不会有那些疯狂的想法了，对吧？

turns.en: But you have to understand again the why behind it. And at Nest when we had limited resources, you know putting a screwdriver in the box, a custom designed screwdriver in the box, was born out of those experiences I had at Apple and seeing how you can create something that's emotional, it's part of marketing and it's part of the product experience overall even though it seems extraneous. I went back and made the design team and the mechanical team changed some curves on the Nest Protect, the smoke and CO detector we did at Nest, after they had already tooled it. And I said they're saying this cost more. I said it doesn't look right. There is a but they're like oh well we had I said no you're going to go back and you're going to make that change. I told you you want we needed to do it. We had a better looking model that is going to get done. I know it's going to be a terrible cost to you but we already had this discussion and that's the way it's gonna have to be and I'm sorry but it is what it is. And you know, because it's better for the customer and it looks better and the pictures and all the other stuff. And then we did it and it was great and everyone agreed it was great at the end, but it was pain to get there. Those are where those little details are where the magic comes out. And you know, if you don't do, if you don't take those pains and put in the love, the customer's going to feel it. They're either going to feel the pain or they're going to feel the love if you put it in right. So it depends on you know how much time and effort you want to put into something and what really matters to you. And so how you communicate what you do, we're human beings after all.
turns.zh: 但你必须再次理解背后的“为什么”。在 Nest，当我们资源有限时，把一把螺丝刀放进包装盒里——一把定制设计的螺丝刀——正是源于我在苹果的经历，以及看到如何创造有情感的东西。它是营销的一部分，也是整体产品体验的一部分，尽管看起来有些多余。我回去让设计团队和机械团队改变了 Nest Protect（我们做的烟雾和一氧化碳探测器）上的一些曲线，那时他们已经开好模具了。我说他们告诉我这样成本更高，我说它看起来不对。他们说“哦，我们已经……”，我说“不，你们要回去做这个改动。我告诉过你们我们需要这样做。我们有一个更好看的模型，必须完成。我知道这对你们来说成本很高，但我们之前已经讨论过了，事情必须这样。对不起，但事实就是如此。”你知道，因为这对客户更好，看起来更好，照片效果也更好。然后我们做了，结果很棒，最后每个人都同意它很棒，但过程很痛苦。正是这些细节创造了魔力。如果你不付出这些痛苦，不投入爱，客户会感受到的。他们要么感受到痛苦，要么感受到爱——如果你做对了的话。所以这取决于你愿意投入多少时间和精力，以及什么对你真正重要。以及你如何沟通你所做的，毕竟我们是人。

turns.en: Is there something you've learned from sort of the the tensions that are natural or that happen in teams when they're passionate and they're trying to solve these problems? Is that the way of life and there's the human drama? Is that just is that always going? Is it what it is? Does that make you better?
turns.zh: 你从团队中自然产生的或当人们充满热情、试图解决这些问题时发生的紧张关系中学到了什么吗？这是生活的常态，是人类的戏剧吗？它总是这样吗？它就是那样吗？它让你变得更好吗？

turns.en: Actually, the drama of the tension between personalities and all that kind of stuff, look, a roller coaster ride without ups and downs is no fun. It's the journey. Yeah, it's the journey that brings out the best in everyone. We're forged, we're tempered by those experiences. Not all the ups, but also the downs. And that's when you get the humanity in the connection. And we can tell these stories till we're blue in the face and smile every time because we did something together that each of us couldn't do apart. But when it comes together, that's where all the emotions happen. And that's where if it's born out of the right reasons and the right story in the right way, that's where the magic happens. Not just for the customer, but for how it transforms each person who is working on it. And they will never forget those experiences in their life, positively and negatively that happened at the time, but they look back and it's only positive because they did something that mattered.
turns.zh: 实际上，个性之间的紧张关系等等，你看，过山车如果没有起伏就不好玩了。重要的是旅程。是的，正是旅程让每个人展现出最好的一面。我们被这些经历锻造、淬炼。不仅是高潮，还有低谷。正是在那时，你获得了人性中的连接。我们可以把这些故事讲到脸色发青，每次都微笑，因为我们一起做了每个人单独无法完成的事。当它汇聚在一起时，所有的情感就产生了。如果它源于正确的理由、正确的故事和正确的方式，那就是魔法发生的地方。不仅对客户如此，对每个参与其中的人也是如此，它改变了他们。他们永远不会忘记那些经历——无论是当时积极的还是消极的——但回首往事，只有积极，因为他们做了有意义的事。

transcript.secZh: 设计哲学：平衡理想与约束

transcript.sec: Nest Thermostat: Making People Care

turns.en: Yet another brilliant idea that you brought to life is Nest, Nest thermostat, and the big umbrella of Nest again as part of this Netflix series season three. What was the most memorable, the most painful, the most insight-leading challenge you had to overcome to bring Nest to life?
turns.zh: 你实现的另一个绝妙想法是 Nest，Nest 恒温器，以及 Nest 这个大框架，再次作为 Netflix 系列第三季的一部分。为了将 Nest 变为现实，你不得不克服的最难忘、最痛苦、最具洞察力的挑战是什么？

turns.en: Well, the first thing for me was making someone care about their thermostat, right? No one considers it. They never had any customer choice. They didn't install it. They usually don't even use it because it's so complicated or what have you. They just like, they just at it, they hide it in the corner and then they just pay the bill, right? Of whatever it is. Right, it's totally unloved, unconsidered. Right. So how do you wake up, like I said, the virus of doubt, how do you wake that up and get people going? You know, remember every day when you go in and it's like you're just frustrated and then you get the bill and you pay the bill. So you have to do that. So that was one thing. I think the other big one was not delivering. You know, it was all of it was hard, right? It was constrained. We had only so much stuff. We were bootstrapped. We didn't have massive funding. We didn't get hundreds of millions of dollars. It was but we did it for the right reasons. But I think the other big part of it was not just building a disruptive product, because a lot of the people on the team had done that. We knew what we were doing. And that was if we got the design right, we could deliver it with the enough time. It was getting the disruptive go to market. In other words, how to take that product from the end of the production line and get it into the customer's hands, because there was no retail or customer choice in thermostats. No one even it was never considered purchase. They never thought they had choice. Some guy usually in suspenders and a butt crack told them, looked around, looked at their house and said, um, this looks like somebody who's got is well to do. This thermostat is now going to cost you $350. Thank you very much. And you're like, I'll take whatever you give me, right? And then it goes into another house.
turns.zh: 嗯，对我来说第一件事是让人们关心他们的恒温器，对吧？没人考虑它。他们从来没有客户选择权。他们不安装它。他们通常甚至不用它，因为它太复杂了之类的。他们只是把它藏在角落里，然后付账单，对吧？不管多少。它完全不被爱，不被考虑。对吧。那么如何唤醒——就像我说的，怀疑的病毒——如何唤醒它并让人们行动起来？你知道，记住每天你进去时，你只是感到沮丧，然后你收到账单，你付钱。所以你必须做那件事。所以那是一件事。我认为另一个大问题是交付。你知道，所有事情都很难，对吧？我们受到限制。我们只有这么多资源。我们是白手起家。我们没有大量资金。我们没有拿到数亿美元。但我们出于正确的理由做了。但我认为另一个重要部分不仅仅是构建一个颠覆性产品，因为团队中的很多人以前做过。我们知道自己在做什么。如果我们设计正确，我们可以在足够的时间内交付。关键是颠覆性的市场进入。换句话说，如何将产品从生产线的末端送到客户手中，因为恒温器没有零售或客户选择。甚至没人考虑过购买。他们从未想过自己有选择。通常某个穿着背带裤、露着股沟的家伙，环顾四周，看看他们的房子，然后说：“嗯，这看起来像是个有钱人。这个恒温器现在要花你 350 美元。非常感谢。”然后你就像“你给我什么我就拿什么”，对吧？然后它被装进另一所房子。

transcript.sec: Disrupting the Thermostat Market

turns.en: It's worth a hundred dollars was the same damn thing. Yeah, right. So there's no price transparency, there was no choice, you just got what you were given. So how do you go and... and this was an entrenched industry, that's why there was no innovation in it, because it was doing just fine, because every house needed them. All the installers were programmed by the product deliverers, by bonuses, to say you're going to only carry our product, and if you sell this many you're going to get a free trip to Hawaii. Right, yeah. And for these guys who install, a free trip to Hawaii is a dream for them, right. So this whole channel was fully controlled by the product guys, and it was almost monopolistic in a way. So how do you go around that? Creating a disruptive go-to-market channel: one was direct to consumer, right, and all the marketing that was necessary to get that message across. Another one was getting the installation right. No one was self-installing thermostats. So how do we get enough people who are early adopters to be able to self-install them confidently, so they didn't still have to call the guy to come and install it? Because then he would say, "This is a crap product, no, I got the much better product." Right. So you had to get rid of that friction. And then ultimately, how do you get the people who were not just early adopters, but people who needed to see it and touch it before they bought it? How do you get that into retail, when the large brands of the time of thermostats and Home Depot and Lowe's had contracts that they couldn't bring in any other brands? They were owning the channel all the way to where there was any sort of slight customer choice, and it was really contractor choice more than it was consumer choice. So all of that had to be innovated along with the product. And so to me, that was a huge challenge and something I had never done, most of us had never done, and we had to create that as much as a project as actually delivering the product itself. So it turned out to be a giant hit, and it was acquired by Google for 3.2 billion dollars.
turns.zh: 一个价值一百美元的东西，结果还是那玩意儿。没错。所以没有价格透明度，没有选择，你只能拿到别人给你的东西。那么你怎么去……这是一个根深蒂固的行业，这就是为什么它没有创新，因为它过得很好，因为每个房子都需要它们。所有的安装工都被产品供应商用奖金给“编程”了，让他们说“你只能卖我们的产品，如果你卖这么多，就能免费去夏威夷旅游”。对吧。对这些安装工来说，免费去夏威夷就是他们的梦想。所以整个渠道完全被产品方控制，在某种程度上几乎是垄断的。那么你怎么绕过这个呢？创建一个颠覆性的市场渠道：一是直接面向消费者，以及必要的营销来传递信息。另一个是搞定安装问题。没人会自己安装恒温器。那么如何让足够多的早期用户能够自信地自行安装，这样他们就不必再打电话叫人来安装？因为如果叫人来，那人会说“这是个垃圾产品，不，我有好得多的产品”。所以你必须消除这个摩擦。最后，如何让那些不只是早期用户、而是需要看到和摸到才购买的人？如何让产品进入零售渠道，而当时恒温器的大品牌和家得宝、劳氏都有合同，不能引入其他品牌？他们完全控制了渠道，以至于只有一点点客户选择，实际上更多是承包商的选择，而不是消费者的选择。所以所有这些都必须和产品一起创新。对我来说，这是一个巨大的挑战，也是我从未做过的事，我们大多数人从未做过。我们必须像交付产品本身一样，把它作为一个项目来创造。结果它大获成功，被谷歌以 32 亿美元收购。

transcript.sec: Considering Acquisition: Saying No

turns.en: As a founder and leader, just out of curiosity, in these cases of acquisition, is it always a good thing? Is there any part of you and the team that considers saying no?
turns.zh: 作为创始人和领导者，出于好奇，在这些收购案例中，这总是好事吗？你和团队有没有考虑过说不？

turns.en: Oh, we considered saying no all the way along the process. Right. We had all been in big companies before. We knew what it was like, the politics and all the other stuff. And what I came to learn, especially from Philips, because Philips was a very... it was 375,000 people, it was a big, massive company, right, and tons of politics. And I was like, do we want to go back into that world? Because I had so many negative experiences from that. But then going to Apple, which was not big, but it was big enough that it could have all these dynamics. But then when you saw a leader rise up and get rid of those dynamics or not allow many of them to flourish, then you're like, oh, with the right leadership, this can be a beautiful marriage. Right. And so for four months we were working together with them, with Google, to make sure that we had the right leadership and we were going to be in the right environment that it felt right. So that happened. It absolutely happened. We worked on all the details. We didn't even talk about price. We were talking about how's the brand going to work, who's the team going to work with, how are we going to get IP, how are we going to do exchanges, how are we going to get budgets and all that stuff done. So we worked through all of that before we actually sealed any kind of deal, because they were already an investor in the company, so we already knew, you know, they knew relatively where the end point was for the price. So working through all those prerequisites, I knew that as an individual product company that was trying to create a platform, no investors were going to invest in a platform that could take three or four years and many many hundreds of millions of dollars to build without all kinds of new products at the same time, and products that we were having which were successes but they weren't even break-even yet, right. We were still developing them. So how are we going to get more people to fund all of these things and this platform that I really want to create? Because my worry, and I had seen this many times in Silicon Valley, is these small startups have bravado and they say, "I'm going to take on the big guys, right, with a platform." But when those platform guys show up and Apple says they're going to get in the house... at the time nobody cared, they were curiously curious, "What's Nest?" Yeah, yeah. But Apple wasn't in the market, Google wasn't in the market yet, Amazon wasn't there, Microsoft, Samsung, they were not, they were all just, "That's curious." Right, yeah. And I had watched, if you said, "I'm going to go challenge them and I'm going to build a platform," and then they all of a sudden one by one go, "Oh well, we're building a platform now, we're building a player," they flooded you to death, fear, uncertainty, doubt, and the developers run away, and you can't make that platform. So I'm like, before the landscape gets changed on us because we've attracted so much attention, they announced something, we need to change the landscape on them. Let's go to the best place where we can build out the platform, have the right leadership behind us to help us grow this thing into what the vision it should be. And that's what we believe we were doing with the Google acquisition.
turns.zh: 哦，我们在整个过程中一直在考虑说不。没错。我们之前都在大公司待过。我们知道那是什么样子，政治斗争和其他一切。我从飞利浦那里学到很多，因为飞利浦是一个非常……它有 37.5 万人，是一家非常大的公司，政治斗争无数。我当时想，我们想回到那个世界吗？因为我有太多负面经历。但后来去了苹果，苹果不算大，但也足够大，可以有所有这些动态。但当你看到一个领导者崛起，消除这些动态，或者不让它们泛滥时，你就会想，哦，有正确的领导，这可以是一段美好的婚姻。所以有整整四个月，我们和谷歌一起工作，确保我们有正确的领导，并且我们处于合适的环境中，感觉对了。所以那确实发生了。我们讨论了所有细节。我们甚至没谈价格。我们讨论的是品牌如何运作，团队与谁合作，如何获取知识产权，如何进行交换，如何获得预算等等。我们在实际敲定任何交易之前就解决了所有这些问题，因为他们已经是公司的投资者，所以我们知道他们大致知道价格的终点。在解决所有这些先决条件的过程中，我意识到，作为一个试图创建平台的单一产品公司，没有投资者会投资一个需要三四年、数亿美元才能建成的平台，同时还需要各种新产品，而我们已有的产品虽然成功，但甚至还没达到盈亏平衡。我们还在开发它们。那么如何让更多人资助所有这些事情和我真正想创建的这个平台呢？因为我的担忧——我在硅谷见过很多次——是这些小初创公司有虚张声势，他们说“我要用平台挑战大公司”。但当那些平台玩家出现，苹果说他们要进入这个领域……当时没人关心，他们只是好奇，“Nest 是什么？”没错。但苹果还没进入市场，谷歌还没进入，亚马逊不在，微软、三星都不在，他们都只是觉得“有点意思”。我观察到，如果你说“我要挑战他们，我要建一个平台”，然后他们突然一个接一个地说“哦，我们现在也在建平台，我们在建一个玩家”，他们就会用恐惧、不确定性和怀疑把你淹没，开发者会跑掉，你就建不成那个平台。所以我想，在我们因为吸引了太多注意力而让格局改变之前，他们宣布了什么，我们需要改变格局。让我们去最好的地方，在那里我们可以建立平台，有正确的领导支持我们，把这个东西发展成它应有的愿景。这就是我们相信通过谷歌收购所做的事情。

transcript.sec: Taking on Platforms: Possible but on Different Turf

turns.en: Is it possible to take on the platforms? So you said there's a lot of startups with bravado and all that kind of stuff, right. Doesn't mean... you know, James Joyce when he was 20 said, "I'm going to be the greatest writer of the 20th century," before he wrote anything of value. You know, one of them might be actually right. In this modern world, when you... so first of all, people should definitely get your book, Build, as just this giant number of advice on this exact question of how to build cool things, how to build a startup, how to all the different stages of that team and hiring, and it's mostly human nature, it's not technical, it's mostly human nature behind it. And it turns out it's turtles all the way down, that's human at the bottom. Yes. So is it possible to build startups that take on the big guys, whatever that is of the modern era? So for now it's these platforms of Apple, Google, Twitter, I don't even know, Meta I guess called now. Sure. Is it possible to take them on?
turns.zh: 有没有可能挑战这些平台？你说有很多初创公司有虚张声势，对吧。但这并不意味着……你知道，詹姆斯·乔伊斯 20 岁时说“我要成为 20 世纪最伟大的作家”，在他写出任何有价值的东西之前。你知道，其中可能有一个是对的。在这个现代世界，当你……首先，人们绝对应该读你的书《创造》，它提供了大量关于如何建造酷东西、如何创建初创公司、如何应对团队和招聘的不同阶段的建议，而且这主要是人性，不是技术，背后主要是人性。结果发现，这就像层层叠叠的乌龟，最底层还是人性。是的。那么，有没有可能创建挑战大公司的初创公司，不管现代的大公司是什么？目前是这些平台：苹果、谷歌、推特，甚至我不知道，现在叫 Meta 了吧。当然。有可能挑战它们吗？

turns.en: Absolutely. But you don't take them on on their same turf. You take them on on the turf they're going to want to have in the future. Right. Spotify is a platform. It started as an application, is now a platform. Yeah, right. Think of WeChat.
