Think of all the super apps out there that are now wallets and delivery services and travel services and transportation services all within an app. Yeah. They've innovated in a different level, in a different space that the platform...
turns.zh: 绝对可以。但你不能在他们的地盘上挑战他们。你要在他们未来想要的地盘上挑战他们。没错。Spotify 是一个平台。它最初是一个应用，现在是一个平台。是的，没错。想想微信。想想所有那些超级应用，它们现在集成了钱包、配送服务、旅行服务和交通服务，都在一个应用里。是的。他们在不同的层面、不同的空间进行了创新，而平台……

transcript.secZh: 挑战平台：可能但需另辟蹊径

transcript.sec: Building a platform company starts with a great app

turns.en: Companies weren't right or they, you know, Google was an app company. It was solving search and then it became a platform company. Apple was solving personal computing and then, our iPhone was doing internet browsing, all that stuff, and then became a platform company when the App Store was added. If you look it up, there's no such thing as building a platform company. You build a great app first and then you can expand it and have the right to become a platform.
turns.zh: 有些公司一开始并不对，比如谷歌是一家应用公司，它解决搜索问题，然后才成为平台公司。苹果解决个人计算问题，我们的 iPhone 解决了上网浏览等等，然后在加入 App Store 后才成为平台公司。如果你仔细看，根本不存在直接建立平台公司这回事。你先打造一个出色的应用，然后才能扩展，才有资格成为平台。

transcript.secZh: 打造平台公司始于优秀应用

transcript.sec: Career advice: what to learn and who to learn from

turns.en: Your whole book is just a bunch of advice for young people. But let me ask you, I know other people and old—well, everyone is young at heart. If you're not, you should be. So, in terms of picking a career, you have advice on this point. What advice would you give to a person on how to pick a career?
turns.zh: 你的整本书都是给年轻人的建议。但我想问你，我也认识其他人和年长者——好吧，每个人都有一颗年轻的心。如果你没有，你应该有。那么在职业选择上，你有什么建议？你会给一个人如何选择职业什么建议？

turns.en: What is it you want to learn and who is it you want to learn from? Just like you pick a university—you're like, I want to go here for this expertise, I've heard about these programs, especially graduate studies. You go for a certain program with a certain set of people. Why don't you do that when it comes to a job or a career? You just don't go and say, I just want to go work at Google or I just want to work at Apple. You want to go to a certain team with a certain set of people and work with them on something that you're really curious about and you want to learn about.
turns.zh: 你想学什么？你想向谁学？就像你选大学一样——你会说，我想来这里是因为这个专业，我听说过这些项目，尤其是研究生项目。你为了某个项目、某群人而去。为什么找工作或选职业时不这么做呢？你不能只是说，我就想去谷歌工作，或者我就想去苹果工作。你应该去某个团队，和某群人一起，做你真正好奇、想学习的事情。

turns.en: That's such a subtle but brilliant framing: just ask the question, what do I want to learn, and then see what career path is going to maximize that. That's so interesting. It's the first question I ask anyone who interviews with me. When I say I'm going to bring somebody on the team, first question is, what do you want to learn? I don't want the expert—like we talked about earlier—says, I'm the expert in this, you're going to hire me as the expert. We're doing something new. You're not an expert because we're not an expert either. What is it you want to learn?
turns.zh: 这是一个微妙但 brilliant 的框架：就问自己，我想学什么，然后看哪条职业道路能最大化这一点。太有趣了。这是我面试任何人时问的第一个问题。当我要招人进团队时，第一个问题就是，你想学什么？我不想要那种专家——就像我们之前聊过的——说，我是这方面的专家，你雇我当专家。我们在做新东西。你不是专家，因为我们也不是专家。你想学什么？

transcript.secZh: 职业建议：学什么和向谁学

transcript.sec: Learning by doing and versioning yourself

turns.en: On the topic of learning, what is the best way to learn? You go into this new place, into this new world, into maybe v1. You said you're building v1. I mean, the whole world is late, it's full of v1s or v0s waiting for the v1 to come along. Zero to one. What's the process of that look like? What's the process of learning? How do you learn?
turns.zh: 关于学习，最好的学习方法是什么？你进入一个新地方、新世界，也许是 v1。你说你在构建 v1。我的意思是，整个世界都晚了，到处都是 v1 或等待 v1 出现的 v0。从零到一。这个过程是什么样的？学习的过程是怎样的？你怎么学习？

turns.en: Let me put a framing and then we'll talk about that last piece. I have now, looking back especially writing this book, I have a version one of myself, a version two, a version three, a version four. I had a lot of opinions about myself and what I wanted to do. Sometimes those opinions for certain people are formed and they get the data from their parents and they go do what their parents told them to do or their surroundings. My opinions were like, I want to go and learn this, I'm curious about that. I made the zero to one move and then over time by doing, I was refining those things and learning what I was really curious about and what I was really good at because I was getting data. And then I was like, then I had another set of opinions to create version two of me, and then I would go and do it. It's always learning by doing. Starting with the opinion, you're not gonna get any facts. Most people like, where do I make the most money from my position? They're trying to start with data. Start with the why. What's your curiosity? What do you want to learn? And then follow that. I took the lowest job on the totem pole at General Magic because I wanted to get in there to work with the right team. I didn't even know what they were doing, but I thought that it felt right. I was barely living above the poverty line working there, working 80 hours a week because it was so amazing to learn, just like a college student. That's what I was doing. And then that set, and then I learned more from that and then changed those opinions into data and then I found other opinions. So it's the same thing, but it was by doing. The way you find out what you want to do in life is by figuring out what you don't want to do, and the only way you find that out is by doing a bunch of stuff and refining it.
turns.zh: 让我先给个框架，然后再聊最后那部分。现在回头看，尤其是写这本书时，我有版本一的自己、版本二、版本三、版本四。我对自己和想做的事有很多看法。有时这些看法对某些人来说，是从父母那里得到数据，然后去做父母或环境告诉他们的。我的看法是，我想去学这个，我对那个好奇。我做了从零到一的移动，然后通过实践，我不断精炼这些东西，了解自己真正好奇和擅长的，因为我获得了数据。然后我又有了新的看法，创造版本二的自己，再去实践。永远是干中学。从看法开始，你得不到任何事实。大多数人会问，我在这个位置怎么赚最多钱？他们试图从数据开始。从为什么开始。你的好奇心是什么？你想学什么？然后追随它。我在 General Magic 做了最低级别的工作，因为我想进入那个团队，和合适的人一起工作。我甚至不知道他们在做什么，但我觉得感觉对。我在那里工作，收入勉强高于贫困线，每周工作 80 小时，因为学习太棒了，就像大学生一样。那就是我做的。然后那一段经历让我学到更多，把那些看法变成数据，再找到新的看法。所以是一样的，但通过实践。你找到一生想做什么的方法，就是弄清楚你不想做什么，而唯一的方法就是做一堆事情并不断精炼。

turns.en: That's hilarious. Yeah, that's brilliant.
turns.zh: 这太有趣了。是的，很 brilliant。

transcript.sec: What it takes to found a startup

turns.en: In terms of the career path of leaping into the startup world and launching a startup, what does it take to successfully found a startup, to have a chance to succeed? And maybe how do you decide to take that leap? Having founded, having been part of many v1s, many of some of the most successful v1s ever, what's it take to take that leap? Maybe leave your job at a cushy job at a company and do the startup. What does it take?
turns.zh: 关于跳入创业世界、创办公司的职业道路，要成功创办一家初创公司、有机会成功，需要什么？也许你怎么决定迈出这一步？你创办过、参与过许多 v1，其中一些是最成功的 v1，迈出这一步需要什么？也许离开你在公司的舒适工作，去做创业。需要什么？

turns.en: It takes belief in yourself. That's the first thing. Belief that you can do it, but hopefully with mirrors or mentors around you, your coaches around you, to make sure you know you're not crazy. It's a crazy smart idea but you're not crazy, and you're just working on something as a lone mad man or woman. You have a great idea, and like I said, great ideas chase you. In this world, there are so many people who have more ideas than they have time to implement. I used to be like that. I would like, oh my god, have this idea, this idea, and you try to do all of them. But the best ideas are the ones that you can really focus on, and you shut out all those other things, and you bring those other ideas into the thing you're trying to do. So I try to run away from a great idea and then it stalks me. Yeah, it hunts you down because you're like, ah, that's gonna have this problem, I'm gonna put it aside, and then all of a sudden, a few days later, oh, I think I know how to solve that problem, or I talk to somebody, and you're just always kind of niggling around the edges of it. And then at some point, it just becomes like this black hole that just sucks you, and you're like, I can't think about anything else but this. It's almost like a relationship in the world, right? You know when you have it with a person, you find your partner, you're like, hmm, wait, um, something, and then you're trying to like, and then all of a sudden it just comes together. It's kind of like that. Ultimately, achieve focus. See, I'm different, I just dive right in. I used to do that too, I used to dive right in. But I learned that you need more time to run away from it. Run away from it and so it chases you because it makes you think harder about that. This is not dating advice we're talking about. But ultimately, you have achieved a focus on it. But you also said, believe in yourself. So it's not necessarily even the idea, it's the human. You have to believe in yourself and the idea that you have, because if you don't have that belief, then you can't project that to other people to say, join the team.
turns.zh: 需要相信自己。这是第一件事。相信你能做到，但最好有镜子或导师在你身边，你的教练，确保你知道自己没疯。这是一个疯狂但聪明的想法，但你没疯，你不是一个孤独的狂人。你有一个好主意，就像我说的，好主意会追逐你。在这个世界上，有很多人的想法比他们实施的时间还多。我以前就是这样。我会想，天哪，有这个想法，那个想法，你试图全部实现。但最好的想法是那些你能真正专注的，你屏蔽所有其他事情，把其他想法融入你正在做的事情。所以我试图逃离一个好主意，然后它跟踪我。是的，它追捕你，因为你会想，啊，这会有这个问题，我把它放一边，然后几天后，哦，我想我知道怎么解决那个问题了，或者我和某人聊了，你总是在它的边缘徘徊。然后在某个时刻，它就像一个黑洞一样把你吸进去，你除了这个什么都想不了。这几乎就像一段关系，对吧？你知道当你遇到一个人，找到你的伴侣，你会想，嗯，等等，嗯，然后你试着，然后突然一切就对了。差不多就是这样。最终，实现专注。看，我不同，我直接跳进去。我以前也那样，直接跳进去。但我学到你需要更多时间逃离它。逃离它，这样它就会追逐你，因为它让你更深入地思考。这不是在讲约会建议。但最终，你实现了对它的专注。但你也说，相信自己。所以不一定只是想法，而是人。你必须相信自己和你的想法，因为如果你没有那种信念，你就无法把它传递给其他人，让他们加入团队。

transcript.secZh: 创办初创公司所需条件

transcript.sec: Finding a Mentor

turns.en: You mentioned mentors and you've talked about having had incredible mentors in your life. You're also a mentor to a very large number of people. What does it take to find a mentor? How do you find a great mentor?
turns.zh: 你提到了导师，也聊过自己生命中遇到过非常棒的导师。同时，你也是很多人的导师。要怎样才能找到一位导师？怎么找到一位好导师？

turns.en: Usually they also find you. It's like with the ideas, no. What happens is you're in the right community around you. Because you've been building a network, because you can't do it alone. So you have to create this network around you of relationships that are not transactions but relationships over time that you really cherish. People you talk to, share vulnerable or nascent ideas with, or crazy opinions, and then you argue them through. But you start to see resonate. And it's not about age, it's just about this connection. I have mentors. Obviously when I was young, all my mentors were older. And as I get older, I have mentors who are younger than me or the same age. They're not all just older. So it's about that connection, about being on that same wavelength. But they also counterbalance you, they complement you in some way. Like my best mentors had nothing to do with technology, they didn't know anything about technology the way we know it. They were all about human nature and they could reflect that and help me get more human focused and more empathetic. Because I was so detailed in the technology, I needed to see from other perspectives. But then they wanted to learn more about the technology, or they thought that this idea was so great that it should exist, now let's work together on that. So it's really they have to find you and you have to find them, and that's by sharing. You just don't go and look it up on the internet and say who are the best mentors in the world. It just doesn't work that way. So form a network of people and see where it goes. It's like finding relationships, finding love, all that kind of human nature.
turns.zh: 通常他们也会找到你。就像想法一样，不是的。关键在于你身处一个合适的社群。因为你一直在建立人脉，因为你不可能单打独斗。所以你必须围绕自己建立一个关系网络，这些关系不是交易，而是你真正珍惜的、经得起时间考验的关系。你和他们交谈，分享脆弱或初生的想法，或者疯狂的观点，然后一起辩论。你会开始看到共鸣。这和年龄无关，只关乎这种连接。我有导师。显然，年轻时我的导师都比我年长。但随着年龄增长，我也有比我年轻或同龄的导师。他们并不都比我大。所以关键在于那种连接，在于同频共振。但他们也能平衡你，在某种程度上与你互补。比如我最好的导师和科技毫无关系，他们对我们所理解的科技一无所知。他们关注的是人性，能反映人性，帮助我更关注人、更有同理心。因为我在科技细节上过于投入，需要从其他视角看问题。而他们也想了解更多科技，或者觉得这个想法太棒了，应该存在，那就一起合作吧。所以真的是他们找到你，你也找到他们，而这通过分享实现。你不能只是上网搜索“世界上最好的导师是谁”，那样行不通。所以要建立一个朋友圈，看看会怎样。这就像寻找伴侣、寻找爱情，都是人性使然。

transcript.sec: VCs: Help or Hurt?

turns.en: Venture capitalists, money. Do VCs help or hurt a business in general? In those early stages, in the chase of developing a V1, what is the constructive and destructive power of money in the development and deployment of a brilliant idea?
turns.zh: 风险投资家，钱。风投总体上对生意是帮助还是伤害？在早期阶段，在开发第一个版本的过程中，金钱在开发和部署一个绝妙想法时的建设性和破坏性力量是什么？

turns.en: I have seen brilliant venture capitalists, I have seen horrible ones. Ones that care about their LPs more than they care about the entrepreneurs. Of course, everyone's in it, at the end of the day, especially venture capital, they have to give a return to their limited partners, the people who invest in money that they have to shepherd and make sure it's watched over properly. But when there's not a balance, a pushback in a venture capitalist between what the LP needs and what the entrepreneur needs, and the entrepreneur might be trying really hard, but if the VC doesn't see the exit happening in two years and then just leaves them hanging, when the value exchange is only money and not mentorship or ideas or other things, when there's not a relationship but really a transaction, that's when money is toxic. Because you can get money everywhere. Maybe it's a little harder today over the last month, but you can still find people with money who are on that mission, who want to enable your mission and can be mentors. Not always, not all of them, but some of them can be mentors. But if they're on your side, then it's incredibly powerful, because not just one plus one equals two, it's something bigger than that. Then they can bring their networks of people, their networks of companies, and other people they worked with that might want to join your mission. That's the kind of venture capitalist and smart money that's out there. But you have to build a relationship. People go, "Oh, look at that valuation, oh, it's the brand name of the VC that's investing me." No, it boils down to who's that partner and how experienced are they. Don't just give me the brand, give me the person, because that's the person I'm going to be interacting with.
turns.zh: 我见过出色的风险投资家，也见过糟糕的。有些更关心他们的有限合伙人（LP），而不是创业者。当然，说到底，每个人都在其中，尤其是风投，他们必须为有限合伙人带来回报，那些投资他们的人，他们必须管理好那笔钱。但当风投在 LP 的需求和创业者的需求之间失去平衡，没有制衡，创业者可能非常努力，但风投看不到两年内能退出，就撒手不管，当价值交换只有金钱，没有指导或想法等其他东西，当关系不是关系而是交易时，金钱就是有毒的。因为钱到处都能找到。也许过去一个月更难一些，但你仍然能找到有钱人，他们认同你的使命，愿意支持你的使命，并且能成为导师。不总是，不是所有人，但有些人可以成为导师。如果他们站在你这边，那就非常强大，因为不只是 1+1=2，而是更大的东西。他们可以带来他们的人脉网络、公司网络，以及他们合作过的、可能想加入你使命的人。这就是那种风投和聪明的钱。但你必须建立关系。人们会说：“哦，看那个估值，哦，投资我的风投是大品牌。”不，归根结底是那个合伙人是谁，他们有多经验丰富。别只给我品牌，给我那个人，因为那才是我要互动的人。

transcript.sec: The Why of Lawyers

turns.en: It seems like out of all the brilliant things you write about, this might not be an important question, but it's fascinating to me. Does the company need lawyers? And why, and what kind? You write about the value of this game, the legal game. Why do we need lawyers?
turns.zh: 在你写的所有精彩内容中，这个问题可能不重要，但我觉得很有意思。公司需要律师吗？为什么？需要什么样的律师？你写过这个游戏的价值，法律游戏。我们为什么需要律师？

turns.en: Why of lawyers? Yes, exactly. The why of lawyers. Thank you. You even do the question: we just have to put "why" in front of everything you ask. Why lawyers? Why even mafiosos had lawyers? Tony Soprano, Scarface, all of them had lawyers. Why? Because there are things in this world that you don't always consider: in the government, in laws, in competitors. Because you're so focused on what you're doing, they have to watch out for you. The best kind of lawyers are the ones who try to work with you to enable what you're doing and see gray areas. Law is not black or white, it's how it's interpreted. So they can help interpret things a certain way or help push on things a certain way to get change to happen or allow change to happen. Because if you have lawyers who are always like you were talking about PR people, if you have lawyers who are always saying no to everything because their job is to really say no or maybe, they'll never say yes. And you also said their job was to say no and bill you by the hour. Exactly, it's depressing. If you don't want to know, don't ask them, because you're going to get a no, maybe, and you'll get charged for it anyway. So to have a partner, to have them on your team to help you see some things you don't see, some consequences, they help to rein that in or change your language. Like, "Are you going to get sued for this ad?" "Yeah, just change this one word and it helps." So you need to have a partner. Most of the time, especially engineers or designers, they see lawyers as only stifling. Lawyers can actually, if you do it right and you have the right type, they can actually open up a whole new world for you because of the interpretation and how we go about doing things. And they help you not get bogged down in the pain of stupid little mistakes that didn't mean anything. You shot yourself in the foot and you didn't even know it, you didn't even know you were carrying the gun.
turns.zh: 律师的“为什么”？对，就是律师的“为什么”。谢谢。你甚至把问题都问出来了：我们只需要在你问的每件事前面加个“为什么”。为什么需要律师？为什么连黑手党都有律师？托尼·索普拉诺、疤面煞星，他们都有律师。为什么？因为世界上有些事情你并不总是考虑到：政府、法律、竞争对手。因为你太专注于自己正在做的事，他们必须为你把关。最好的律师是那些试图与你合作、促成你的事业、并看到灰色地带的人。法律不是非黑即白的，而是如何解释的。所以他们可以帮助以某种方式解释事物，或以某种方式推动事物，从而促成改变或允许改变发生。因为如果你的律师总是像你提到的公关人员那样，总是对所有事情说“不”，因为他们的工作就是说不或者也许，他们永远不会说“是”。你也说过他们的工作就是说“不”并按小时收费。没错，这很压抑。如果你不想知道，就别问他们，因为你会得到一个“不”或者“也许”，而且你还会为此付费。所以，要有一个伙伴，让他们加入你的团队，帮你看到你看不到的东西，一些后果，他们帮你控制或改变你的措辞。比如，“你会因为这个广告被起诉吗？”“是的，改一个词就好了。”所以你需要一个伙伴。大多数时候，尤其是工程师或设计师，他们认为律师只会扼杀创意。实际上，如果你做对了，找对了人，律师可以为你打开一个全新的世界，因为解释和做事的方式。而且他们帮你避免陷入那些毫无意义的愚蠢小错误的痛苦中。你搬起石头砸了自己的脚，却还不知道，甚至不知道自己手里有枪。

transcript.sec: Work-Life Balance

turns.en: Just to jump around, Charles Bukowski once wrote, "Find what you love and let it kill you." So the question is about work-life balance. That's like finding an idea, let it chase you, yes, but a little more aggressive. So what is work-life balance?
turns.zh: 跳转一下，查尔斯·布考斯基曾写道：“找到你热爱的东西，让它杀死你。”所以问题是关于工作与生活的平衡。这就像找到一个想法，让它追逐你，是的，但更激进一点。那么什么是工作与生活的平衡？

transcript.sec: Work-Life Balance and Boundaries

turns.en: Balance like that maximizes success and happiness? Is there such a thing as work-life balance? Can you speak to this? Your work is your life, and I mean that in the positive sense.
turns.zh: 那种能最大化成功和幸福的平衡是什么样的？存在工作与生活的平衡这回事吗？你能谈谈这个吗？你的工作就是你的生活，我是在积极的意义上说这话的。

turns.en: When you're on a mission that really matters and you know that you can really affect not just yourself but other people's lives, that is very rewarding. That's not work; it's a mission. You adopt it. But that said, you still need to have boundaries. At General Magic — wonderful documentary, if no one's seen it, you've got to see it, it's amazing — I was physically and mentally unhealthy, socially unhealthy as well, because I put every waking minute into this thing, every ounce of me into it. And when it was a spectacular disaster — we were making the iPhone 15 years too early — the bottom fell out. I had nothing left. I had to get healthy socially, emotionally, physically after that trauma. I let everything go. I learned from that that you have to, even though you might put everything into your work, you need to find balance outside of it. Now, that doesn't mean you're working three days a week and four days off or whatever. You're still working as hard as ever, but what you're doing is making sure that when your thinking time is during work, you're not ruminating at 3 AM. You use the tools you have to put those ideas into databases or on pages or somewhere else so you can go back and look at them, so you're not always having to remember. Because what happens is most people don't write stuff down, so they just sit here thinking, 'I've got to remember this, I've got to remember this.' If you just put it into the tools and you can come back to it, you can come back fresh.
turns.zh: 当你投身于一项真正重要的使命，并且知道自己不仅能影响自己，还能影响他人的生活时，那是非常有成就感的。那不是工作，而是使命。你接受了它。但即便如此，你仍然需要设定边界。在 General Magic——那部精彩的纪录片，如果没人看过，你一定要看，太棒了——我身心俱疲，社交也不健康，因为我醒着的每一分钟都投入其中，倾注了全部心血。当它遭遇惨败时——我们做 iPhone 早了 15 年——一切崩塌了。我一无所有。在那次创伤之后，我不得不恢复社交、情感和身体健康。我放下了所有。我从中学到，即使你可能全身心投入工作，也需要在外部找到平衡。这并不意味着你每周工作三天、休息四天之类的。你仍然像以前一样努力工作，但你要确保思考时间在工作期间，而不是凌晨 3 点还在反复琢磨。你利用工具把这些想法存入数据库、记在纸上或其他地方，这样你可以回头查看，而不必一直记着。因为大多数人都不写下来，所以他们只能坐在那里想‘我得记住这个，我得记住这个’。如果你把它放进工具里，然后可以回来再看，你就能以全新的状态回归。

turns.en: A lot of the time is about ruminating about what I need to get done and remembering everything instead of doing the work. That's fascinating. See, if you just put it down on paper, you can actually escape it for a time, to have peace for a time.
turns.zh: 很多时候，我们都在反复思考需要完成什么、记住所有事情，而不是真正去做工作。这很有意思。你看，如果你把它写在纸上，你就能暂时摆脱它，获得片刻的宁静。

transcript.sec: Darkest Moments and Childhood

turns.en: You mentioned General Magic. Let me ask you the Russian question: what's been the darkest moments of your life? What are some of the darkest places you've gone in your mind? You've talked about how if you're doing these kinds of things with startups, you're going to have to face a crisis. Absolutely. If you're doing it right, you're going to face it. So for you personally, where were some of the tougher moments in your life?
turns.zh: 你提到了 General Magic。让我问你那个俄罗斯问题：你生命中最黑暗的时刻是什么？你脑海中曾到过哪些最黑暗的地方？你谈到过，如果你在创业中做这类事情，就一定会面临危机。绝对如此。如果你做得对，你就会面对它。那么对你个人来说，你生命中有哪些比较艰难的时刻？

turns.en: Growing up, I went to 12 schools in 15 years. I was always the new kid. Put yourself in those shoes. Were you picked on? Well, absolutely. But even more so, I was the geek with the computer. Remember the nerds in the 80s? You probably don't know this, but believe me, we were made fun of. 'What were these computers? What were these things?' You're off in a corner, they're all off partying or whatever, and I'm sitting there. They're like, 'This guy is just this alien. Who's this new guy who just showed up?' And then you would ask the smart questions, and you couldn't be the smartest in the class because then you'd get picked on too. And you're the new kid, so you're in this environment that's ever-changing, you don't fit in, and you are just asking questions because you think they're the right questions to answer, but then they're like, 'You're making us look bad. Don't ask these smart questions because you make us do more work.' So right there, it's pretty tough. And I'm moving cities, right? I didn't have the internet to stay connected to people. There was no internet. Phone calls were two dollars a minute. So it was lonely too. It was lonely. I was a latchkey kid. I had my brother, but he was a skateboarder and he had a different social way of working. He loved to do that stuff and be outside. I loved the computer. So even in the family, I was alone with the computer. I was absolutely alone. That was just me. But then you could find the other geeks, right? But there were just a few of us, and we made this little thing. But then when you moved away, I had to use a BBS, a bulletin board system, and a dial-up modem. And then I started hacking the phone system to get free codes of MCI and Sprint back in the day to get long distance, to get free codes to call my friends, the geeks on the other side, or to dial into a BBS cheaply that was in another part of the world. So this was this subculture, and that was not accepted in any way, and not the heroes that you see today that are the richest people in the world and everything. So that was the first set of trauma. And then the next one really was General Magic, the end of that, like I described before, and pulling yourself out. I became so insular in that world of geeking out and building stuff that I just tore all the social ties, because it was just a drug. I was hooked on that. I was a junkie. I had to get clean.
turns.zh: 成长过程中，我 15 年内换了 12 所学校。我永远是那个新来的孩子。设身处地想想。你被欺负过吗？当然有。但更甚的是，我是那个玩电脑的极客。还记得 80 年代的书呆子吗？你可能不知道，但相信我，我们被嘲笑过。‘这些电脑是什么？这些东西是什么？’你缩在角落里，他们都在聚会或做别的，而我坐在那里。他们觉得‘这家伙就是个外星人。这个新来的家伙是谁？’然后你会问一些聪明的问题，但你不敢成为班上最聪明的，因为那样你也会被欺负。而且你是新来的，所以处在一个不断变化的环境中，你格格不入，你只是问问题，因为你认为那是该回答的正确问题，但他们却说‘你让我们显得很糟糕。别问这些聪明的问题，因为你让我们做更多的工作。’所以那真的很艰难。而且我还要搬家，对吧？我没有互联网可以和人保持联系。没有互联网。电话费每分钟两美元。所以也很孤独。很孤独。我是挂钥匙儿童。我有哥哥，但他是个滑板少年，他有不同的社交方式。他喜欢在外面玩那些东西。我喜欢电脑。所以即使在家庭里，我也是独自和电脑在一起。我完全孤独。只有我一个人。但后来你能找到其他极客，对吧？但我们只有几个人，我们组成了这个小团体。但当你搬走之后，我不得不使用 BBS（公告板系统）和拨号调制解调器。然后我开始入侵电话系统，获取当年 MCI 和 Sprint 的免费代码，用来打长途电话，免费打电话给我的朋友——另一边的极客们，或者廉价拨入世界另一端的 BBS。所以这是一种亚文化，完全不被接受，不像今天那些成为世界首富的英雄们。所以那是第一段创伤。然后下一个真的是 General Magic，它的结局，就像我之前描述的，以及如何把自己拉出来。我变得如此封闭在那个极客和构建东西的世界里，以至于我撕毁了所有社会关系，因为它就像毒品。我上瘾了。我是个瘾君子。我必须戒掉。

turns.en: And that made you who you are, tempered.
turns.zh: 而这造就了现在的你，经过淬炼的你。

transcript.sec: Death and Legacy

turns.en: Steve Jobs is no longer with us. One day you also will no longer be with us. That's the thing about this life: it ends. So no matter how many incredible things you brought to this world, no matter how many inventions you built, you too shall perish. Do you think about this? Are you afraid of your death?
turns.zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯已经不在了。总有一天你也会离开我们。这就是生命的本质：它会结束。所以无论你给这个世界带来了多少不可思议的东西，无论你创造了多少发明，你也会消亡。你考虑过这个吗？你害怕死亡吗？

turns.en: I am not afraid of my death. I am an atheist, and I think about the soul. Because I do, even though I'm an atheist, I think about the soul. And the soul is the thing that you instill in others when you go, that lives on. It's not this thing that's magically in space; it's the thing that you've imparted onto people that you worked with and those relationships you've had. And that soul lives on in the stories that they tell. And through build, I'm hopeful that those stories stay relevant because they're human nature. They're not about who knows what the next iPhone thing is or the next iPod thing. The stuff that I have been able, the privilege to make and work with people, those are all ephemeral. The iPod's gone now. This week it was announced iPod's dead. And after it is, those human connections, it's that growth that you've helped someone, just like they helped me. Just like Bill Campbell or Steve Jobs is gone, but they made me be better. That's the soul that I believe in.
turns.zh: 我不害怕死亡。我是无神论者，但我思考灵魂。因为即使我是无神论者，我仍然思考灵魂。灵魂是你离开时灌输给他人、得以延续的东西。它不是某种神奇地存在于太空中的东西；而是你传授给共事过的人以及你所拥有的那些关系的东西。那个灵魂活在他们的故事里。通过构建，我希望那些故事能保持相关性，因为它们关乎人性。它们不是关于谁知道下一个 iPhone 或下一个 iPod 是什么。我有幸能够创造并与他人合作的那些东西，都是短暂的。iPod 已经消失了。本周宣布 iPod 停产了。而在那之后，是那些人际联系，是你帮助他人成长的过程，就像他们帮助我一样。就像比尔·坎贝尔或史蒂夫·乔布斯已经离世，但他们让我变得更好。这就是我所相信的灵魂。

turns.en: That's fascinating that you say that. So even though the iPod is an end of an era, using it every day, I think the number of people that it impacted is just... I suppose the soul is carried by the people. Exactly. Sometimes the products you create are the sort of the transport mechanism, it's the vessel, and they felt the love, and that love transformed it, even if they don't have the vessel anymore. And that way, the soul lives.
turns.zh: 你这么说很有意思。所以即使 iPod 是一个时代的终结，每天使用它，我认为它影响的人数……我想灵魂是由人承载的。没错。有时你创造的产品是一种传输机制，是容器，人们感受到了那份爱，那份爱改变了它，即使他们不再拥有那个容器。这样，灵魂就永存了。

transcript.sec: Meaning of Life and Human Curiosity

turns.en: Just like the body is the vessel that's beautifully put. Why do you think we're here? What's the meaning of life, Tony? Jesus, we're going all around. Meaning of life. Why? Because you said it's important to have a press release. Okay. Humanity, if life on Earth, if this thing, the consciousness, the falling in love and building bridges and iPods and rockets and trying to extend out into the cosmos, why? Why do you think we're doing it? Is there anything?
turns.zh: 就像身体是容器，这个比喻很妙。你觉得我们为什么在这里？生命的意义是什么，托尼？天哪，我们聊得真广。生命的意义。为什么？因为你说过发新闻稿很重要。好吧。人类，如果地球上的生命，如果这个东西——意识、坠入爱河、建造桥梁、iPod 和火箭、试图向宇宙延伸——为什么？你觉得我们为什么在做这些？有什么原因吗？

turns.en: We are naturally curious individuals. We are always looking for meaning, always trying to ascribe meaning to something or understand something. And through that, it's just like evolution, right? Darwinism. It's just that thing that's baked into our being at the most fundamental level, driven by curiosity. Driven by curiosity and creating some pretty cool things along the way.
turns.zh: 我们天生就是好奇的个体。我们总是在寻找意义，总是试图赋予某物意义或理解某物。通过这个过程，这就像进化论，对吧？达尔文主义。这不过是刻在我们存在最底层的东西，由好奇心驱动。由好奇心驱动，并在此过程中创造出一些很酷的东西。

transcript.secZh: 生命意义与人类好奇心

transcript.sec: Appreciation and Future Plans

turns.en: Tony, you and speaking of cool things, you've created some of the coolest things ever. And on top of that, you're just an amazing human being. It's a huge honor that you'll sit and talk to me today.
turns.zh: 托尼，说到酷的东西，你创造了一些有史以来最酷的东西。除此之外，你本身就是一个了不起的人。你今天能坐下来和我聊天，是我莫大的荣幸。

turns.en: This is a fun Lex, this is great. I didn't know where it was going and I'm let's talk. I've been looking for seats. I would love seven, eight, nine, six, seven, yes. Let's go hang out and have dinner and just rap about all kinds of crates. I'd love to continue this.
turns.zh: 这很有趣，莱克斯，太棒了。我不知道会聊到哪里，但让我们聊吧。我一直在找座位。我很想七、八、九、六、七，是的。我们一起去闲逛、吃晚餐，随便聊聊各种事情。我很想继续聊下去。

turns.en: I would too. Thank you so much. Thank you.
turns.zh: 我也是。非常感谢。谢谢。

turns.en: Thanks for listening to this conversation with Tony Fadell. To support this podcast, please check out our sponsors in the description. And now let me leave you with some words from Tony Fadell himself. The most wonderful part of building something together with a team is that you're walking side by side with other people. You're all looking at your feet and scanning the horizon at the same time. Some people will see things you can't, and you will see things that are invisible to everyone else. So don't think doing the work just means locking yourself into a room. A huge part of it is walking with your team. The work is reaching your destination together or finding a new destination and bringing the team with you. Thank you for listening and hope to see you next time.
turns.zh: 感谢收听本期与托尼·法德尔的对话。要支持本播客，请查看描述中的赞助商。现在，让我用托尼·法德尔本人的话作为结尾。与团队一起构建事物最美好的部分是，你与他人并肩而行。你们同时低头看路，又抬头眺望地平线。有些人能看到你看不到的东西，而你也能看到别人看不见的事物。所以不要认为工作就是把自己锁在房间里。很大一部分工作是和团队一起前行。工作就是一起到达目的地，或者找到新目的地，并带领团队一起前往。感谢收听，希望下次再见。
